problem: Recursive neural networks for sentence encoding typically require supervised parse trees, which are costly to obtain and may not align with the optimal structure for a given downstream task. The challenge is to learn task-specific, discrete tree compositions directly from plain text while remaining trainable end-to-end.
method: Introduce Gumbel Tree-LSTM, a bottom-up Tree-LSTM that repeatedly composes adjacent nodes into parent candidates and selects one parent per layer until a single sentence vector remains. A trainable composition query vector scores each candidate (via q·h), and Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax is used to sample a discrete parent during training (argmax at test), enabling standard backpropagation through the otherwise discrete selection. The layer-wise construction supports efficient batched computation without explicit parse inputs.
paper_title: Learning to Compose Task-Specific Tree Structures

problem: Composing trees from plain text using only local word representations can yield suboptimal merge decisions because leaves lack broader sentence context.
method: Augment the model with an LSTM-based leaf transformation that runs over the input words to provide each leaf node with sequential context before any tree composition. This hybridizes sequential context with tree-structured composition (without a parser/tracking LSTM), improving parent selection and yielding substantial performance and convergence gains in experiments.
paper_title: Learning to Compose Task-Specific Tree Structures

problem: Existing coordinate-based meta-analysis (CBMA) tools (e.g., Neurosynth) yield statistically reliable forward-inference maps mainly for single-term queries; complex logic-based queries (e.g., conjunctions/disjunctions of terms) are underpowered and lack a clear probabilistic semantics for voxel activation probabilities.
method: Encode a CBMA database as a CP-Logic probabilistic logic program (NeuroLang subset) with predicates for reported voxels and term–study relations and an equiprobable SelectedStudy choice, then define Activation(v) and TermAssociation(t) via deterministic existential rules. Translate this program to an equivalent Bayesian network and derive that the joint/conditional distributions reproduce standard CBMA estimators for conjunctions and disjunctions, proving soundness of probabilistic answers to logic-based queries.
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: At neuroimaging scale (hundreds of thousands of voxels, thousands of studies/terms), knowledge-compilation approaches (e.g., SDDs) to evaluate probabilistic queries are prohibitively slow (e.g., >30 minutes for a two-term CQ).
method: Leverage lifted query processing for unions of conjunctive queries (UCQs) using the Dalvi–Suciu dichotomy: rewrite P[Activation(v) | T_i, T_j] as P[Q1(v)]/P[Q2], construct extensional query plans, and execute with a custom relational algebra engine; fall back to KC only if queries are not liftable. This lifted approach solves two-term CQs in seconds while maintaining exactness under liftability.
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: Hard thresholding of TFIDF features to relate terms and studies excludes partially relevant studies, reducing statistical power for conjunctive queries, especially on small databases.
method: Replace the hard indicator 1[x>τ] with a probabilistic soft-threshold ω(x; α, τ)=σ(α(x−τ)) and use ω as the probabilities of TermInStudy facts; extend to mixed Boolean formulas via f(x1,x2)=1−(1−ω1)(1−ω2). Experiments on simulated data and the Neurosynth database show improved two-term CQ performance: higher F1 for smaller samples (with α≈300) and higher activation-map consistency across subsamples on real data (e.g., +20% at sample size 2395 and +10% at 3856).
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: There is no formal, general way to quantify an LLM’s tendency to favor its own outputs during self-refinement, leading to contradictory findings about whether self-refine helps or hurts.
method: Define self-bias with two complementary statistics: (i) statistical bias between the LLM’s self-evaluated quality and the true/ground-truth quality, and (ii) distance skewness of the residual distribution E[θ̂]−θ to capture asymmetric over/underestimation. Instantiate task-ground truths per setting: MQM-aligned scores via quantile-mapped BLEURT for machine translation, exact concept coverage for constrained generation, and binary correctness for math reasoning. Run an iterative self-refine protocol that only accepts refinements when the (self-)feedback score improves, enabling bias and skewness tracking across iterations.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
method: Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: Mitigating self-bias is necessary to elicit real self-correction and avoid false-positive optimization in self-refine.
method: Replace self-feedback with external, low-bias feedback using InstructScore, which provides fine-grained error spans, types, and severities with access to references (oracle-like) while leaving corrections to the LLM; this significantly lowers bias during refinement and yields consistent BLEURT gains. Additionally, scale model size (e.g., LLaMA2-70B vs 7B/13B) to improve robustness: larger models exhibit reduced and stabilized self-bias over iterations, enabling more accurate self-assessment and better self-correction.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: LLM-based driving planners are computationally heavy and slow for real-time use, while fast learning-based planners have limited controllability and struggle in rare scenarios.
method: AsyncDriver decouples the LLM from the real-time planner by extracting persistent scene-associated instruction features from a Llama2-13B backbone given vectorized scene data and a series of routing instructions, then reusing these features across multiple planning steps. The transformer planner runs every frame, but the LLM runs at a configurable interval; during gaps, previously computed high-level features continue to guide planning. This asynchronous scheme maintains closed-loop performance while reducing cost, e.g., running the LLM every 3 frames cuts inference time by ~40% with ~1% accuracy loss, and remains robust even at large intervals.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Injecting language-informed guidance into a transformer-based real-time planner can disrupt its native scene processing if not fused carefully, reducing reliability and controllability.
method: The Adaptive Injection Block augments each decoder layer to cross-attend the planner’s query to the LLM’s instruction features (the projected last-token embedding) and fuses the result with the original attention output via a learnable gate initialized to zero. This preserves the planner’s original attention over scene features while adaptively incorporating instruction guidance. The design is model-agnostic and can be plugged into any transformer-based real-time planner.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Aligning language instructions with vectorized driving scenes is challenging; naïve fusion fails to yield useful high-level guidance for planning.
method: A Scene-Associated Instruction Feature Extraction Module combines a Vector Map Encoder/Adapter with language embeddings and feeds them into Llama2-13B to produce hidden features. Training is aided by an Alignment Assistance Module with auxiliary heads supervising ego velocity/acceleration regression, adjacent-lane existence (left/right), relevant traffic-light state, lane-change requirement, and future velocity decision (accelerate/decelerate/maintain). This module is used only during training to improve multimodal alignment, and ablations show it yields notable performance gains.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Existing mental health LLMs ignore that different counselors have distinct linguistic styles and therapy techniques; mixing styles in training data leads to unstable, non-personalized responses, and real multi-turn counseling data are scarce.
method: PsyDT constructs a specific counselor’s digital twin by synthesizing multi-turn dialogues that mirror that counselor’s style and therapeutic approach, using only a small number of real cases. The pipeline: (1) collect 12 real text-based counseling cases across 12 topics from one professional counselor (cost ≈$2000) and use GPT-4 to summarize the counselor’s linguistic style and therapeutic type (all identified as REBT), then retrieve technique details from a therapy knowledge base; (2) select 5,000 single-turn long-text dialogues from SoulChatCorpus and simulate clients’ Big Five (OCEAN) personalities with GPT-4 to diversify client behaviors; (3) per-topic match single-turn queries with the counselor’s style and technique, and guide GPT-4 (with emotion/response guides) to expand them into multi-turn dialogues (PsyDTCorpus). The synthesized dialogues achieve about 70% similarity to the counselor’s real cases on linguistic style and therapy technique, outperforming baseline synthesis prompts when judged by GPT-4o and Claude 3.5.
paper_title: PsyDT: Using LLMs to Construct the Digital Twin of Psychological Counselor with Personalized Counseling Style for Psychological Counseling

problem: There is no deployable LLM that reliably exhibits a particular counselor’s counseling style and therapy technique in multi-turn settings.
method: Fine-tune Qwen2-7B-Instruct with multi-turn instruction fine-tuning on PsyDTCorpus to obtain PsyDTLLM. Trained on 4,760 synthetic dialogues and evaluated on 240 test dialogues spanning 12 topics, PsyDTLLM surpasses closed-, open-, and domain-specific baselines on automatic metrics (ROUGE-1/2/L, BLEU-4, BERTScore) and on professional criteria (emotional empathy, cognitive empathy, conversation strategy, state/attitude, safety) using GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 as evaluators, demonstrating stronger semantic understanding and counseling competence.
paper_title: PsyDT: Using LLMs to Construct the Digital Twin of Psychological Counselor with Personalized Counseling Style for Psychological Counseling

problem: Existing long-context LLM benchmarks are limited by relatively short documents, potential data leakage from outdated sources, and tasks that mostly test short-range dependencies rather than true long-range reasoning across a document.
method: Introduce LooGLE, a long-context, generic evaluation benchmark built from post-2022 documents across arXiv papers, Wikipedia, and movie/TV scripts. It includes 776 extra-long documents (avg. ~19.3k words; many >100k words), 6,448 test instances, and 1,101 manually crafted, cross-validated long-dependency QA pairs spanning four challenging types (multiple information retrieval, timeline reorder, computation, and comprehension/reasoning), plus short-dependency QA, cloze, and summarization. The dataset construction, prompts, and evaluation code are open-sourced to enable reproducibility and periodic updates.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: Timeline reordering in long texts is hard to evaluate reliably due to heterogeneous answer formats and a lack of robust sequence-similarity metrics.
method: Standardize timeline-reorder outputs and propose four numeric-sequence deviation metrics—LSD (location square deviation), LMD (location mean deviation), SD (swap deviation), and SDD (swap distance deviation). Answers are normalized (e.g., Roman numerals), non-standard outputs are identified, and metrics are computed on standardized outputs to robustly score temporal ordering quality; implementations and usage are provided in the benchmark toolkit.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: It is unclear which interventions (larger context windows, retrieval augmentation, and Chain-of-Thought prompting) actually improve long-context understanding in LLMs across realistic tasks.
method: Conduct a comprehensive evaluation of eight LLMs (commercial and open-source) on LooGLE using automatic metrics and GPT-4-as-judge, while varying input length, adding retrieval via LlamaIndex, and applying zero-/few-shot CoT. Findings: commercial models outperform open-source; models do well on short QA/cloze/summarization but struggle on long-dependency QA; extending context length helps long-dependency QA but brings little gain for summarization; retrieval often degrades long-dependency QA for GPT-4/3.5; zero-shot CoT substantially boosts timeline reorder (~20% abs.) and computation (~10% abs.) but few-shot CoT can hurt these types.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: Depression detection from social media requires clinically grounded symptom understanding, but keyword- or simple model-based DSM/PHQ mappings miss nuanced expressions and fully annotating all posts with LLMs is prohibitively costly.
method: Use clinician-crafted DSM-5 symptom templates and text embeddings to score each post’s depression risk, then only send the top k% highest-risk posts to an LLM for fine-grained DSM-5 symptom annotation, yielding a 9D binary vector per post; unselected posts receive zero vectors. Average post-level vectors to form a user-level diagnostic criteria feature (F_DC). This operationalizes DSM-5 knowledge while dramatically reducing LLM calls and retaining human-readable, medically aligned evidence.
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: Prior detectors largely ignore the longitudinal mood course needed to distinguish clinical depression from transient mood swings.
method: Filter for emotionally intense content using five expert-validated emotion templates (anger, disgust, anxiety, happiness, sadness), keeping the top m% posts per emotion. From this subset, have an LLM synthesize a temporal mood course summary (T_MC). Embed the summary and combine it with embeddings of the selected posts using weights α and β to produce a mood course representation (F_MC) that captures the types and evolution of affect over time.
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: Direct LLM classification can be unreliable and hard to calibrate; clinicians require accurate predictions with transparent, evidence-based explanations.
method: Construct three features per user: diagnostic criteria (F_DC), mood course (F_MC), and full post-history embedding (F_PH). Sum F_MC and F_PH, concatenate with F_DC, and classify with a Gradient Boosting Trees model (XGBoost), optionally calibrating probabilities via Isotonic Regression. For interpretability, prompt the LLM to generate an explanation that explicitly ties the detected DSM-5 symptoms and mood course to the predicted label. This hybrid design achieves state-of-the-art results on SWDD (AUPRC 0.8147; +0.0361 over the best baseline) and on the Twitter Mental Disorder dataset, with ablations showing the DSM-5 feature and LLM annotation as the largest contributors.
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: LLM-as-a-Judge is widely used to evaluate personalization, but its reliability for inferring user-specific preferences is unclear and often low, partly due to sparse/irrelevant persona information.
method: Augment the LLM-as-a-Personalized-Judge pipeline with verbal uncertainty estimation: instruct the LLM to output both a preference judgment and a certainty score on a 1–100 scale, then filter evaluations by a certainty threshold (e.g., ≥80). Across PRISM, OpinionQA, EC, and Public Reddit with models including GPT-4 and Command R+, high-certainty subsets reach around or above 80% agreement with human ground truth on binary tasks, while a tie option is rarely used and degrades utility. This makes certainty-thresholded judging a more reliable proxy than standard judging without uncertainty.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: Persona sparsity—limited or task-irrelevant persona variables—reduces the predictive power for preference inference, and evaluators need a way to detect when the persona is insufficient.
method: Use the LLM’s self-reported certainty as a proxy for persona sufficiency and sample/task difficulty. Conduct ablations that restrict persona inputs (e.g., using only three key features vs. a single least-informative feature) and observe large drops in both the number of high-certainty samples and accuracy (e.g., on OpinionQA, high-confidence cases drop from 480 to 10 when reduced to one feature), while confidence distributions correlate with known task difficulty (PR > PRISM/OpinionQA > EC). This enables principled filtering/deprioritization of uncertain cases driven by persona sparsity.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: Third-person human judging for personalization is costly and variable; it is unclear whether an LLM-based personalized judge can match human evaluators.
method: Run a crowdsourcing study on OpinionQA where annotators infer preferences from personas with the same prompts and certainty reporting. Compare GPT-4’s certainty-aware judgments to majority-vote human labels: overall accuracy is comparable (62.3% vs. 63.3%), and on high-certainty samples GPT-4 surpasses humans (79.2% vs. 71.4%). This validates certainty-aware LLM-as-a-Personalized-Judge as a scalable alternative when first-person labels are unavailable.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: State-of-the-art diffusion models excel at text-to-image generation but lack a unified way to accept arbitrary text-image interleaved instructions, especially when composing or merging elements from multiple reference images.
method: DREAM ENGINE replaces the diffusion model’s text encoders (e.g., CLIP/T5 in SD3.5) with a large multimodal model (Qwen2-VL) and a lightweight two-layer MLP adapter that maps LMM hidden states into the DiT conditioning space, while using the average-pooled condition plus timestep as modulation and removing token-length limits to accept any interleaved sequence. It is trained with a two-stage recipe: Stage 1 freezes LMM and DiT and trains only the adapter (~20M samples) for joint text-to-image and image-to-image alignment using rectified flow; Stage 2 unfreezes the DiT (via LoRA) for interleaved image-text instruction tuning. This alignment preserves base model quality (GenEval 0.69 vs SD3.5 0.71) while enabling arbitrary text-image control.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Editing and object-driven generation require preserving visual identity and fine details from input images, which can be degraded when passing through LMM backbones.
method: A visual feature blending skip is introduced: image patch states h_I are a weighted sum of the LMM decoder outputs (h_LLM) and raw ViT image features (h_ViT) with a controllable blending ratio r. During training, r is sampled uniformly in [0,1], enabling inference-time control over consistency vs. variation. Ablations show that higher r yields greater reconstruction consistency, supporting reliable identity preservation for editing and object-driven use cases.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Existing systems and datasets do not effectively train diffusion models to compose elements from multiple images under textual guidance, limiting compositional and multi-image control.
method: An Object-Driven Generation task is formulated and used in Stage 2: the model accepts multiple input images plus a text instruction and is trained on data constructed from object detection datasets (e.g., COCO), pairing images with captions and selecting multiple objects per image whose names appear in the caption. Combined with free-form editing data (UltraEdit), this instruction tuning yields emergent abilities to synthesize and mix features from different images and improves interleaved control compared to prior LMM-diffusion baselines.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Existing evaluations of interleaved image-text generation are too small, narrow, or text/image-only, and do not reflect real-world open-ended needs, making progress hard to measure and compare fairly.
method: OpenING provides a large, high-quality benchmark of 5,400 human-annotated instances spanning 23 meta-topics and 56 real-world tasks, each as multi-step interleaved image-text sequences. It is curated via a top-down pipeline with expert-guided annotation using the IntLabel tool, cross-checking and filtering, and translation (text and in-image text) to ensure consistency and reduce contamination risks. The dataset is split into a Dev Set (3,240) and Test Set (2,160) to support judge training and zero-shot evaluation. Compared to prior interleaved benchmarks, OpenING substantially expands coverage and diversity.
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: Open-ended multimodal generation is hard to evaluate: human review is costly and inconsistent, while GPT-based judges can be biased and raise privacy/leakage concerns.
method: The paper proposes Interleaved Arena for scalable pairwise comparisons across seven criteria with a roulette matching algorithm to ensure broad, fair agent coverage. Building on this, IntJudge is an offline judge model based on Qwen2-VL-7B trained with a combined objective (cross-entropy, contrastive, MSE, and pairwise ranking losses) to directly compare interleaved outputs. IntJudge achieves 82.42% agreement with human judgments under the FDT metric, outperforming GPT-4o as a judge by 11.34%, and generalizes to unseen models. This yields a reproducible, cost-effective evaluator aligned with human preferences for open-ended interleaved outputs.
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: Training a reliable multimodal judge requires substantial preference data, but high-quality human pairwise annotations are expensive and limited.
method: A Reference-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline scales judge-training data by pairing standard model generations with RAG outputs produced using gold answers, labeling the RAG outputs as winners. IntJudge is trained on 31,996 samples combining 6,014 arena pairs with 25,982 RAG pairs using the multi-term loss. Ablation shows that adding RAG data boosts FDT agreement on unseen models by 7.8%, demonstrating improved robustness and generalization of the judge.
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: Current LLM evaluations rarely test holistic rule learning in interactive settings; they often provide all information upfront, restrict agents to coarse actions, or involve rules likely seen during pretraining, making it hard to assess genuine rule discovery.
method: Introduce RULEARN, a fully interactive, text-based benchmark with a fine-grained action space where agents submit arbitrary strings parsed into character-level feedback. It contains 300 manually created puzzles across three environments—Function Operator, Escape Room, and Reactor—with hidden, previously unseen rules. Agents must actively experiment, gather observations, and infer, test, and revise hypotheses to solve tasks, directly evaluating end-to-end rule learning.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: LLM agents tend to take uninformed, repetitive actions and struggle to uncover and apply hidden rules due to the lack of an integrated process for hypothesis generation, testing, and refinement.
method: Propose IDEA, an agent framework that iteratively couples abduction (form initial hypotheses from sparse observations), deduction (devise and execute plans to test/use hypotheses), and induction (refine hypotheses from new feedback), supported by explicit components for goal, memory, plan, hypothesis, and dynamic action space. Implemented via structured prompting and an action-selection loop (Algorithm 1), IDEA reduces repeated actions and guides evidence collection. Empirically, IDEA yields roughly 10% absolute gains in success rates over a ReAct baseline for Llama3-70B, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4o, and reduces repeated actions (e.g., ~30% reduction), while remaining effective with task-agnostic prompts.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: It is unclear where LLM rule-learning diverges from human behavior across abduction, deduction, and induction in interactive environments.
method: Conduct a fine-grained human–LLM comparative study with 50 participants, evaluating each reasoning stage on RULEARN trajectories. Results show LLMs guess correct rules during abduction about 30% of the time vs ~10% for humans, but humans produce higher-quality plans and gather ~20% more diverse observations; during induction, ~40% of human refinements improve hypotheses vs <20% for LLMs, and humans require fewer interactions (~4) per effective induction. These diagnostics localize LLM weaknesses in planning and hypothesis refinement.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: Generative NER methods typically use entity-centric training that ignores non-entity tokens, limiting contextual learning and weakening label boundaries. This leads to unlabeled, noisy, and boundary errors and poor zero-shot generalization.
method: GNER introduces a token-by-token instruction-tuning schema that labels every token in the sentence, explicitly incorporating negative instances ('O') as context and adding BIO tagging to strengthen boundaries. The prompt pairs each token with its predicted label, with regularization via class-order shuffling and external entity sampling. This reduces noisy and boundary errors (Table 1) and yields strong gains in zero-shot NER, e.g., LLaMA-7B improves by ~12.7 F1 over UniNER on CrossNER while sharing the same backbone and data (Table 2; lines 177, 101–112, 127–129, 261).
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Including context and BIO labels lengthens generations, causing additions/omissions/substitutions that misalign outputs with the source text. Standard LCS post-processing is slow (O(N^2)) and brittle, making conversion to structured entities inaccurate and inefficient.
method: They propose an optimized LCS Matching algorithm tailored to NER that parses x(y) pairs with regex, aligns generated tokens to the input via hierarchical matching—trivial and omission cases in O(N), and a fast LCS in O(N log N)—and adds Back Tokenization to handle vocabulary/OOV mismatches. This accelerates alignment up to 17.3× on long sequences and improves accuracy; removing Back Tokenization or the LCS step degrades performance (Tables 5–6; lines 187–193, 415–418, 267–274, 279–286).
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Beam search often fails or degrades performance in entity-centric generative NER because it encourages repeated or inconsistent answers and cannot revise early mistakes.
method: Under GNER’s token-by-token schema, beam search enables a self-correction mechanism: later context can revise earlier token labels within beams. Increasing beam size consistently improves zero-shot F1 across backbones, whereas UniNER’s performance declines with larger beams. Case studies show corrections from 'O' to proper B/I labels when later tokens provide decisive cues (Table 7 and Fig. 6/Table 10; lines 317–321, 288–298, 299–307, 474–491).
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Unsupervised reconstruction-based time series anomaly detectors can inadvertently learn to reconstruct unlabeled anomalies in training data, making it hard to capture normal patterns and detect discontinuous anomalies.
method: Leverage implicit neural representations (INRs) and their spectral bias to prioritize low-frequency (smooth) normal signals while underfitting high-frequency, discontinuous anomalies. A feed-forward transformer predicts the full INR parameters for a given time series, enabling one-shot inference without per-series fine-tuning. INR parameterization treats the series as a continuous function over time, enhancing temporal continuity modeling and sensitivity to anomalous discontinuities.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: Time series exhibit distinct trend/seasonal components and complex inter- and intra-channel dependencies that simple reconstruction functions struggle to capture, especially in multivariate settings.
method: Design a novel INR continuous function as the sum of three components: (1) a trend term modeled via a low-degree polynomial with coefficients predicted by FC layers; (2) a seasonal term modeled via a Fourier series with learnable coefficients; and (3) a residual term captured by a group-based architecture with M global layers (to learn inter-channel information) and N group layers per group (to learn intra-channel details), whose outputs are concatenated. Ablations show both the decomposition and the group-based residual block significantly improve reconstruction and anomaly detection.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: Subtle or channel-local anomalies may be weak in the raw space and hard to separate from normal patterns, reducing detection sensitivity.
method: Introduce a frozen pre-trained LLM encoder (GPT-2) to map the input time series into a feature domain that amplifies anomaly fluctuations across both time and channel dimensions. The encoder’s self-attention aggregates inter-channel cues so multiple variables manifest anomalous fluctuations in the same interval, increasing separability for the downstream INR reconstruction. Ablation studies show consistent gains on multivariate benchmarks, confirming the encoder’s effectiveness.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: Sentence-level SFT/DPO and distillation struggle to ensure step-wise correctness in mathematical reasoning, while online RL methods are expensive and unstable. Existing step-DPO typically assumes zero value at each step, ignoring state-dependent future value and leading to suboptimal step-level optimization.
method: Formulate step-level reasoning as an MDP and, using the Bellman optimality equation with a step-level Bradley–Terry model, derive a step-wise DPO objective that includes a dynamic value margin term V*(s_w^{t+1}) − V*(s_l^{t+1}) reflecting the difference in optimal future value between preferred and rejected next-states. Approximate V* with a PRM score to obtain a practical, state-dependent margin, and introduce a reward-equivalence scaling γ to control the margin; standard step-DPO is recovered at γ=0. Ablations show dynamic margins outperform no-margin and fixed-margin variants on MATH/GSM8k.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: Multilingual instruction tuning often relies on machine-translated data, but it is unclear whether translated instructions are sufficient to match native-language data and whether translated benchmarks faithfully surface any differences.
method: Run controlled monolingual instruction tuning in Spanish, Russian, and Chinese using either language-native Aya data or size-matched English Aya translated with Google Translate and Cohere Command R (instructions and responses translated separately). Evaluate eight base LLMs (Llama 2, Gemma 2B/7B, Qwen1.5 0.5B–14B) on native vs translated and structured vs generative benchmarks (TyDi QA, C-Eval, CMMLU, XQuAD, MGSM, MT-/HT-MMLU, and open-ended QA scored by GPT-4-Turbo and Command R+), plus a correlation analysis between native performance and the native–translated gap. Results show native-tuned models outperform on language-native and generative tasks (TyDi QA, C-Eval/CMMLU, XQuAD, open-ended QA), while gaps largely vanish on translated structured tests (MGSM, MT-/HT-MMLU); the gap is stronger as base performance rises and for generative evaluations.
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: If a performance gap exists when using translated instruction data, what causes it more: translation defects (translationese/errors) or missing language-specific knowledge?
method: Construct round-trip translated (RTT) instruction sets by translating native Russian/Chinese Aya items to English and back (X→en→X), preserving native knowledge while introducing translationese. Train and compare models on RTT data versus single-pass English-origin translations on native tests (TyDi QA ru, CMMLU zh). RTT matches or outperforms English-origin translations—consistently on CMMLU and often on TyDi QA—indicating that missing native, language-specific knowledge in instructions is more detrimental than translation defects.
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: When native instruction data are unavailable, how can we mitigate the native–translated performance gap in multilingual instruction tuning?
method: Apply regularization during SFT: (a) reduce learning rate from 1e-4 to 1e-6; (b) perform multilingual instruction tuning by mixing eight languages with balanced sampling. Across larger models, these settings can close or narrow the gap on native structured benchmarks (e.g., lower LR benefits TyDi QA; multilingual tuning helps CMMLU), but they fail to remove the gap on generative tasks (XQuAD, open-ended QA), where native-trained models retain a clear advantage.
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: Existing visual commonsense benchmarks often rely on template-based, text-only construction that introduces textual bias, inflates/unstabilizes scores, and under-represents models’ need for visual grounding. A human-aligned, image-aware evaluation covering general visual attributes is missing.
method: Introduce IMAGENETVC, a human-annotated benchmark of 4,076 QA pairs spanning 1,000 ImageNet categories and five attribute groups (color, shape, material, component, others), including region-based questions. It supports zero-/few-shot evaluation of both LLMs and visually-augmented LMs (VaLMs), and incorporates multiple image sources (ImageNet, web search, Stable Diffusion synthesis) to reflect shared visual properties across related images. Empirically, IMAGENETVC shows reduced prompt variance versus template-based ViComTe and is more challenging (e.g., GPT-Neo-1.3B achieves 40% on ViComTe color vs 28% on IMAGENETVC), while human assessment confirms higher diversity/difficulty and superior factuality over baselines.
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: It is unclear at what scale LLMs begin to exhibit visual commonsense from text pretraining alone and how in-context learning (ICL) impacts both performance and calibration on visual commonsense.
method: Evaluate a broad suite of LLMs (OPT, GPT, Pythia, Falcon, LLaMA) under zero-/few-shot cloze-style prompting with probability calibration. Analysis shows an emergent capability threshold around ~1.3B parameters on the component sub-task: sub-1.3B models hover near random (~50%), while larger models improve (e.g., OPT-30B: 59% zero-shot, 66% few-shot). ICL consistently boosts accuracy and stabilizes prompt variance (e.g., LLaMA-7B average improves by ~12 points to 62% across sub-tasks) and markedly improves calibration on components (confidence–accuracy correlation r increases from 0.57 to 0.98).
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: Prior evaluations underplay VaLMs by omitting relevant images and lack a principled assessment of how image source/quantity and integration strategy affect visual commonsense.
method: Propose a VaLM evaluation protocol that supplies multiple relevant images per QA via web retrieval and synthesis, ranks them with CLIP, and averages predictions over the top-K (K=10). Assess both prefix-based (BLIP-2, MAGMA) and ensemble-based (Z-LaVI) VaLMs against their frozen LLM backbones. Findings: visual grounding substantially improves color/material commonsense (e.g., BLIP-2 + OPT-2.7B yields +18.9 and +21.7 absolute points on color and material vs OPT-2.7B), with smaller gains on shape; CLIP-ranked images outperform random/no images, and both search and synthetic sources help; performance increases with more top-ranked images up to ~10, after which gains saturate.
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: Human evaluation of generated text is costly, hard to reproduce, and often unstable, making fair comparison of NLP systems difficult.
method: Propose LLM evaluation: present large language models with the same instructions, samples, and 5-point Likert questions used in human studies, then parse their free-form outputs to extract scores (simple regex-based rules). Validate on open-ended story generation (WritingPrompts) comparing human-written vs GPT-2 outputs across grammaticality, cohesiveness, likability, and relevance. Strong instruction-following LLMs (text-davinci-003, ChatGPT) significantly prefer human-written stories and align with expert judgments (e.g., positive Kendall’s τ up to 0.43 on relevance), while weaker models (T0, text-curie-001) fail to reliably distinguish. text-davinci-003 also shows higher inter-sample consistency than T0/curie, demonstrating LLM evaluation’s feasibility as an alternative to expert annotation.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: Assessing the quality of adversarial text attacks (fluency and meaning preservation) typically requires time-consuming human judgments.
method: Apply LLM evaluation to synonym substitution attacks (TextFooler, PWWS, BAE) on AG-News titles, asking ChatGPT (and supplementary text-davinci-003) to rate fluency of single titles and meaning preservation between benign–adversarial pairs. A sanity check with identical benign pairs yields a perfect 5.00, indicating task understanding. LLM ratings mirror expert trends—adversarial < benign and BAE > TextFooler/PWWS—though LLMs are generally more lenient; in cases of teacher disagreement, ChatGPT matches the majority preference. This shows LLM evaluation generalizes beyond standard texts to perturbed, adversarial inputs.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: For LLM-based evaluation to be practical, it must be robust to prompt formatting and sampling stochasticity.
method: Investigate robustness by varying instructions (adding a "you are a human worker" persona; requiring explanations) and decoding temperature for text-davinci-003. Absolute score shifts are small—typically <0.1 for grammaticality/cohesiveness and ≤0.25 for likability/relevance—and do not alter the relative ranking (human-written > GPT-2) across attributes. Lower temperatures slightly reduce mean scores and increase agreement among sampled outputs. These results indicate LLM evaluation’s stability under reasonable prompt and decoding variations.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: Open-source Chinese medical datasets are largely Q&A without explicit instructions, which weakens LLM instruction-following and generalization, especially in multi-turn dialogues.
method: Introduce question-oriented instruction generation via chained examples. Examples are LLM-scored and ordered from low to high quality, then linked in a chain-of-thought style prompt (E1 + instruct + E2 + instruct* + ... + EN + instruct* + target question) to drive step-by-step refinement. This progressive context encourages the model to learn why later examples are better and produce higher-quality instructions, outperforming standard few-shot prompting and training without instructions (ablation Tables 10–11; method details in Sec. 3.2).
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Preference datasets for RLHF/DPO in the medical domain contain noisy pairs (contradictory, indistinguishable, or overly easy), which degrade training of reward models and downstream alignment.
method: Train multiple reward models (five with different seeds) and compute a per-sample preference distance rθ(x,yc)−rθ(x,yr); average distances across models to stabilize judgments. Filter out samples whose distances are consistently negative (label contradictions) or excessively large (trivial/easy) based on thresholds, thereby denoising the preference data. This ensemble-based filtering improves performance across multi-turn/single-turn dialogues, terminology, and benchmarks (Sec. 3.3; Table 12).
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Chinese medical LLMs risk leaking memorized training data at deployment, threatening patient privacy.
method: Propose PPVD, a four-step privacy-preserving vector database approach: (1) model memory search by splitting training samples into prompt/label and detecting memorization via ROUGE-L threshold; (2) build a high-risk database using intermediate embeddings of memorized samples; (3) construct a secure database by prompting a general LLM with the prompt and concatenating its response, then extracting intermediate embeddings; (4) match-and-replace at inference by cosine similarity between current intermediate embeddings and the high-risk DB, returning the secure embedding’s content when above threshold. On 100k samples, only 1.81% were highly memorized; PPVD reduced their average similarity from >0.85 to 0.58 (~0.27 drop) with minimal utility loss (Sec. 4; Tables 14–15; privacy reduction also summarized in abstract).
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Transformers achieve strong performance but incur quadratic time/memory with sequence length, while classic RNNs are memory-efficient but hard to parallelize and suffer from gradient issues. There is a need for a sequence model that retains transformer-like training efficiency with RNN-like inference efficiency.
method: Introduce RWKV, a Receptance Weighted Key Value architecture that replaces dot-product attention with an exact linear attention variant (WKV) using a channel-wise, time-decayed weight vector W and receptance gating. It employs token shift to mix current and previous inputs, and a time-dependent softmax for stable gradients. The design supports transformer-like, time-parallel training (matrix multiplications in parallel with a lightweight serial WKV scan) and RNN-like, time-sequential inference, achieving O(Td) time and O(d) memory at inference without approximation.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: Training deep, RNN-like models at scale is hindered by inefficient sequential kernels and unstable/slow early training dynamics from standard initializations.
method: Develop a custom CUDA kernel for the WKV operator to execute the sequential computation efficiently as a single kernel on accelerators. Propose small-init embeddings with an extra LayerNorm to accelerate and stabilize early training, and a custom initialization strategy (including depth-shaped time_decay/time_first and many zero-initialized weights) to improve convergence and gradient flow. These optimizations enable scalable, stable training of deep RWKV stacks.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: It is unclear whether an RNN-formulable architecture can scale to billions of parameters and remain competitive with transformers on NLP tasks and long-context scenarios.
method: Train and release RWKV models from 169M to 14B parameters on The Pile, showing transformer-like scaling laws (loss vs. compute fit with r^2≈0.994) and FLOP-matched competitiveness versus Pythia/OPT/BLOOM across standard NLP benchmarks. Demonstrate effective long-context use via progressive finetuning from 1K to 8K tokens (yielding lower test loss), strong performance on Long Range Arena tasks related to text/code, and linear-time, constant-memory inference on CPUs/GPUs.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: There is no unified understanding of when and how to incorporate non-text modalities into retrieval-augmented generation for LLMs, despite its promise for improving factuality, reasoning, interpretability, and robustness.
method: The paper formalizes multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as a coherent paradigm, defines its background (multimodal learning and RAG), scopes five key modalities (image, code, structured knowledge, audio, video), and systematically curates ≈145 relevant works via ACL Anthology/Google Scholar keyword search with manual filtering and trend analysis. This establishes common terminology and boundaries for the field and positions multimodal RAG as an emerging method family.
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: Designers of multimodal RAG systems lack consolidated guidance on retrieval targets, fusion stages, and synthesis procedures across modalities and tasks.
method: The survey organizes prior work modality-by-modality and application-by-application, detailing end-to-end pipelines and shared patterns: e.g., image VQA using OCR/object detection plus dense passage retrieval and fusion (RA-VQA), multimodal retrieval of text+images (MuRAG), captioning with retrieved visual or textual contexts; code tasks using hybrid sparse+dense retrievers for generation/completion/APR; structured knowledge QA/dialogue using dense, cross-encoder, and multi-grained retrieval with input-, model-, and inference-time synthesis; audio tasks for captioning and data augmentation; and video-grounded dialogue/captioning with retrieval. It highlights where retrieval is injected (input prompts, model adaptation, inference-time interaction) and how these choices improve factuality, reasoning, interpretability, and robustness.
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: There is no clear roadmap for scaling and strengthening multimodal RAG along reasoning, indexing, and training-time integration.
method: The paper proposes concrete future directions: (i) retrieval-augmented multimodal reasoning (e.g., Multimodal-CoT) to generate rationales then answers; (ii) building a unified multimodal knowledge index with compact dense representations and scalable search (e.g., locality-sensitive hashing) across modalities; and (iii) integrating retrieval/tool use during pretraining (e.g., LaMDA/Toolformer/REALM-style), including discussion of labeling challenges and potential neuro-symbolic solutions (e.g., prototype learning and deepKNN).
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: LLMs have strong reasoning but cannot directly process raw multimodal robot sensory inputs or issue valid low-level actions; closing the loop for interactive perception under partial observability (epistemic uncertainty) is challenging.
method: Introduce Matcha, a modular agent that uses an LLM (few-shot, no fine-tuning) prompted to "chat" with the environment. The LLM emits constrained, case-sensitive function-like commands (e.g., 'robot.knock on()', 'robot.touch()', 'robot.weigh()', 'robot.pick up()') that a language-conditioned execution policy carries out. Modality-specific perception modules (ViLD for open-vocabulary vision, a trained impact-sound classifier, lightweight haptics and weight descriptors) translate sensor feedback into natural-language sentences. These feedback sentences, together with the last command, are fed back to the LLM for iterative reasoning and selection of epistemic actions. Natural language serves as a uniform interface, making modules plug-and-play and enabling interactive planning grounded in multimodal context.
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: Picking the correct object by latent material properties not visually discernible requires fusing heterogeneous modalities and reasoning over uncertain or ambiguous cues.
method: Build a simulated tabletop picking task (CoppeliaSim with NICOL) where the robot must select objects by material. Provide two auditory regimes: (i) distinct material labels with probabilities, and (ii) indistinct human-like adjectives (e.g., 'tinkling', 'dull'). Matcha plans which epistemic actions to perform and fuses multimodal language feedback to disambiguate. Quantitatively, it surpasses a sound-only theoretical baseline when using distinct descriptions (90.57% vs 89.18%) and exceeds chance with ambiguous cues (56.67% vs 33.33%). Case studies show multimodal feedback correcting misclassifications from sound alone and highlight failure modes (glass vs ceramic similarity).
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: Grounding LLMs to a robot’s executable action space without retraining is difficult; smaller LLMs often produce non-executable commands.
method: Use a 5-shot prompt that enumerates the robot’s exact, case-sensitive action APIs with exemplars, constraining generation to the robot’s capabilities. Empirically, a strong LLM (text-davinci-003) adheres to the API and achieves high success, while a weaker model (text-ada-001) frequently violates the format and performs poorly (distinct: 28.57%; indistinct: 19.05%). The promptable, language-mediated design allows easy extension to new actions and perception modules by updating the skill inventory.
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
method: Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
method: Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: Open-ended video reasoning answers are hard to evaluate reliably; exact-match or simple similarity metrics can misjudge semantically correct but phrased-differently outputs and are vulnerable to confounding content.
method: Develop a two-step objective evaluation: (1) compute semantic similarity between the model output and reference answer and require it to exceed an 80% threshold; (2) compare similarity against three carefully crafted distractors and mark outputs incorrect if any distractor is closer. Complement this with subjective evaluation via GPT-4o scoring logic, factuality, description accuracy, and conciseness, all organized over 14 diverse video reasoning tasks.
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: Language-guided robot planners can generate opaque and logically fragile action sequences, while symbolic planners require explicit, machine-readable PDDL problem descriptions (objects, initial state, goals) that are hard to derive automatically from natural-language instructions and visual scenes.
method: Introduce ViLaIn, a modular vision-language interpreter that generates full PDDL problem descriptions from a linguistic instruction and a scene image without additional training. It (1) detects task-relevant objects using an open-vocabulary detector (Grounding-DINO) with domain-knowledge–augmented text queries (e.g., “round cutting board,” “kitchen knife”) to form PDDL objects; (2) infers the initial state by captioning each detected object with BLIP-2 and prompting GPT-4 with few-shot examples to synthesize PDDL predicates; and (3) derives the goal specification by prompting GPT-4 with the instruction plus the estimated objects and initial state. The assembled PD is then passed to a symbolic planner (Fast Downward) to obtain a plan, yielding interpretable, planner-verifiable task specifications.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: Automatically generated PDDL problems often contain syntactic inconsistencies or contradictory/insufficient predicates that make planning fail; manual debugging breaks scalability and interpretability.
method: Propose Corrective Re-prompting (CR) with Chain-of-Thought: when Fast Downward returns an error, ViLaIn re-prompts GPT-4 with the original inputs, the generated PD, the planner’s error message, and few-shot exemplars; GPT-4 first explains the error (CoT) and then outputs a refined PD. This loop is repeated up to n times, substantially improving robustness. In ablations on the cooking domain, removing CR drops Rsyntax/Rplan/Rall from 0.99/0.99/0.71 to 0.60/0.18/0.09, and removing CoT further degrades performance, demonstrating that planner-feedback–guided, reasoning-based refinement is critical.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: There is no standard multimodal benchmark or general metrics to evaluate PDDL problem generation quality across domains.
method: Release the ProDG dataset spanning three domains (Cooking, Blocksworld, Hanoi) with paired instructions, scene images, and PDDL domains/problems, and introduce four domain-agnostic metrics: Rsyntax (VAL-based syntactic correctness), Rplan (valid plan existence via Fast Downward), Rpart (recall of objects/initial/goal parts), and Rall (all-parts exact recall). On ProDG, ViLaIn achieves ≥99% Rsyntax across domains and Rplan of 0.99 (Cooking), 0.94 (Blocksworld), and 0.58 (Hanoi), quantifying strengths and remaining challenges in PD generation.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: Spurious correlations in NLP models are mostly studied at token/syntax levels, but models also rely on higher-level semantic concepts (e.g., food, music). Because datasets lack concept annotations, it has been hard to detect and quantify concept-level shortcuts learned during fine-tuning or in-context learning (ICL).
method: Use ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) to annotate whether each input contains a selected set of frequent, sentiment-irrelevant concepts per dataset, and define a quantitative metric (Bias@C) that measures the discrepancy in model accuracy between inputs with vs. without a concept across labels (Section 2.2). Evaluate DistilBERT and LLaMA2 under fine-tuning on original vs. concept-biased data, and GPT-3.5 under ICL with balanced vs. concept-biased prompts. Results show concept-level spurious correlations exist and intensify with biased training/prompts, including sign flips of Bias@C consistent with biased label distributions (e.g., Yelp ‘service’, Amazon Shoe ‘size’) and reduced robust accuracy on concept-containing texts (Tables 3–4, 7; Sections 4.1–4.2).
paper_title: Explore Spurious Correlations at the Concept Level in Language Models for Text Classification

problem: Mitigating concept-level shortcuts is challenging: downsampling discards data and harms utility, while masking a few high-PMI tokens cannot remove concept-level bias because concepts have many surface realizations.
method: Propose counterfactual upsampling via LLM-generated data: sample minority-label texts that lack a target concept and ask ChatGPT to inject the concept while preserving the original sentiment/label, yielding a balanced, upsampled training set (D_c up-bal). Compared to downsampling (D_c down-bal) and token masking (D_c mask), this approach more effectively reduces concept bias and maintains or improves utility. On DistilBERT, mean absolute Bias@C drops from 4.90% (original) to 2.74% with upsampling and Acc@C increases from 79.24% to 80.38% (Table 6; Section 5.2); similar trends hold for LLaMA2 (Tables 8–9).
paper_title: Explore Spurious Correlations at the Concept Level in Language Models for Text Classification

problem: CLIP-based deepfake detectors lack effective, domain-tailored prompts to capture diverse and subtle manipulation artifacts, and VLM-based explanation methods underperform at detection. There is a need to adapt CLIP to localize forgeries with minimal supervision while improving generalization and interpretability.
method: Forgery Prompt Learning (FPL) builds Universal Forgery Prompts that combine trainable general-forgery tokens with image-specific tokens produced by an MLP from the CLIP image embedding, appended to a fixed phrase ("forged face"). The CLIP text encoder is frozen, but trainable layer-wise forgery tokens are injected into every Transformer layer to adapt it to deepfake cues. The resulting text embedding is compared patch-wise with CLIP image features to generate a forged attention map Mb via cosine similarity, which acts as a spatial prior to guide global detection. Trained end-to-end using only binary labels (Mb learned unsupervised), FPL improves localization and detection AUC in ablations (both UF-prompts and LF-tokens contribute, with their combination yielding further gains).
paper_title: Rethinking Vision-Language Model in Face Forensics: Multi-Modal Interpretable Forged Face Detector

problem: It is challenging to simultaneously exploit CLIP’s generalizable visual features and deepfake-specific knowledge for robust detection, and to translate these cues into faithful natural-language explanations with an LLM.
method: The Bridge Adapter (EA) consists of Transformer blocks that take intermediate features from the frozen CLIP image encoder (EI) and a deepfake encoder (ED), producing robust fused representations. These are concatenated with ED outputs into F0 and, together with the forged attention map Mb, yield a refined forgery vector f0 for classification. For interpretability, F0 is transformed into a frequency-based token HF that, alongside visual tokens HV from EI, conditions a Vicuna-7B LLM to generate both a judgment and rationale; lightweight MLP projectors align these tokens to the LLM, and LoRA is used for efficient fine-tuning on DDVQA. Ablations show Bri-Ada boosts AUC across datasets, and removing HF drops judgment accuracy by 10.12% and F1, validating the benefit of frequency-informed alignment.
paper_title: Rethinking Vision-Language Model in Face Forensics: Multi-Modal Interpretable Forged Face Detector

problem: Graph-based RAG often retrieves redundant and noisy information (e.g., entire communities or full ego-networks), which inflates token usage and degrades answer quality. A retriever is needed that preserves structured dependencies while minimizing irrelevant content.
method: PathRAG introduces a flow-based pruning algorithm with distance awareness to select key relational paths between retrieved nodes. Starting from a source node, resources are propagated along edges with a decay factor α, combined with an early-stopping threshold θ to prune negligible nodes; path reliability is scored as the average resource over its edges, and top-K reliable paths are retained. This algorithm has low time complexity O(N^2/((1-α)θ)) and substantially reduces redundancy. Empirically, PathRAG cuts token consumption by 13.69% versus LightRAG while improving performance across five evaluation dimensions.
paper_title: PathRAG: Pruning Graph-Based Retrieval Augmented Generation with Relational Paths

problem: Flat prompt organization of retrieved graph content loses relational structure and suffers from the 'lost in the middle' effect in long contexts, leading to suboptimal logicality and coherence in LLM outputs.
method: PathRAG converts each selected graph path into a textual relational path by concatenating the involved nodes’ and edges’ descriptions in traversal order, preserving the end-to-end semantics. It then orders paths by reliability in ascending order so the most reliable path appears at the end of the prompt (a high-retention position for LLMs), with the query placed at the beginning. Ablations show path-based prompting outperforms flat formatting, and reliability-based ordering outperforms random or hop-first ordering.
paper_title: PathRAG: Pruning Graph-Based Retrieval Augmented Generation with Relational Paths

problem: Instruction-tuned student LLMs are typically distilled from proprietary teacher LLMs in a one-way manner that ignores feedback on the student’s weaknesses; prior adversarial KD methods require access to teacher weights/gradients, which is infeasible for black-box LLMs.
method: Introduce an adversarial knowledge distillation framework that treats a single proprietary LLM as three prompt-driven roles: Teacher (produce responses), Referee (score two responses), and Generator (create new instructions). Each iteration runs a three-stage loop: (1) imitation—fine-tune the student on teacher outputs with the standard autoregressive LM objective; (2) discrimination—have the LLM-as-judge score teacher vs. student answers on a growing Cache Pool, mitigate positional bias by swapping answer order and averaging the two runs, and mark an instruction as hard if the score gap ≥ τ (τ=1.0); (3) generation—condition the LLM-as-generator on sampled hard instructions to create domain- and task-matched new hard instructions, and also generate easy long-tail ones to avoid catastrophic forgetting, enforcing diversity via ROUGE-L < 0.7 and a 1:1 hard:easy ratio, then refresh the Train Pool and expand the Cache Pool. This forms a min-max game over hard samples without accessing teacher parameters or logits.
paper_title: Lion: Adversarial Distillation of Proprietary Large Language Models

problem: Achieving strong instruction-following and reasoning performance with limited, fully automatic data while distilling from a black-box teacher.
method: Initialize Train/Cache Pools with Alpaca’s 52k instructions and run three AKD iterations, generating 6k new instructions per iteration (total ~70k). Train LLaMA-based students (7B/13B) only on teacher-produced responses. Empirically, Lion-13B reaches 98.38% of ChatGPT on Vicuna-Instructions (GPT-4 judged) and substantially surpasses Vicuna-13B on hard reasoning: +55.4% average on BIG-Bench Hard and +16.7% on AGIEval (zero-shot, no CoT). Ablations show robust behavior, with τ≈1.0–1.5 and a 1:1 hard:easy generation ratio performing best.
paper_title: Lion: Adversarial Distillation of Proprietary Large Language Models

problem: Prior work claimed language models encode entity states, but evaluations may be confounded by trivial cases that don’t require tracking changes over discourse.
method: Reanalyze Li et al. (2021) and show that 62.7% of probed states equal the initial state and 32.4% are empty finals, leaving only 12.4% truly non-trivial; the probing classifier achieves 86.8% on trivial but only 3.1% on non-trivial cases, undermining earlier conclusions about genuine entity tracking. This establishes the need for a more controlled test of discourse-driven state tracking.
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Evaluate entity tracking in a way that is robust to lexical shortcuts, distributional priors, and slot-filling, and that requires temporal composition of operations.
method: Introduce a programmatically generated benchmark in a boxes-and-objects world: initial states followed by 0–12 operations (move/put/remove), with cloze-style queries asking for a box’s contents after each step. The dataset is built to satisfy four desiderata (avoid pretraining priors, forbid single-word giveaways, enforce low lexical overlap via AltForms, and prevent slot-filling via initial-state ‘signatures’). Context-dependent variants (Move contents of Box N to M; ambiguous referring expressions with optional adjectives) further require using discourse context to resolve underspecification.
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Determine whether pretrained LLMs actually track entities, what training factors make this ability surface, and whether smaller text-only models can learn it.
method: Using 2-shot prompts, compare GPT-3, GPT-3.5, and Flan-T5: only GPT-3.5 text-davinci-003 exhibits non-trivial entity tracking and remains above a strong random baseline even after many operations; performance persists under low lexical overlap (AltForms) and degrades with increased context dependence (AmbiRef/MoveContents). Ablations across GPT-3 vs. GPT-3.5 indicate models pretrained on code systematically outperform text-only counterparts, including code-davinci-002 without RLHF, implicating code pretraining as key. Finetuning T5-base on the new datasets yields near-perfect accuracy on the base split, generalizes to longer sequences and novel vocab with some degradation, and stays above random under AltForms when trained on longer sequences; context-dependent operations remain challenging.
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Few-shot node classification on text-attributed graphs suffers from extremely limited labeled nodes, and invoking LLMs per node is costly and often ineffective when supervision is scarce.
method: LLM4NG performs class-level node generation: it prompts an LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5) to produce M synthetic labeled texts per class that match the dataset’s node-text format, then obtains sentence embeddings (e.g., Sentence-BERT or e5) for both real and generated texts and augments the graph with these new labeled nodes. The approach is plug-and-play with any GNN and requires only O(M|C|) LLM calls (e.g., 400 vs. 169,343 for TAPE), enabling even zero-shot training with strong accuracy (e.g., PubMed 0-shot 75.36%).
paper_title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Node Generation in Few-Shot Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs

problem: Synthetic nodes must be structurally integrated so their labels can propagate via message passing; naive similarity-based connections are noisy and insufficient.
method: LLM4NG adds edges via a two-stage edge predictor. First, it pre-filters candidate pairs between generated and raw nodes using a cosine similarity threshold δ over sentence embeddings to form E_P. Then it trains an MLP edge predictor as a binary classifier on raw edges vs. sampled non-edges (concatenate pair embeddings, cross-entropy loss) and scores E_P, finally adding the top-k highest-probability edges (and their reverse) to the graph. This structural integration improves few-shot accuracy over treating generated nodes as isolates (w/o A) and is lightweight to train.
paper_title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Node Generation in Few-Shot Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs

problem: KG-based LLM reasoning often relies on verbose natural language prompts that omit key graph structure and require many LLM calls, making multi-hop KGQA retrieval inaccurate and inefficient.
method: LightPROF introduces a relation-centric retrieval pipeline that constructs a refined reasoning graph. It first performs semantic extraction from the query by predicting the required hop count h_q (via a fine-tuned PLM such as BERT) and extracting anchor entities to narrow the search space (Stage1: Semantic Extraction). It then runs a constrained BFS over relations from anchors up to h_q, treating relations as stable retrieval units (Relation Retrieval). Retrieved relation links are scored by an LLM for relevance, top-k are selected, and multiple reasoning paths are sampled to form the final reasoning graph GR (Reasoning Graph Sampling).
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: Injecting KG knowledge as plain text into LLMs loses rich structural cues and creates redundancy, hindering precise multi-hop reasoning and inflating token usage.
method: LightPROF proposes a compact Transformer-based Knowledge Adapter that fuses textual and structural information of each reasoning path into a single token-like representation. Entities and relations are embedded (e.g., with BERT), local triple structure is encoded (StructEmb), and path-level global structure is aggregated (Linear) to form structural features zs, while text features zt are formed by concatenating entity/relation text representations (Fusion + simple concatenation). A KnowledgeEncoder integrates zt and zs into a fused vector zf per path, which the Projector maps into the LLM’s token embedding space to produce a knowledge soft prompt (Φ is a two-layer MLP). This encodes each reasoning path as one LLM-friendly token, reducing input tokens while preserving crucial structure.
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: Updating or fine-tuning LLMs to incorporate up-to-date KG knowledge is costly and risks catastrophic forgetting; there is a need for a parameter-efficient way to inject external KG knowledge into diverse LLMs.
method: LightPROF freezes the LLM and performs mixed prompting by inserting the learned knowledge soft prompts into carefully designed natural-language hard prompts in a chat-style template. Training optimizes next-token likelihood while only tuning the Knowledge Adapter (KnowledgeEncoder + Projector), making the approach plug-and-play for any open-source LLM that accepts token embeddings. Empirically, integrating LightPROF with small LLMs (e.g., LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B) yields strong KGQA performance and efficiency, demonstrating broad compatibility and parameter efficiency.
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: There is no benchmark for automatic live commenting, a task that requires understanding dynamic video content and interacting with surrounding user comments near a timestamp.
method: The authors build the Live Comment Dataset from Bilibili with 2,361 videos and 895,929 timestamped comments spanning 19 categories, enabling alignment of each comment with nearby frames and neighboring comments. They sample nearest m frames (1s interval) and n surrounding comments to provide visual and textual context, and split by video to prevent leakage. They analyze neighbor-comment correlation, showing semantic similarity decays with temporal distance, motivating the use of nearby context. The dataset and code are released to support training and evaluation.
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Generating live comments requires modeling complex dependencies between surrounding video frames and neighboring comments to produce relevant, human-like remarks.
method: They propose two joint-encoder generators. (1) Fusional RNN: a CNN (ResNet-based) encodes each frame and an LSTM aggregates across frames; a hierarchical text encoder uses a word-level LSTM per comment and a sentence-level LSTM with attention over video states so textual representations incorporate visual context; a decoder generates comments attending to both visual and textual encodings. (2) Unified Transformer: a transformer encodes frame embeddings; the text encoder concatenates all surrounding comments into a single word sequence with self-attention and cross-attention to video outputs; the decoder uses three multi-head attentions over previous tokens, video, and text. Both models outperform Seq2Seq baselines across video-only, comment-only, and joint settings (e.g., with both inputs, Recall@1 improves from 12.89 to 17.25/18.01 and MRR to 0.2710/0.2753).
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Reference-based generation metrics (e.g., BLEU/ROUGE) are unreliable for live comments due to diverse valid outputs, necessitating a robust evaluation method.
method: They introduce a retrieval-based evaluation protocol that asks models to rank 100 candidate comments by log-likelihood, mixing one ground-truth, 30 plausible items retrieved via TF-IDF similarity to the video title, 20 frequent/popular generic comments, and the rest random. Performance is measured with Recall@k, Mean Rank, and Mean Reciprocal Rank, enabling consistent comparison of models’ ability to prefer correct comments over distractors.
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
method: ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Standard VLP training has underutilized techniques like whole word masking and image augmentation, partly due to reliance on cached detector features, which may limit cross-modal learning.
method: ViLT incorporates whole word masking during pre-training (masking all wordpieces of a word at 0.15 probability) to force greater reliance on visual context, and applies RandAugment during fine-tuning (N=2, M=9; excluding inversion and cutout). Ablations show these choices consistently improve downstream scores across VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval compared to baselines without them.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Without region supervision, it is challenging to encourage fine-grained alignment between words and visual elements when using patch-based embeddings.
method: ViLT introduces Word Patch Alignment (WPA), computing an optimal-transport-based alignment between word and patch token representations using IPOT. The approximate Wasserstein distance (weighted by 0.1) is added to the ITM loss to regularize cross-modal alignment, with transport plan visualizations indicating meaningful word-to-patch correspondences.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Large vision-and-language (VLP) models (e.g., UNITER) are over-parameterized, making them costly to deploy; it is unknown whether sparse, trainable subnetworks (lottery tickets) exist that retain performance across diverse VL tasks.
method: Conduct the first empirical study of the lottery ticket hypothesis in VLP by applying Iterative Magnitude-based Pruning (IMP) to pre-trained UNITER and re-training from the pre-trained initialization. Across 7 VL tasks (VQA, GQA, VCR, NLVR2, VE, RefCOCO+, Flickr30k IR/TR), identify 'relaxed' winning tickets that maintain ≥99% of full-model accuracy at 50–70% sparsity (e.g., VQA/GQA/RefCOCO+ at 70%, NLVR2/VE/ITR at 60%, VCR at 50%). Show that learned masks are crucial (random pruning underperforms) and that initialization matters (BERT init < UNITER init; shuffled init fails), while rewinding offers little benefit in this setting.
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Finding task-specific lottery tickets via IMP is expensive per task; can a single, task-agnostic sparse subnetwork be discovered that transfers well to many downstream VL tasks?
method: Run IMP on UNITER’s pre-training objectives (MLM/MRM/ITM/WRA) using large image–text corpora to learn a universal pruning mask, then fine-tune this subnetwork on downstream tasks.
