The pre-training–found subnetworks transfer universally, matching on average ~98% (at 60% sparsity) and ~96% (at 70% sparsity) of full-model accuracy over all 7 tasks, and often outperform tickets sourced from individual downstream tasks, especially at higher sparsities.
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Graph rationale identification for graph property prediction struggles with small datasets and typically discards the non-causal environment, leading to poor generalization; prior methods (e.g., DIR) rely on distribution interventions and explicit subgraph construction, which are data-hungry and inefficient.
method: Introduce environment-based data augmentations tailored for rationalization: (1) environment removal (train on rationale-only representations) and (2) environment replacement (for each graph i, combine its rationale with environment representations from other batch graphs j to create B−1 virtual samples). These augmentations are performed in latent space using an aggregation function (e.g., elementwise sum) to form h_(i,j) and are trained so that predictions for both h_r,i and h_(i,j) match the original label y_i, with losses L_rem and L_rep jointly optimized.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: It is unclear whether lottery ticket behaviors persist under different training regimes and architectures, and whether robustness-oriented training can help sparse VL models.
method: Evaluate compatibility across VLP backbones and with adversarial training. For LXMERT, similar lottery-ticket behavior holds (winning tickets around 70% sparsity), whereas ViLT tolerates far less sparsity (~30%) without degradation. Incorporate adversarial training by adding feature-space PGD perturbations and KL regularization during fine-tuning of sparse subnetworks; this improves performance at 60–70% sparsity and effectively allows ~10% higher sparsity to match standard-training accuracies.
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Explicitly decoding/encoding rationale and augmented subgraphs is computationally prohibitive and mixing subgraphs across graphs is technically difficult, which limits scalability and coherence of learned rationales.
method: Propose GREA, a latent-space framework that avoids explicit subgraph construction. A separator (GNN1+MLP1) produces node-level rationale probabilities m; a second encoder (GNN2) yields contextual node embeddings H. Rationale and environment graph representations are computed via masked pooling (h^(r)=Σ(m⊙H), h^(e)=Σ((1−m)⊙H)), and augmented representations are formed by aggregating h^(r)_i with h^(e)_j across the batch. Training alternates between separator and predictor, includes a size regularizer L_reg to control rationale size, and uses rationale-only predictions at inference, yielding higher efficiency and accuracy.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: Edge-selection-based rationalization can produce fragmented, incoherent subgraphs that lose atomic context, reducing interpretability and accuracy on real molecule/polymer data.
method: Learn node-level rationale masks with a lightweight rationale GNN and gating MLP (separator) to select coherent node sets as rationales. The separator gates node embeddings and aggregates them with scatter-add to obtain rationale and environment representations, while a size regularization (gamma) encourages concise rationales. Case studies show the method yields chemically coherent functional groups and improved predictions (e.g., O2 permeability) compared to edge-based selection.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: Grounded vision-language tasks (e.g., grounded captioning) require generating both natural language and object bounding boxes while explicitly aligning words to image regions. Prior systems rely on multiple separate modules (detector + text generator + alignment head), which adds complexity, runtime, and limits generalization.
method: UniTAB models both text and box prediction as a single auto-regressive sequence over a unified vocabulary that contains text tokens and quantized box-coordinate tokens. It introduces special <obj> and </obj> tokens to mark when decoding should switch to box tokens for a phrase and to inline the word–box alignment (the words and the four subsequent box tokens enclosed by <obj> ... </obj> refer to the same entity). A single shared encoder–decoder is trained end-to-end with a language modeling objective, without any explicit object detector or separate alignment head. This simple design significantly improves grounded captioning on Flickr30k Entities, e.g., CIDEr 69.7 vs. 62.5 and F1_all 12.95 vs. 8.44 over prior art.
paper_title: UniTAB: Unifying Text and Box Outputs for Grounded Vision-Language Modeling

problem: Vision-language benchmarks span diverse output formats (text-only, boxes-only, or both), leading to task-specific heads and duplicated models that are parameter-inefficient and hinder cross-task transfer and generalization.
method: UniTAB uses a task-agnostic output sequence (text+box) and a single training objective to support many VL tasks—grounded captioning, phrase/referring grounding, image captioning, and VQA—within one model. This enables multi-task finetuning to learn a single set of parameters across all tasks, improving performance via shared data while avoiding task-specific heads. The unified model achieves competitive or superior results across 7 benchmarks and is parameter efficient (e.g., multi-task finetuning performs comparably or better than separate finetunes while using 7× fewer model parameters) and exhibits zero-shot generalization to new settings such as ImageNet object localization.
paper_title: UniTAB: Unifying Text and Box Outputs for Grounded Vision-Language Modeling

problem: Many datasets lack full grounded annotations, making it challenging to learn both fluent text generation and accurate grounding within a single framework.
method: UniTAB employs a unified language modeling pre-training that predicts text+box sequences conditioned on image and optional input text (randomly empty or a description), and flexibly incorporates heterogeneous supervision. Adding large-scale image–text pairs without boxes (e.g., CC, SBU) improves text outputs (e.g., grounded captioning CIDEr +8.6, COCO CIDEr +3.8, VQA +2.5% vs. pre-training without them), while adding extra box-only annotations from detection datasets boosts both grounding (e.g., RefCOCO/RefCOCO+/RefCOCOg accuracy and Flickr30k F1_all) and text quality (e.g., grounded captioning CIDEr 70.0 vs. 65.6).
paper_title: UniTAB: Unifying Text and Box Outputs for Grounded Vision-Language Modeling

problem: Efficiently adapting large vision-language models like CLIP to few-shot downstream tasks often either forgets valuable prior knowledge (prompt tuning builds a new classifier) or over-relies on pre-trained features (adapter-style tuning), limiting task-specific learning.
method: Task Residual Tuning (TaskRes) freezes the pre-trained text-based classifier (the CLIP text embeddings) and learns a prior-independent residual matrix x that is element-wise added and scaled to form the target classifier t' = t + αx. Only x is optimized while keeping the base classifier and image branch fixed, preserving prior knowledge and enabling flexible, task-specific learning without invoking the text encoder each step. Ablations show t + αx surpasses adapter-style t + αφω(t) on ImageNet few-shot (Table 2), and TaskRes achieves SOTA/SOTA-comparable results across 11 datasets and shots while being simple to implement (Sections 4.2, 5.3.1; Tables 2–3).
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: It is unclear where to best apply efficient tuning in VLMs—on image embeddings, text embeddings, or both—given risks of overfitting and instability in few-shot settings.
method: TaskRes variants compare applying the residual to CLIP’s text branch (TaskRes-T), image branch (TaskRes-I), or both. Empirically, TaskRes-T consistently outperforms TaskRes-I and TaskRes-I&T across 1–16 shots on ImageNet (Table 4), attributed to reduced overfitting and more stable task-level learning when tuning text embeddings rather than the more diverse image embeddings (Sec. 5.4, Variants of TaskRes).
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: There is limited understanding of how difficult it is to transfer a pre-trained VLM to a given task and how this difficulty should guide adaptation.
method: Define a relative transfer difficulty metric as the ratio of a baseline random classifier’s precision (1/K) to the pre-trained model’s zero-shot precision, and analyze learned residual magnitudes. Across 11 datasets and multiple shot settings, the magnitude of the learned task residual positively correlates with this difficulty (Fig. 4, Fig. 5), e.g., ImageNet is easiest while EuroSAT is hardest; correspondingly, TaskRes yields large gains on high-difficulty tasks (e.g., EuroSAT 1-shot +23.71%) (Sec. 5.5).
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: Scaling pretrained language models to many tasks with minimal tunable parameters remains challenging: adapter- and prefix-tuning still introduce sizable per-layer/block parameters and are usually trained per task.
method: Use a single shared hypernetwork conditioned on hyper-embeddings that encode task, layer-id, and block-type to generate all weights for both prefix-tuning (HyperPrefix) and adapter-tuning (HyperPELT) across the T5 encoder–decoder. For prefixes, expand the hyper-embedding to the prefix length and project it directly to prefix key/value vectors, eliminating the per-block embedding matrix and MLP of standard prefix-tuning; for adapters, generate the down/up projection weights for adapters inserted in parallel to the FFN with a learnable scaling. Only the hypernetwork, input embeddings (task/layer/block), and layer norms are trained; PLM weights are frozen. On GLUE multi-tasking, this cuts trained parameters per task by about 11× vs Prefix-tuning while improving average performance (e.g., HyperPELT 87.09 with 0.24% trained params/task).
paper_title: HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks

problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
method: VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.
paper_title: HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks

problem: There is no consensus on how the number of negative samples (K) in contrastive learning affects downstream classification. Existing surrogate bounds relating contrastive loss to supervised loss either apply only when K is large enough (K+1 ≥ C) or become prohibitively large as K grows due to label collision/coverage terms, failing to explain the large-K benefit.
method: Derive new surrogate bounds that relate the mean supervised cross-entropy loss (with a mean classifier) R_μ-supv(f) to the contrastive (InfoNCE) loss R_cont(f) for any K and C. Provide both an upper (Theorem 1) and a lower bound (Theorem 2) with a constant coefficient on R_cont(f) independent of K and C, and intercept terms ΔU and ΔL. Using the shared log-sum-exp structure of the two losses and Jensen’s inequality (with bounded feature norm ‖f(x)‖2 ≤ L and conditional independence x ⟂ x+ | c+), show the surrogate gap ΔU−ΔL shrinks as O(1/K) under uniform class priors, so larger K tightens the proxy while remaining valid even for small K. Empirically validate tightness versus prior bounds on CIFAR-10/100 and Wiki-3029, where prior bounds diverge or are too loose.
paper_title: On the Surrogate Gap between Contrastive and Supervised Losses

problem: It is unclear whether small K fundamentally prevents achieving optimal downstream performance, and prior narratives suggest a collision–coverage trade-off that would imply an intrinsic optimal K.
method: Analyze the feasible region in the (R_cont, R_μ-supv) plane by combining the surrogate bounds with essential lower bounds for each loss to identify the achievable limits R*cont and R*μ-supv. Show the optimal point (R*cont, R*μ-supv) always remains attainable for any K, while increasing K mainly shrinks the surrogate gap (through ΔU and ΔL) and eases estimation rather than changing the optimum. Support this with synthetic experiments where learning trajectories lie between the theoretical bounds with matching slopes and reduced variance as K grows.
paper_title: On the Surrogate Gap between Contrastive and Supervised Losses

problem: Existing factuality metrics for abstractive summarization correlate well with human judgments on text-only inputs but ignore the vision modality, making them unreliable for multimodal (image+text) summarization.
method: Propose CLIPBERTSCORE, a zero-shot multimodal factuality metric that combines image–summary similarity from CLIPScore (using CLIP RN50x64) with document–summary precision from BERTScore (using RoBERTa-large-MNLI), aggregated via a tunable weighted sum α (α=0.25 tuned on validation). This simple combination outperforms text-only baselines and an existing multimodal metric (MMAE) and matches fine-tuned alternatives on combined judgments, while remaining robust across MUFAME, WikiHowFact, FOIL, BISON, and FRANK benchmarks.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: There is no meta-evaluation benchmark with human judgments to assess multimodal factuality metrics that must consider both document and image consistency.
method: Create MUFAME, a step-level WikiHow-based meta-evaluation with AMT annotations that separately label factuality w.r.t. the document and the image and define a combined judgment; also introduce WikiHowFact, a ranking task that requires selecting the correct summary from distractors under document-only, image-only, or combined prompts. Use MUFAME to tune CLIPBERTSCORE’s α and show higher correlations with human judgments than text-only metrics and MMAE, and competitive performance with a fine-tuned triplet network.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: Multimodal summarization models can hallucinate and underutilize visual information; practical training methods are needed to improve factuality with respect to both text and images.
method: Leverage CLIPBERTSCORE as a training signal in two ways: (1) Visual guidance—rank and select important images via CLIPBERTSCORE and add an auxiliary guidance loss to encourage attention to selected images, improving image precision and factuality metrics on MSMO with minimal ROUGE trade-off; (2) Self-critical sequence training—use CLIPBERTSCORE (upweighted) alternated with ROUGE-2 as rewards under REINFORCE (high RL weight), yielding consistent gains in BERTScore, FactCC, DAE, and QuestEval on MMSS/MSMO and statistically significant human-rated factuality improvements.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: Existing tests for unimodal collapse in vision-language models largely depend on task accuracy or modality ablations, which can mismeasure modality use when predictions are wrong and are ill-defined for image-sentence alignment; practitioners need a performance-agnostic way to quantify each modality’s contribution at sample and dataset levels.
method: Introduce MM-SHAP, a model-agnostic multimodality score grounded in Shapley values that quantifies how much text and vision contribute to a specific prediction. Tokens from both modalities are jointly masked via Monte Carlo sampling of coalitions (≈2^p+1 subsets), replacing text with [MASK] and zeroing adaptive-size image patches to balance token counts across modalities. Absolute SHAP contributions are aggregated per modality to define T-SHAP and V-SHAP as proportional degrees, leveraging Shapley properties (efficiency, symmetry, additivity) to support sample- and dataset-level analysis independent of accuracy.
paper_title: MM-SHAP: A Performance-agnostic Metric for Measuring Multimodal Contributions in Vision and Language Models & Tasks

problem: It is unclear how different VL architectures and fine-tuning regimes actually rely on modalities across tasks and datasets, and whether reported unimodal collapse trends are uniform across models.
method: Apply MM-SHAP to six VL models (LXMERT, CLIP, and four ALBEF variants) across four tasks/datasets (ISA on MSCOCO/VQA/GQA, VQA, GQA, and the fine-grained VALSE benchmark), comparing modality reliance to accuracy. Findings show model-specific modality use: CLIP is near-balanced (~50% T-SHAP), LXMERT leans visual (e.g., ~41.9% T-SHAP), and ALBEF variants lean textual (~56–62% T-SHAP). Fine-tuning alters modality degree (e.g., VQA fine-tuning increases textual reliance), and MM-SHAP exhibits low correlation with accuracy and remains stable under distribution shifts (e.g., counting adversarial), demonstrating it complements performance metrics and reveals non-uniform, task- and model-dependent collapse directions.
paper_title: MM-SHAP: A Performance-agnostic Metric for Measuring Multimodal Contributions in Vision and Language Models & Tasks

problem: Current multimodal benchmarks cover few tasks and mostly test rudimentary skills, making them inadequate to track LVLM progress toward multitask AGI across diverse domains and inputs.
method: Introduce MMT-Bench, a curated benchmark of 31,325 multi-choice visual questions spanning 32 meta-tasks and 162 subtasks across 13 visual input types and multiple modalities (image, text, video, point cloud). It uses a hierarchical task taxonomy, unified metadata, and question/answer generation via manual rules or ChatGPT with hard distractors, while ensuring visual grounding; evaluation follows OpenCompass-style robust answer extraction.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: Developers lack a principled way to understand relationships among multimodal tasks, predict performance on new tasks, and identify in-domain vs out-of-domain regions for LVLMs.
method: Construct a task map by encoding each subtask as a task vector: LoRA-fine-tune a probing LVLM (QwenVL-Chat) per subtask to obtain weight deltas, compute cosine distances, and analyze Kendall’s tau ranking correlations. Hierarchical clustering over this map reveals in-domain clusters (e.g., recognition/description) and out-of-domain clusters (e.g., fine-grained localization, GUI/tables), enabling prediction and targeted improvement.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: There is limited, standardized evidence on how prompting strategies (multi-image inputs, coordinate formats, visual referring prompts) impact LVLM performance across heterogeneous tasks.
method: Within MMT-Bench, evaluate 30 LVLMs under controlled prompting settings: true multi-image vs single-image concatenation on 28 multi-image tasks, visual referring prompts vs text-specified coordinates (normalized vs pixel), and combined modes. Findings show multi-image prompting substantially boosts performance (including for some single-image LVLMs like BLIP2), while pure visual referring prompts generally underperform due to lack of SFT exposure; an expert error taxonomy further characterizes failure modes across models.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: Selective prediction for vision-language models often over-abstains at low risk tolerance, discarding many correct answers and yielding impractically low coverage.
method: ReCoVERR is an inference-time framework that verifies a low-confidence VLM prediction by collecting visual evidence that is both reliable and relevant, without additional training. It converts the initial Q–A into a hypothesis and (i) initializes textual evidences from vision tools (e.g., object detections, region captions, captioning), then (ii) iteratively uses an LLM to generate sub-questions that the VLM answers. Evidence is retained as reliable only if the VLM’s calibrated confidence meets a risk-aware threshold (π_VLM ≥ 1−r), and deemed relevant via a defeasible NLI criterion measuring the change in hypothesis entailment between the evidence and its negation; aggregated evidences must meet an NLI entailment threshold to accept the original answer. Across BLIP2, InstructBLIP, and LLaVA-1.5 on A-OKVQA and VQAv2, this increases coverage and recall at fixed risk tolerance (e.g., up to ~20% coverage gains and substantial recall gains), maintaining the risk budget at 20% and slightly exceeding it at 10% (Tables 1–2; §3–§5).
paper_title: Selective 'Selective Prediction': Reducing Unnecessary Abstention in Vision-Language Reasoning

problem: Generative VLMs lack well-defined, calibrated confidence estimates needed for reliable selective prediction and evidence reliability checks.
method: The paper introduces a Self-Prompting confidence estimator: after generating an answer, the VLM is re-prompted to answer a yes/no verification, and the next-token probabilities for ‘yes’/‘no’ form the confidence score; this can be further calibrated via Platt scaling trained on held-out data. They compare against token-probability product/mean baselines and select the most calibrated method per model (Self-Prompting for BLIP models; mean token probability for LLaVA-1.5), as validated by lower calibration error on A-OKVQA (Figure 3; §4.2.1; Appendix A). This calibrated confidence is then used both for vanilla selective prediction thresholds and ReCoVERR’s reliability gating (π_VLM ≥ 1−r).
paper_title: Selective 'Selective Prediction': Reducing Unnecessary Abstention in Vision-Language Reasoning

problem: Vision-Language Models often hallucinate objects because current multi-scale designs indiscriminately fuse patches across scales, diluting object-centric evidence, and training new multi-scale encoders is costly.
method: SECOND introduces a training-free, selective multi-scale integration. It appends down-sampled vision encoders by bilinearly interpolating positional embeddings and resizing inputs, forming 3–4 coarse-to-fine stages. At each stage, it computes a visual attention map by combining vision self-attention with language cross-attention, then uses an entropy-driven dynamic ratio (p_select) to pick the top-attended patches for the next higher-resolution stage, accumulating attentions across scales. This attention-guided, coarse-to-fine patch selection preserves object-relevant density while suppressing irrelevant background patches, reducing hallucinations.
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: Universal adversarial perturbations for CLIP/VLMs rarely transfer simultaneously across data, domains, models, and tasks under strict black-box settings.
method: X-Transfer generates universal (and targeted) perturbations in image space using a generic embedding-space objective that averages per-model losses, making it agnostic to encoder architectures, embedding sizes, and training losses. It ensembles multiple surrogate CLIP encoders but dynamically selects only a small subset each step via a multi-armed bandit with UCB, using the per-encoder loss as a reward to emphasize hard-to-fool encoders. A projected gradient update under L∞ (with L2 and patch variants also studied) optimizes a single perturbation. The resulting UAPs/TUAPs achieve state-of-the-art black-box 'super transferability' across zero-shot classification, image-text retrieval, and downstream VLM tasks like captioning and VQA.
paper_title: X-Transfer Attacks: Towards Super Transferable Adversarial Attacks on CLIP

problem: Single-step contrastive decoding in VLMs typically focuses on suppressing language priors and underuses the structured progression of visual evidence across scales, limiting hallucination mitigation.
method: SECOND proposes multi-stage Contrastive Decoding that treats early (sparse, low-res) stages as amateurs and later (dense, high-res) stages as experts, contrasting their logits across consecutive stage pairs with parameters α, β, and γ. By leveraging the staged evolution of visual detail introduced via selective patch integration, this decoding amplifies distinctions between sparse and dense visual information. It consistently improves over patch selection alone and over single-stage CD on object perception benchmarks (e.g., POPE) and shows advantages on broader evaluations (e.g., MMStar).
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: Ensembling many surrogate models to improve transferability is computationally prohibitive because gradients must be computed for all surrogates at every step.
method: X-Transfer introduces efficient surrogate scaling: at each optimization step it ranks a large search space of N encoders with UCB and selects only top-k (k ≪ N), tracking rewards via a moving average of the loss per encoder. This reduces computation to roughly k/N of standard fixed-ensemble scaling while preserving or improving transferability. Empirically, performance scales with the total search space size N, and even k=1 can match the best fixed selection in a 16-model space; measured GPU-days confirm the k/N savings. Ablations show UCB slightly outperforms ε-greedy and far exceeds random selection, indicating the reward design, not the sampler, is the main driver.
paper_title: X-Transfer Attacks: Towards Super Transferable Adversarial Attacks on CLIP

problem: There is no formal bridge between a VLM’s visual focus and its hallucination likelihood, leaving multi-scale design choices under-motivated.
method: SECOND introduces a diagnostic/theoretical framework: define hallucination probability P_Hal from generation likelihood and propose the Attention Dice Coefficient that aligns combined vision self-attention and language cross-attention with object masks. Empirically, lower Dice correlates with higher P_Hal; Theorem 3.2 shows smaller patches (higher resolution) increase the Attention Dice when attention aligns with objects. Theorem 3.3 proves multi-stage patch selection monotonically lower-bounds/increases the Attention Dice across stages, justifying SECOND’s selective multi-scale design.
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: There is no standardized, open benchmark of universal and targeted perturbations to evaluate super transferability of CLIP encoders and VLMs.
method: The authors release X-TransferBench, a comprehensive open-source collection of UAPs and TUAPs (L∞, L2, and adversarial patches) together with standardized evaluation scripts across tasks and datasets. The perturbations are plug-and-play (apply in a few lines of code) and cover diverse settings used in the paper’s experiments. This resource establishes a new benchmark for studying and comparing super-transferable adversarial attacks.
paper_title: X-Transfer Attacks: Towards Super Transferable Adversarial Attacks on CLIP

problem: Multimodal fusion often aligns heterogeneous modality embeddings (e.g., EHR, images, text) via concatenation or simple similarity losses, which fails to model complex cross-modal dependencies and can yield biased joint representations.
method: CM2 introduces a copula-driven alignment that explicitly models the joint distribution of modality embeddings while learning flexible marginals. Each modality’s latent embedding is modeled with a K-component diagonal-covariance Gaussian mixture whose CDF F_m(z_m) is computed, and a parametric copula C (e.g., Gumbel, Frank, Gaussian) with learnable parameter α couples the marginals to form the joint C(F_1(z_1),…,F_M(z_M)). Training maximizes an ELBO that combines the copula log-likelihood (log c(Q_1,…,Q_M) + Σ_m log f_m) with the task loss, and is theoretically grounded by Sklar’s theorem ensuring a unique joint for continuous marginals. This end-to-end alignment improves AUROC/AUPR over strong baselines on matched multimodal MIMIC datasets.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Alignment via Variational Copula Modelling

problem: In real-world settings, one or more modalities are often missing; naive imputations (zeros/means) ignore marginal distributions and joint dependencies, harming downstream performance.
method: CM2 treats the learned marginal GMM of each modality as a generator to impute missing modality embeddings by sampling z_m ~ F_m in the latent space under a MAR assumption. Because the copula parameter α is learned jointly, the marginals encode cross-modal dependence, yielding pseudo-embeddings that better reflect the joint structure. A reparameterized mixture sampler (GPS) supports training-time imputation, and the imputed features are fused via a 2-layer LSTM and MLP classifier. Experiments on partially matched MIMIC datasets show consistent gains over baselines, with ablations confirming the benefits of copula alignment and GPS.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Alignment via Variational Copula Modelling

problem: Classical copula inference (e.g., MCMC) is computationally prohibitive for high-dimensional deep multimodal learning.
method: CM2 employs stochastic variational inference with a factorized variational family over modality marginals (densities q_m and CDFs Q_m), deriving an ELBO that aggregates task-specific loss and copula-based joint likelihood. The copula parameter α and GMM parameters (μ, Σ) are optimized via backpropagation, using a tractable approximation to multivariate Gaussian CDFs and diagonal covariances to enable reparameterization. This scalable optimization supports large MIMIC cohorts and standard backbones (ResNet34, LSTM, TinyBERT), maintaining robustness across different copula families.
paper_title: Cross-Modal Alignment via Variational Copula Modelling

problem: General-purpose VLMs and prior RS-VLMs use monolithic architectures that over-rely on global context and struggle to balance fine-grained local perception with global scene understanding in remote sensing imagery. Existing MoE-based RS models often lack mechanisms that enforce meaningful expert specialization by task granularity.
method: SkyMoE embeds Mixture-of-Experts layers into the LLM, with a router that computes token-to-expert probabilities and routes each token to the top-2 experts, while non-selected experts remain inactive. MoE layers are interleaved with standard MLP layers by replacing every second layer, and experts are initialized by cloning the FFN weights to ease specialization. Training uses a dual-stage strategy: Stage I LoRA-tunes a CLIP-ViT(L-14)-based VLM to establish multimodal competence; Stage II inserts MoE layers and fine-tunes with a differentiable load-balancing auxiliary loss to encourage expert decoupling. This dynamic routing yields task- and granularity-aware specialization across diverse RS interpretation tasks.
paper_title: SkyMoE: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Enhancing Geospatial Interpretation with Mixture of Experts

problem: MoE experts can learn overlapping functions without explicit inductive biases, and RS-VLMs tend to conflate local and global cues, leading to poor granularity sensitivity and over-reliance on background context.
method: A context-disentangled data augmentation strategy creates contrastive, granularity-aware supervision to steer expert specialization. It comprises: (1) Count-Varying Cutout, which masks a random fraction of annotated objects (r ~ U(0.15, 0.30)) to produce multiple variants per image with updated counts, improving density-invariant counting and local sensitivity; and (2) Attribute Editing, which edits referring expressions and applies matched visual transforms—relocating targets within a 3×3 grid with Poisson blending and IoU < 0.1, and swapping color attributes via palette transfer—thereby disentangling spatial and appearance cues. These augmentations promote level-specific representations and better local-global balance.
paper_title: SkyMoE: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Enhancing Geospatial Interpretation with Mixture of Experts

problem: There is no comprehensive, multi-granularity benchmark to systematically evaluate RS-VLMs across diverse tasks and resolution variations.
method: MGRS-Bench is introduced as a multi-granularity, cross-resolution evaluation suite spanning five tasks (IC, VQA, VG, OC, SC). It is built via a four-step pipeline—attribute extraction, prompt engineering, GPT-4 inference for candidate responses, and human verification—to ensure quality and consistency. The final benchmark contains 10,415 RS images and 18,433 annotated instances (with balanced coverage per task), enabling rigorous assessment of generalization and granularity-aware performance.
paper_title: SkyMoE: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Enhancing Geospatial Interpretation with Mixture of Experts

problem: Most VLAs directly map observations and language to actions without explicit intermediate reasoning or temporal planning, which limits performance on multi-step manipulation tasks.
method: CoT-VLA adds a visual chain-of-thought step that first autoregressively predicts a subgoal image n frames ahead, then predicts a short sequence of m actions conditioned on the instruction, current observation, and the generated subgoal. Subgoal generation is trained using both robot demonstrations and action-less captioned videos, while action generation is trained only on demonstrations. The policy runs in closed loop by iterating goal-image prediction and action-chunk execution.
paper_title: CoT-VLA: Visual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action Models

problem: Causal next-token decoding across modalities is ill-suited for multi-dimensional action prediction, making it inefficient and weakening correlations among action dimensions; predicting only single-step actions further hurts control quality.
method: CoT-VLA introduces a hybrid attention mechanism: causal attention for image/text token generation, and full attention for the action token block to jointly predict all action dimensions. Actions are discretized into tokens and decoded with special tokens for parallel decoding, and the model predicts action chunks (short sequences) instead of single actions. Ablations show that action chunking and hybrid attention both improve success rates on LIBERO.
paper_title: CoT-VLA: Visual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action Models

problem: VLAs typically rely on scarce action-annotated robot data and cannot leverage abundant action-less video, limiting visual reasoning and generalization.
method: Built on a 7B unified multimodal generator (VILA-U), CoT-VLA is pretrained on a mixture of Open X-Embodiment robot demonstrations and action-less video datasets (EPIC-KITCHENS, Something-Something V2), freezing the vision tower and optimizing the LLM backbone, projector, and depth transformer. Training samples subgoal horizons from dataset-specific ranges at 256×256 resolution, then fine-tunes on downstream robot demos. This yields state-of-the-art results on LIBERO (81.13% avg) and strong real-world performance, with a 46.7% relative improvement from pretraining on Franka-Tabletop.
paper_title: CoT-VLA: Visual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Vision-Language-Action Models

problem: Off-the-shelf CLIP with hand-crafted prompts underperforms at zero-shot anomaly detection because it is trained on natural image-text data and not tailored to detect generic normal/abnormal patterns in unseen categories. Prior adaptations either avoid training or use only static text prompts, limiting robustness across diverse anomalies.
method: AdaCLIP adapts CLIP with multimodal hybrid prompts: lightweight prompting layers replace early transformer layers in both the image and text encoders, injecting learnable tokens. These prompts are a sum of static prompts learned from auxiliary anomaly datasets and dynamic prompts generated per test image by a Dynamic Prompt Generator that uses a frozen CLIP backbone to extract image features and linear layers to project them into prompt tokens. A learnable projection layer aligns patch-token and text-embedding dimensions and adds adaptation capacity. The model computes patch–text cosine similarities for normal/abnormal captions (e.g., “a photo of a normal/damaged {CLS}”) and is trained on auxiliary annotated AD data with pixel-level dice + focal losses and an image-level focal loss, yielding superior ZSAD performance and strong generalization across diverse categories and domains.
paper_title: AdaCLIP: Adapting CLIP with Hybrid Learnable Prompts for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection

problem: Image-level anomaly scoring that relies on the maximum of pixel-wise anomaly maps is noisy and brittle, missing region-level context that is critical for robust zero-shot anomaly detection.
method: AdaCLIP introduces a Hybrid Semantic Fusion (HSF) module that aggregates region-level context before computing image-level scores. It first clusters multi-hierarchy patch embeddings via KMeans, ranks clusters by their average anomaly-map scores, and aggregates centroids from the most abnormal clusters into a semantic-rich image embedding. This embedding is mixed with the class (CLS) token feature and compared to normal/abnormal text embeddings to produce a robust image-level anomaly score. HSF consistently improves image-level AUROC over max-based scoring across both industrial and medical datasets.
paper_title: AdaCLIP: Adapting CLIP with Hybrid Learnable Prompts for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection

problem: Zero-shot visual classification with CLIP often improves by averaging over LLM-generated, fine-grained class descriptors, but this relies on external LLM queries and it is unclear whether added semantics are the true cause of the gains.
method: Propose Waffle CLIP, which replaces per-class GPT descriptors with class-independent randomized word and character sequences appended to the prompt (e.g., “A photo of a {c}, which … {random sequence}”), with sequence lengths matched to the dataset’s average class-name word/character counts to avoid new hyperparameters. Similarities are averaged over multiple randomized descriptors (typically 30 per class, i.e., 15 word/char pairs) to form a structured prompt ensemble. Across 8–11 benchmarks and multiple backbones, Waffle CLIP matches DCLIP on average and often improves it, without querying LLMs. Ablations show averaging—not fine-grained semantics—is the key driver (e.g., mean > max aggregation and randomized descriptor studies), making Waffle CLIP both a practical zero-shot alternative and a sanity check for future LLM-augmented methods.
paper_title: Waffling around for Performance: Visual Classification with Random Words and Broad Concepts

problem: Fine-grained, sentence-level descriptors can be noisy and do not address context-dependent class-name ambiguities (e.g., generic or polysemous labels), limiting how well true semantics are leveraged in zero-shot VLM classification.
method: Introduce automatic high-level concept guidance: query GPT-3 with a short prompt over a list of class names to extract a shared, coarse concept (e.g., “They are all …”), filter generic outputs, and prepend this concept to the prompt as “A photo of a {concept}: a {c}”. This simple semantic prior consistently boosts performance, especially for ambiguous/generic datasets (e.g., EuroSAT CLIP 40.78% → 48.86%), improves averages even when applicable to only some datasets, and complements both Waffle CLIP and GPT-descriptor variants (e.g., Waffle CLIP + GPT descr. + Concepts further increases the average to 60.21%). A concept-interchange study (“semantic confusion matrix”) shows concepts provide complementary semantic context rather than mere noise.
paper_title: Waffling around for Performance: Visual Classification with Random Words and Broad Concepts

problem: 3D open-vocabulary scene understanding without aligned images suffers from excessive, low-quality instance masks when using class-agnostic proposals, hurting downstream recognition.
method: Introduce a class-agnostic Mask Proposal Module (built from Mask3D with all classification heads and label-dependent components removed) augmented with (i) Mask Scoring that predicts each mask’s IoU via a shallow MLP trained with L2 loss and a γ-weight to down-weight unmatched masks, and (ii) Mask Filtering that keeps only masks above a learned IoU threshold β, enforces stability via dual-threshold consistency with offset α (IoU > 80%), and removes tiny masks below N_min points. These choices sharply reduce noisy proposals and improve mask quality, as verified by AP gains in ablations.
paper_title: OpenIns3D: Snap and Lookup for 3D Open-vocabulary Instance Segmentation

problem: Per-object projections of sparse/broken 3D instances are slow, lack context, and exhibit a domain gap to natural images, making them hard for 2D vision-language models to recognize.
method: Propose Snap: efficient multi-scale synthetic scene-level image rendering that captures context while minimizing jobs. Cameras are placed for global, corner, and wide-angle views with calibrated extrinsics and intrinsics to tightly cover regions of interest while preserving aspect ratio. The rendered images are fed to any 2D open-vocabulary detector to build a Class Lookup Table (CLT) of detected categories and pixel locations. This scene-level rendering drastically reduces the number of images and runtime while improving recognition quality and allows detector swapping (e.g., Grounding DINO→ODISE) without retraining.
paper_title: OpenIns3D: Snap and Lookup for 3D Open-vocabulary Instance Segmentation

problem: Assigning categories to 3D mask proposals precisely from 2D detector outputs is challenging due to occlusion, imperfect masks, and view dependence when no aligned images are available.
method: Introduce Lookup with Mask2Pixel Guided Lookup and Local Enforced Lookup. Mask2Pixel projects each 3D mask into the same 2D camera frames used in Snap (with depth to handle occlusion), then matches masks to CLT detections via IoU, discarding matches below 20% and aggregating across multiple views using normalized average IoU to form final probabilities. Remaining masks are handled by Local Enforced Lookup: select top-K least-occluded views via an Occlusion Report, crop enlarged boxes around mask regions, and re-run the 2D detector; matches are again resolved using Mask2Pixel maps. This multi-view, IoU-guided search yields robust open-vocabulary labels for 3D masks.
paper_title: OpenIns3D: Snap and Lookup for 3D Open-vocabulary Instance Segmentation

problem: General vision-language models struggle as GUI agents because they lack robust OCR and pixel-level grounding on large, text-rich screenshots and have limited knowledge of GUI element functions and control actions.
method: GUICourse provides a two-stage, data-driven training pipeline that transforms general VLMs into position-controlling GUI agents. Stage 1 pre-trains on GUIEnv-global (10M page–annotation pairs) to boost OCR/grounding; Stage 2 SFTs on GUIEnv-local (0.7M text2bbox/bbox2text), GUIAct (67k single-step web; 5,696 multi-step web; smartphone), and GUIChat (44k single-turn; 6k multi-turn) to teach GUI control semantics and dialogue. A unified, position-based action space (11 types) plus model-specific parse/position formats are used. Trained agents (MiniCPM-GUI 3.1B, Qwen-GUI 9.6B, Fuyu-GUI 9.4B) achieve effective performance; e.g., MiniCPM-GUI (3.1B) reaches mean StepSR 57.1, and increasing input resolution to 1344×1344 raises mean StepSR from 49.0 to 56.1.
paper_title: GUICourse: From General Vision Language Models to Versatile GUI Agents

problem: Existing VLMs exhibit weak OCR on diverse-font, large-resolution GUI screenshots and poor grounding at pixel level, which directly limits GUI action localization.
method: GUIEnv is introduced to explicitly train OCR and grounding: (a) GUIEnv-global (10M annotated screenshots) for pre-training on full-page text, grounding, and layout; (b) GUIEnv-local (0.7M) QA pairs for targeted 'text2bbox' and 'bbox2text' SFT. Ablations show scaling GUIEnv data steadily improves OCR/grounding and correlates with GUI navigation gains: Bbox2Text EM rises from 14.92 to 44.12, Text2Bbox IoU@0.5 from 2.15 to 47.96, and Web-Single StepSR from 52.84 to 70.57 as GUIEnv grows to 10M, while action Type EM remains largely unchanged.
paper_title: GUICourse: From General Vision Language Models to Versatile GUI Agents

problem: There is a shortage of large, diverse, real-world web navigation datasets with position-based actions and multistep trajectories needed to teach GUI control knowledge.
method: GUIAct provides broad, real-world GUI navigation data under a unified, position-based 11-action space: 67k single-step web instructions auto-labeled by GPT-4V using dual images (raw + element-indexed) and human-verified (accuracy improved from 55% to 92%); 5,696 crowdsourced multi-step instructions across 121 websites with a browser plugin; and 9,157 smartphone trajectories converted from AITW to the unified format. Training VLMs on GUIAct improves generalization to external tasks: on Mind2Web, StepSR improves over baselines (e.g., Qwen-GUI 24.4 vs Qwen-VL 20.3; MiniCPM-GUI 20.8 vs 8.5), and on AITW overall accuracy increases modestly (e.g., Qwen-GUI 67.3 vs 65.8).
paper_title: GUICourse: From General Vision Language Models to Versatile GUI Agents

problem: Multimodal LLMs (e.g., LLaVA) struggle with knowledge-intensive visual questions that require information not contained in model parameters or the input image.
method: Introduce Wiki-LLaVA, the first MLLM augmented with an external retrieval module. It performs hierarchical retrieval over a multimodal Wikipedia-like memory: (1) entity/document retrieval by matching the query image’s CLIP embedding against document title embeddings via inner-product with approximate k-NN (Faiss HNSW), returning top-k documents; (2) passage retrieval within each document using Contriever to embed question and text chunks, selecting top-n chunks by inner-product similarity. The raw retrieved passages are concatenated into the MLLM prompt alongside image tokens and the question, without changing the base architecture, reusing the same CLIP ViT-L/14@336 encoder for both vision tokens and the image query. This design substantially improves knowledge-based VQA, with further gains when oracle entities are used.
paper_title: Wiki-LLaVA: Hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multimodal LLMs

problem: Fine-tuning an MLLM to use retrieved context can degrade its general multimodal capabilities across standard benchmarks.
method: Adopt a data-mixing fine-tuning strategy using LoRA adapters: train on knowledge-based VQA pairs (Encyclopedic-VQA, InfoSeek) mixed with LLaVA-Instruct samples within each mini-batch to regularize learning. This preserves performance on general MLLM benchmarks (e.g., MME, MMMU, MMB, POPE) compared to fine-tuning solely on knowledge-based data, while enabling the model to better exploit retrieved passages when provided as additional context.
paper_title: Wiki-LLaVA: Hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multimodal LLMs

problem: Vanilla self-attention treats individual query–key correlations independently and is permutation-invariant, limiting the ability to exploit geometric and motion structures in images and videos.
method: Proposes Structural Self-Attention (StructSA), which replaces raw correlation scores with structure-aware scoring and aggregation. First, Structural Query-Key Attention (SQKA) applies convolutions over the query–key correlation map to detect D structural patterns (U_K) with a single softmax over all ND entries for stability. Then, contextual value aggregation combines the D pattern scores with D learned context aggregators (U_V) to form per-location dynamic spatial kernels κ_struct that pool local value contexts, yielding diverse, structure-aware attention. The full version leverages channel-wise correlations via a Hadamard product and channel-wise context aggregation (H_K, H_V) with a memory-efficient computation order to avoid O(N^2C) storage, improving accuracy on both ImageNet-1K and Something-Something (Sec. 3.2; Eq. 2–6; lines 55–74; Appendix A, lines 463–491; Appendix B, Table 6a).
paper_title: Learning Correlation Structures for Vision Transformers

problem: Self-attention variants with convolutional projections (ConvSA) add inductive bias but it is unclear how well they capture correlation structures; they may be limited in expressiveness.
method: Derives ConvSA as a special case of StructSA: ConvSA computes attention by projecting the local correlation map with a single pattern detector u_K and aggregates context with a single aggregator u_V, leading to identical aggregation patterns across locations (only scaled), thus limiting diversity. In contrast, StructSA learns D pattern detectors and D aggregators to produce diverse dynamic kernels. The paper provides visualizations and ablations showing performance gains as the structure dimension D increases and with nontrivial kernel sizes, evidencing better use of correlation structures (Sec. 3.3; limitation paragraph; Fig. 2; Table 1a–b; lines 101–105, 125–160).
paper_title: Learning Correlation Structures for Vision Transformers

problem: Build a practical, competitive vision backbone that exploits learned correlation structures across scales for both images and videos without excessive compute.
method: Constructs Structural Vision Transformer (StructViT) by integrating multi-head StructSA into UniFormer’s hierarchical 4-stage design, replacing vanilla SA in later stages. Inputs are tokenized as 3D tublets for videos and 2D patches for images; efficient variants reduce FLOPs by increasing stride in key/value projections. StructViT yields consistent gains over UniFormer with minimal parameter increases and achieves state-of-the-art results across ImageNet-1K and multiple video benchmarks (Kinetics-400, Something-Something V1/V2, Diving-48, FineGym) (Sec. 4.3; lines 164–179, 180–239, 242–347; line 178).
paper_title: Learning Correlation Structures for Vision Transformers

problem: Dynamic scene graph generation in videos is commonly solved with multi-stage pipelines (object detection, tracking/temporal association, and pair enumeration for relation classification), leading to sub-optimal joint performance, large numbers of negative pair samples, and redundant computation.
method: OED reformulates DSGG as a set prediction task by extending DETR to the spatio-temporal setting and representing each subject–object pair with a learnable pair-wise query. It aggregates spatial context using two cascaded transformer decoders: a Pair-wise Instance Decoder that outputs instance-related features/boxes/classes and a Pair-wise Relation Decoder that uses the instance features as strong priors (queries) to decode relation-specific features. Training uses Hungarian matching over fixed-size predictions for end-to-end optimization, avoiding explicit detection–tracking–pair enumeration pipelines. (Supported by: set prediction and DETR extension; cascaded decoders with instance→relation query; Hungarian matching.)
paper_title: OED: Towards One-stage End-to-End Dynamic Scene Graph Generation

problem: Capturing temporal dependencies for predicates and improving robustness to motion/occlusion without relying on external trackers or handcrafted trajectories remains challenging and often computationally heavy.
method: OED introduces a Progressively Refined Module (PRM) that aggregates temporal context from reference frames via iterative selection and interaction. It scores reference pair-wise features by multiplying subject, object, and predicate confidences, then progressively interacts with Top-K subsets (with K reduced across steps, e.g., 80n→50n→30n) to denoise and retain informative temporal cues. This yields long-range, global temporal interactions not constrained by object trajectories and enables fully end-to-end training without trackers or handcrafted trajectories. (Supported by: scoring s_sub·s_obj·s_rel and ranking; progressively reduced Top-K temporal aggregation; long-range global interaction unconstrained by trajectories.)
paper_title: OED: Towards One-stage End-to-End Dynamic Scene Graph Generation

problem: Video datasets for DSGG suffer from incomplete annotations, so treating all unmatched predictions as negatives for predicate classification produces many false negatives and degrades learning.
method: OED uses a matched predicate classification loss that computes predicate losses only on predictions matched to ground-truth via Hungarian matching, mitigating the impact of missing labels. Concretely, it selects matched indices and applies cross-entropy (attention) and focal losses (spatial/contacting) to those predictions only, which improves performance in ablations. (Supported by: training section computing predicate loss only on matched predictions; ablation showing gains with Matched Predicate Loss.)
paper_title: OED: Towards One-stage End-to-End Dynamic Scene Graph Generation

problem: Online knowledge distillation adds substantial training overhead and code complexity, while standard data augmentations do not generalize uniformly across architectures. Practitioners need a drop-in dataset that boosts accuracy and robustness across models with no extra training cost.
method: Dataset Reinforcement (DR): precompute a strong teacher’s outputs on many augmentations per training image and store only augmentation parameters and sparse teacher logits. Concretely, for ImageNet+, DR stores top-10 probabilities for ~400 augmented views per sample along with augmentation metadata (e.g., RRC and RandAugment/RandomErase), totaling ~61GB extra storage. At training time, a modified dataloader replays these teacher-labeled augmentations, keeping epochs/iterations the same as the original dataset and requiring minimal code change. A balanced reinforcement policy (RRC + RA/RE) yields consistent 1–4% top-1 gains across CNNs and ViTs with near-zero training-time overhead.
paper_title: Reinforce Data, Multiply Impact: Improved Model Accuracy and Robustness with Dataset Reinforcement

problem: Selecting a teacher that transfers well across heterogeneous student architectures is nontrivial; the most accurate single model is not always the best teacher for distillation.
method: Conduct a large-scale KD study over 80+ SOTA models and ensembles across MobileNetV3, ResNet-50, and ViT-Small students. Empirically, ensembles consistently outperform individual models as teachers, with a 4× IG-ResNeXt ensemble providing the most architecture-agnostic gains. While very large ‘super-ensembles’ (up to 128 members) can increase ensemble accuracy, they do not translate to better KD than smaller ensembles; the optimal teacher size is around 4, which is adopted to build ImageNet+.
paper_title: Reinforce Data, Multiply Impact: Improved Model Accuracy and Robustness with Dataset Reinforcement

problem: Reinforcement policies that are too hard benefit transformers but can hurt lightweight CNNs, limiting architecture-independent gains. There is a need to balance reinforcement difficulty and optionally adapt it during training.
method: Systematically compare reinforcement augmentations and identify a balanced policy (RRC + RA/RE) that improves both CNNs and transformers. Further, propose curricula that order a sample’s stored augmentations by teacher confidence/entropy and sample easy-to-hard (or vice versa) during training, mitigating the difficulty trade-off. The study also shows that 25–50 stored augmentations per image often suffice to match longer sampling, enabling roughly a 3× reduction in stored samples for a fixed training budget.
paper_title: Reinforce Data, Multiply Impact: Improved Model Accuracy and Robustness with Dataset Reinforcement

problem: Contextual bandits with unknown, highly non-linear reward functions per arm require algorithms that both identify the best arm across contexts and achieve low regret under mild assumptions.
method: Estimate each arm’s mean reward with k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) regression and act via a simple UCB policy (kNN-UCB) that selects the arm maximizing the k-NN estimate plus a confidence bonus scaling as n^{-1/(2+D)} up to logs. Using uniform sampling, the method achieves uniform over-context ε-optimal arm identification with sample complexity ~ ε^{-(2+D)}, matching nonparametric lower bounds up to logs. For online decision-making, it provides a high-probability (not just in expectation) sublinear regret bound of ~ T^{(1+D)/(2+D)} with a straightforward implementation (initial pulls per arm, per-arm k-NN estimators, and σ(n) bonuses).
paper_title: Nonparametric Stochastic Contextual Bandits

problem: Nonparametric methods suffer from the curse of dimensionality when the ambient context dimension is high, even if data lie on a lower-dimensional structure.
method: Assuming contexts lie on a d-dimensional smooth manifold embedded in R^D, leverage k-NN’s intrinsic-dimension adaptation to replace D by d in the uniform consistency rates. The same uniform sampling and kNN-UCB algorithms then attain ε-optimal identification with ~ ε^{-(2+d)} samples and high-probability regret ~ T^{(1+d)/(2+d)}, independent of the ambient dimension. This requires no preprocessing or manifold learning, relying solely on k-NN’s finite-sample guarantees under the manifold assumption.
paper_title: Nonparametric Stochastic Contextual Bandits

problem: A learned context-to-arm mapping does not reveal the topology of regions where each arm is optimal; practitioners may need the number and shapes of these regions.
method: Construct a graph over sampled contexts where a chosen arm i is estimated to be top (via k-NN estimates), connecting points within a radius R, and return the graph’s connected components as the estimated regions. Under mild conditions (uniqueness of the top arm almost everywhere and component separations), this procedure consistently recovers both the correct number of components and their shapes in Hausdorff distance as samples grow, with R chosen below a separation-dependent threshold.
paper_title: Nonparametric Stochastic Contextual Bandits

problem: Rapidly obtaining accurate pixel-wise masks and bounding boxes for novel items (including transparent/mesh objects) is time-consuming and brittle with traditional background/depth-based methods, especially under changing lighting and backgrounds.
method: Train two class-agnostic tutor CNNs on single-instance images—one for semantic masks and one for bounding boxes (SSD)—with comprehensive augmentation to handle background/appearance variability. Fuse their predictions into robust labels via fixed priority rules between mask and box (pm, pb), using intersection when equal, then assign the human-provided class label. This semi-supervised pipeline generates high-quality annotations in real time (10–15 FPS) and handles challenging cases like transparent and mesh items.
paper_title: Semi Supervised Deep Quick Instance Detection and Segmentation

problem: CNNs trained only on single-instance images generalize poorly to cluttered, occluded warehouse scenes, while collecting and manually labeling multi-object images is infeasible under tight time constraints.
method: An occlusion-aware scene synthesis engine creates realistic multi-class clutter by partitioning a base image into an M×M grid (M∈{3,4,5}), selecting objects per grid cell, and pasting their segmented pixels at grid centers while updating labels in parallel. It enforces a visibility threshold by comparing object pixel counts pre/post composition to drop overly occluded fragments. A multi-threaded implementation achieves ~120 FPS synthesis and, as grid size increases, yields higher mIoU on real cluttered images.
paper_title: Semi Supervised Deep Quick Instance Detection and Segmentation

problem: Quickly learning high-accuracy semantic segmentation for many items within tens of minutes (where training full box-regression detectors is too slow) and extending to instance-level outputs with minimal overhead.
method: Design a customized lightweight head on a ResNet50 backbone that merges multi-scale features from conv2_3, conv3_4, conv4_6, and conv5_3 using Feature Smoothing Blocks (1×1 conv–BN–ReLU), Feature Interpolator Blocks (bilinear upsampling), and a Context Extraction Block for richer, more separable features enabling fast convergence. With the backbone pretrained for class-agnostic segmentation, the child model trained with synthetic clutter outperforms PSPNet under the same 35-minute budget and is more robust to higher learning rates. For instance detection, reuse the tutor SSD: apply the child's semantic masks to form class-masked images and run the tutor to get per-instance boxes, providing instance detection and segmentation with minimal added computation and without training box regression in the child.
paper_title: Semi Supervised Deep Quick Instance Detection and Segmentation

problem: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of pre-trained Vision Transformers must balance preserving the model’s generalizable representations with adapting to downstream tasks, but existing methods lack principled guidance and can either over-perturb the model or under-adapt.
method: Introduce a unified Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) lens to decompose each pre-trained weight matrix and reinterpret mainstream PEFT methods (adapters, prompt tuning, scaling/shifting, LoRA) as operations on singular vectors and values. The analysis shows that direct interactions with singular vectors (e.g., W_down on right singular vectors or prompts on left singular vectors) compromise structure such as orthogonality and perturb the spectrum, while LoRA’s low-rank increment can be too weak when matrix dimensionality is large. This framework clarifies the trade-offs and informs desirable PEFT properties to avoid spectrum distortion and excessive deviation from pre-trained capacity.
paper_title: Low-Rank Rescaled Vision Transformer Fine-Tuning: A Residual Design Approach

problem: Design a PEFT mechanism that flexibly adapts ViT weights to downstream tasks without drifting far from the pre-trained model and with minimal inference overhead.
method: Propose Residual-based Low-Rank Rescaling (RLRR): for each weight matrix W(l), learn two scaling vectors that rescale W row- and column-wise (∆W = s_left ⊙ W ⊙ s_right^T) plus a shift f, and add this as a residual to the frozen W. The residual design retains a constant term that preserves intrinsic pre-trained capacity while the dual-sided rescaling provides flexible, controlled adaptation (more flexible than single-sided SSF). The linear nature enables re-parameterization, absorbing scales/shifts into W at inference with zero extra cost. Experiments on VTAB-1k and FGVC show competitive or superior performance to prior PEFT methods, significantly outperforming LoRA with comparable parameter counts, and the approach generalizes to larger ViTs and Swin.
paper_title: Low-Rank Rescaled Vision Transformer Fine-Tuning: A Residual Design Approach

problem: Knowledge distillation for Earth observation image classification often relies on a single teacher or fixed teacher weights, which can be unstable and suboptimal when teacher reliability varies across inputs. There is a need to adaptively leverage multiple teachers so the student prioritizes reliable guidance per instance.
method: Propose DualKD: a dual-teacher distillation framework with dynamic confidence-based weighting. Teacher confidences are computed as the average of the maximum probabilities from temperature-softened outputs. Weights (α, β) are adapted using a threshold δ and a minimum weight w_min: ignore both teachers if both confidences are very low; downweight the less-confident teacher if one is low; use equal weights when both are confident. The KD loss is a weighted sum of KL divergences (scaled by τ^2) between the student and each teacher, combined with cross-entropy on ground-truth labels. On EuroSAT, DualKD outperforms single-teacher KD for ResNet8 (92.88% accuracy vs. 91.77/91.06) and ResNet16 (96.46% vs. 94.49/93.29).
paper_title: Semantic Knowledge Distillation for Onboard Satellite Earth Observation Image Classification

problem: Onboard satellite processing is resource-constrained (compute, memory, power), yet requires accurate real-time image classification. Powerful ViTs perform well but are too heavy for deployment on such platforms.
method: Distill semantic knowledge from two strong ViT teachers (EfficientViT and MobileViT) into lightweight ResNet students (ResNet8/ResNet16) using DualKD. This yields deployable models with high accuracy while drastically reducing resource demands. ResNet8 exceeds 90% accuracy/precision/recall and, compared to ViT teachers, reduces parameters by ~97.5–97.8%, FLOPs by up to 96.7%, inference time by up to 63.5%, and power consumption by up to 86.2%, making it suitable for onboard EO applications.
paper_title: Semantic Knowledge Distillation for Onboard Satellite Earth Observation Image Classification

problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
method: Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).
paper_title: Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers

problem: Vision transformers fine-tuned on partial token inputs (as stored in token-level memories) exhibit large performance drops because they are pre-trained to expect full-length token sequences.
method: Make transformers robust to partial inputs by always feeding full-length sequences where non-retained tokens are zero-padded to preserve positions, and by applying input token dropout on newly observed full samples during training to match the target retention rate. During replay, use stored token indices from the core tokenset to place tokens and pad the rest with zeros. This alignment of training and replay conditions eliminates the degradation from partial inputs and yields large gains in continual settings (e.g., avoiding ~15% drop at 30% retention and achieving ~40% accuracy improvement versus naive partial-input fine-tuning).
paper_title: Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers

problem: Camera–radar fusion is hampered by the sparse, irregular distribution of radar hits on objects, which varies with category, size, orientation, and viewpoint. Black-box fusion often fails to reliably align radar evidence to monocular detections, especially for range estimation.
method: Introduce Stage-1 Radar Hit Prediction (RIC): an object-aligned BEV probability model that predicts per-cell radar hit density conditioned on monocular outputs (class, size, yaw, range, azimuth). The model is a lightweight MLP with separate encoders per input, trained with cross-entropy to ground-truth distributions built by accumulating multiple radar sweeps and compensating motion by combining Doppler (radial) with object/monocular tangential velocity; a spatial smoothness regularizer stabilizes predictions. This learned distribution better matches real radar than uniform or L-shaped baselines, improving range cues for fusion.
paper_title: RICCARDO: Radar Hit Prediction and Convolution for Camera-Radar 3D Object Detection

problem: Given a predicted radar-hit distribution and sparse measurements, estimating the correct object range around a monocular proposal remains challenging due to noisy matches and limited search efficiency.
method: Perform Stage-2 matching by binning motion-compensated radar sweeps into radial–tangential BEV and convolving (dot-product) the RIC kernel with the measured counts while sliding only along the radial axis near the monocular center. The predicted map is rotated from object-centric to radial–tangential coordinates, and a limited ±3.2 m search yields a 1D score curve over range offsets whose peaks define candidate ranges. This efficiently identifies plausible radar-aligned ranges around monocular proposals.
paper_title: RICCARDO: Radar Hit Prediction and Convolution for Camera-Radar 3D Object Detection

problem: Stage-2 similarity curves can be ambiguous (multiple peaks), and monocular reliability varies with distance, requiring a principled way to select the final range and confidence.
method: Train a Stage-3 MLP candidate selector that fuses detection-level monocular attributes (class, range/size, velocity, score) with the Stage-2 matching scores/curve to rescore range candidates and pick the final offset (cross-entropy over binned offsets). The final range is chosen at the peak score (or kept at the monocular value), and detection confidence is updated by blending Stage-3 and monocular scores. This refinement substantially reduces range error across classes and yields state-of-the-art camera–radar detection on nuScenes.
paper_title: RICCARDO: Radar Hit Prediction and Convolution for Camera-Radar 3D Object Detection

problem: Feature imitation–based knowledge distillation for dense object detectors underperforms because it distills large foreground regions using hand-crafted, GT-dependent masks that ignore where the teacher actually makes its best predictions.
method: Prediction-Guided Distillation (PGD) introduces a Prediction-Guided Weighting (PGW) module that scores teacher predictions per location using a quality metric combining classification probability and IoU (per [8]). It selects the top-K highest-quality locations across all FPN levels as key predictive regions and fits an adaptive 2D Gaussian via MLE over these points to produce a smooth, parameterless foreground weighting mask. Distillation losses are then focused on these regions, emphasizing the teacher’s most accurate predictions rather than all GT-covered pixels. This teacher-driven, adaptive weighting outperforms center/box-based or raw quality-weighting strategies.
paper_title: Prediction-Guided Distillation for Dense Object Detection

problem: Classification and localization have different supervision characteristics in dense detection; coupling their distillation and attentions can dilute the training signal and harm performance.
method: PGD decouples distillation for classification and regression heads, generating separate PGW masks with distinct balance parameters (ξcls, ξreg) and applying feature imitation independently for each head. It augments this with attention distillation, using FGD’s spatial attention and a modified channel attention computed per-location (instead of globally), while omitting FGD’s global distillation/adaptation layers. Ablations show the combined, decoupled design yields the strongest AP gains over using neck-only or single-head distillation.
paper_title: Prediction-Guided Distillation for Dense Object Detection

problem: Object detection NAS methods typically search only one component (backbone or neck) in isolation, leading to incompatibility across detector parts and suboptimal accuracy when combined.
method: Introduce Hit-Detector, an end-to-end differentiable architecture search over the detector trinity—backbone, neck, and head—optimized jointly on detection loss. A single stochastic supernet with continuous relaxation (DARTS-style) parameterizes per-layer choices for each component via architecture parameters {α, β, γ}, which are alternately optimized with network weights; final architectures are decoded by selecting the top-scoring operation per layer. This joint search avoids mismatches between components and yields a COCO minival mAP of 41.4% with ~27M parameters, outperforming separately searched parts and their naive combinations.
paper_title: Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection

problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
method: Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.
paper_title: Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection

problem: NAS for detectors must respect computational budgets; without constraint, searched models may be overly expensive.
method: Incorporate a FLOPs-aware regularization into the search objective, adding a cost term C(·) that decomposes as a linear sum over candidate operations for each component and is balanced by a coefficient λ (e.g., 0.01). Optimize architecture parameters and weights jointly with first-order approximation under this accuracy–efficiency trade-off, yielding compact models (e.g., 27.12M params, 272.3B FLOPs) with strong accuracy (41.4% mAP on COCO minival).
paper_title: Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection

problem: Vision Transformers often exploit spurious correlations tied to sensitive attributes (e.g., gender-related cues when predicting hair color). Automatically locating and suppressing such spurious visual cues at the patch level without manual annotation is challenging.
method: DSA first trains a bias-only ViT with an adversarial objective L_B = L_S(x,s) − L_T(x,y), using gradient reversal, so the feature extractor learns to rely on spurious features predictive of the sensitive label s while failing on the target y. It then crafts attention-aware, patch-level adversarial examples against this bias-only model to localize and perturb top-k spurious patches: per-patch importance t_j^(l) is computed from attention distributions to select influential patches for s; an attention-aware loss L_Attn = Σ_{h,i} a_p^(l,h,i) is maximized to draw attention to the attacked patch; perturbations are optimized by combining sensitive-label CE and L_Attn with PCGrad to avoid gradient conflicts and PGD/Adam updates. The resulting adversarial examples x′ mask spurious patches and augment training data.
paper_title: Fairness-aware Vision Transformer via Debiased Self-Attention

problem: Even after masking spurious patches, a debiased ViT must be guided to attend to real features and preserve accuracy, without requiring sensitive attributes at inference.
method: DSA trains the ViT on both original images x and adversarial images x′ with an attention-weights alignment regularizer L_A = D(A_x, A_x′), where D is a discrepancy over attention distributions (MSE, KL-Div, or Attention Transfer). This ViT-specific regularizer steers attention away from perturbed (spurious) regions toward real features. The overall loss combines CE on x, CE on x′, and L_A with tunable weights. Among discrepancies, Attention Transfer yields the best fairness gains (lower EO/DP/DBA) while maintaining or improving BA/Acc across Waterbirds, CelebA, and bFFHQ.
paper_title: Fairness-aware Vision Transformer via Debiased Self-Attention

problem: Snapshot hyperspectral imaging typically relies on bulky, expensive optics and slow post-processing, making real-time, high-resolution deployment impractical. Prior RGB-to-HSI methods or thin-film filters downsample spectra suboptimally and struggle to recover accurate hyperspectral information.
method: Introduce Hyplex, a CMOS-compatible snapshot hyperspectral camera that eliminates spectrometers by encoding spectra with inverse-designed metasurface projectors and reading them with a conventional monochrome sensor. The encoder implements linear spectral projectors (k=9) that approximate PCA components, realized in hardware via ALFRED inverse design to match target transmission responses, and fabricated as 3×3 subarrays with 2.4 µm subpixels in amorphous silicon. A linear/MLP decoder reconstructs spectra from the barcode; the prototype achieves state-of-the-art RMSE across CAVE, Harvard, KAUST, and FVgNET, and supports real-time operation (sensor-limited ~30 FPS; >20 FPS end-to-end).
paper_title: Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders

problem: Fixed filter banks cannot optimally capture task-specific spectral features for downstream vision tasks like semantic segmentation, especially when RGB cues are ambiguous (e.g., real vs. artificial fruits with similar colors).
method: Develop a differentiable, physics-aware encoder–decoder framework (d-ALFRED) that jointly optimizes metasurface projector responses and geometry with the downstream decoder via backpropagation. The encoder is modeled by coupled-mode theory (with learnable resonance parameters mapped from geometry), enabling gradient flow through the metasurface transfer function; a U-Net decoder is trained end-to-end for pixel-wise classification. The learned spectral encoders yield compact barcodes that preserve discriminative cues, achieving mIoU ~0.81 (simulation) and ~0.74 (fabricated devices), outperforming an RGB-only baseline (~0.68 mIoU) and significantly improving IoU/F1 on challenging real–artificial fruit pairs.
paper_title: Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders

problem: There is a lack of large, labeled hyperspectral datasets for semantic segmentation to train and evaluate spectral encoders and segmentation models.
