method: Release FVgNET, a publicly available dataset of 317 hyperspectral scenes (512×512 pixels, 204 spectral bands, 400–1000 nm), with 80% of images annotated with semantic masks across 12 classes of real and artificial fruits/vegetables. The dataset enables training and benchmarking of spectral reconstruction and segmentation pipelines and is used to validate Hyplex’s performance.
paper_title: Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders

problem: Private prediction mechanisms that calibrate noise using global sensitivity are overly conservative, yielding poor privacy-utility trade-offs. We need dataset- and instance-specific bounds on how much a model’s prediction can change when up to k training points are added or removed.
method: Introduce Abstract Gradient Training (AGT) to compute valid parameter-space bounds T_k for gradient-based training under up to k additions/removals. AGT provably over-approximates the reachable parameters by bounding each SGD step’s descent direction using a truncation-based clipping operator (Clip_γ) and interval bound propagation to obtain per-sample gradient bounds, then aggregates worst-case batch perturbations via SEMax/SEMin to update [θ_L, θ_U] (Theorems 4.5, 4.6). Given T_k, certify prediction stability for a query x by verifying f_θ(x) is invariant for all θ ∈ T_k (Lemma 4.4), implying zero local sensitivity up to k−1 (Lemma 4.2) and yielding tight, instance-specific smooth sensitivity bounds.
paper_title: Certification for Differentially Private Prediction in Gradient-Based Training

problem: Subsample-and-aggregate (e.g., PATE-style) private prediction still relies on loose global sensitivity/noisy argmax, causing excessive noise and degraded utility, especially when few queries are allowed.
method: Leverage per-model stability certificates to derive an ensemble-level stable distance. For T disjointly trained classifiers, if each member’s prediction at x is stable to k(i), then at least K = sum of the n smallest k(i) (for n = ceil(|n1−n0|/2)) dataset changes are needed to flip the ensemble’s vote (Theorem 5.3). Privatize the ensemble response using smooth sensitivity with Cauchy noise scaled by 6·exp(−εK/6)/ε; for a single model with certified stability k′, release f(x) + Cauchy(6·exp(−εk′/6)/ε) (Corollary 5.2). This reduces noise relative to global sensitivity and improves private prediction accuracy across datasets.
paper_title: Certification for Differentially Private Prediction in Gradient-Based Training

problem: Discover fine-grained classes from data that only has coarse labels, without any fine-level supervision, while also inferring which fine classes belong to which coarse classes.
method: FALCON jointly models a fine-grained classifier fθ over K_F classes and a binary mapping M between fine and coarse classes so that coarse predictions are obtained from fine predictions via M. It alternates between: (i) training fθ with coarse-label cross-entropy augmented by two fine-level objectives—neighbor consistency over nearest neighbors retrieved within the same coarse class using EMA predictions, and a confidence loss that sharpens fine targets by masking logits to the current coarse parent via M—plus an entropy regularizer to avoid collapse; and (ii) inferring M by approximating the coarse cross-entropy with a Taylor expansion to build a cost matrix C = Y_oh^T P and adding a balance regularizer, then solving the resulting integer quadratic program under row-sum constraints to assign each fine class to exactly one coarse class. This alternating optimization recovers both fine-grained clusters and their coarse–fine relationships from only coarse supervision.
paper_title: Fine-grained Classes and How to Find Them

problem: Leverage multiple datasets that share the same underlying fine-grained classes but have incompatible coarse taxonomies, to improve fine-grained class discovery.
method: FALCON extends to multi-dataset training by maintaining a shared fine-grained classifier and learning a separate coarse–fine mapping Ml for each dataset. Training uses dataset-specific coarse supervision routed through Ml, while all Ml are inferred by solving independent (and parallelizable) discrete optimization problems using each dataset’s cost matrix. This modular design enables effective reuse of different labeling policies and improves fine-grained discovery performance.
paper_title: Fine-grained Classes and How to Find Them

problem: Transformer-based SISR models (e.g., SwinIR/HAT) exhibit a sharp drop in feature-map intensity toward the end of the network, indicating an information bottleneck that loses spatial detail and limits performance as depth increases.
method: The paper proposes DRCT, which stabilizes information flow by adding dense-residual connections within Residual Dense Groups (RDGs) built from Swin-Dense-Residual-Connected Blocks (SDRCBs). Each SDRCB couples Swin Transformer layers (shifted-window self-attention) with dense concatenation across block outputs and 1×1 transition convolutions to adaptively fuse multi-level features; LeakyReLU and residual scaling (α=0.2) are used for stability. This design preserves spatial information, enlarges the effective receptive field with fewer parameters and a simpler architecture, and achieves state-of-the-art results on standard SR benchmarks while being more efficient than prior Swin-based models.
paper_title: DRCT: Saving Image Super-Resolution away from Information Bottleneck

problem: Training SISR transformers to high PSNR can converge sub-optimally and introduce artifacts, reducing reconstruction quality.
method: They introduce a Same-task Progressive Training Strategy (SPTS): pre-train on ImageNet to initialize the model, fine-tune on SR datasets with L1 loss, and finally switch to L2 loss to suppress singular pixels/artifacts and further boost PSNR. The strategy consistently improves DRCT (noted with ‡ in results) and helps push the performance upper bound without changing the inference architecture.
paper_title: DRCT: Saving Image Super-Resolution away from Information Bottleneck

problem: Specifying manipulation tasks via geometric constraints typically requires manual feature pairing and robust tracking, which is tedious and brittle. We need an interpretable, general representation that can infer per-frame geometric associations from a single human demonstration video and output control signals for a robot.
method: Introduce geometric skill kernels: graph-structured units (e.g., point-to-point, point-to-line, line-to-line, coplanarity) that encode association constraints among geometric primitives. For each image, enumerate all possible feature associations (e.g., SIFT/ORB points, LBD lines) and use a message-passing GNN to compute a select-out function g_k that ranks candidate associations; each selected instance produces a control error vector E_k from geometric parameters. Skills are formed as kernel ensembles (e.g., insertion = point-to-point + line-to-line). The resulting human-readable constraints and control errors plug directly into visual servoing controllers, removing manual feature selection and dependence on explicit trackers.
paper_title: Visual Geometric Skill Inference by Watching Human Demonstration

problem: Multiple associations can define the same skill and some may be occluded or leave the FOV, so the model must robustly and consistently select valid associations from only one demonstration.
method: Optimize each kernel with Incremental Maximum Entropy IRL over the demonstration frames. Define a reward r_t based on the decrease in overall control error between successive frames and assume a variational expert with confidence α (truncated-normal/Boltzmann selection over actions). Maximize demonstration likelihood by estimating the partition function via Monte Carlo and backpropagating through g_k and the GNN to update kernel parameters. Add a residual sum of weights (RSW) regularizer to make the select-out sparse/deterministic over the top-p alternatives while preserving the ability to switch to viable candidates when others are unobservable, yielding robust association inference.
paper_title: Visual Geometric Skill Inference by Watching Human Demonstration

problem: RL-based hard prompt tuning (e.g., RLPrompt) parameterizes Q-values via a frozen LM head multiplied by a small MLP, which effectively solves an extremely overdetermined weighted least-squares system over the vocabulary. This induces large approximation errors on low-probability tokens and a dense softmax policy that over-explores unlikely, unnatural tokens, yielding opaque “secret language” prompts.
method: PIN mitigates the overdetermination and dense-policy issues by (1) filtering out ignorable tokens using the frozen policy LM’s own logits: for each prefix, only the top-k tokens by LM logit (k≈10k) are retained for Q estimation and action selection; and (2) replacing Shannon-entropy SQL with sparse Tsallis (q=2) entropy regularized Q-learning, which yields a sparse policy supported on a top-K_α set and uses a sparsemax-based target. Concretely, a filter operator F_I zeroes Q-values for ignorable tokens in both action selection and target computation, and the target value uses sparsemax over Q/α restricted to the supporting set. This combination reduces harmful constraints, curbs improbable-token exploration, and preserves probability mass on plausible tokens, producing more natural prompts.
paper_title: Hard Prompts Made Interpretable: Sparse Entropy Regularization for Prompt Tuning with RL

problem: Prompt tuning must work in black-box or non-differentiable reward settings and remain sample-efficient and interpretable across tasks (few-shot classification, unsupervised style transfer, textual inversion with CLIP). Existing gradient-based hard/soft prompt methods need backprop or embeddings, while RL baselines with softmax and ad-hoc filtering either over-explore or shrink the search space too aggressively.
method: PIN keeps the efficient LM-head Q-parameterization but couples it with ignorable-token filtering and sparse Tsallis-regularized Q-learning, enabling reward-only optimization over a large yet pruned token space. It uses a frozen policy LM (e.g., OPT-125M/350M) to define the filter, a small MLP adaptor, a target network, and sparsemax targets; rewards come from task models including RoBERTa for few-shot text classification, learned style/CTC metrics for style transfer, and CLIP score for textual inversion. Empirically, PIN matches or improves over RLPrompt in accuracy (e.g., avg 82.8 vs 81.6 on few-shot), achieves higher J/GM on style transfer, learns higher CLIP scores with fewer interactions, and yields prompts rated more relevant and interpretable by GPT-4V than PEZ.
paper_title: Hard Prompts Made Interpretable: Sparse Entropy Regularization for Prompt Tuning with RL

problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
method: Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).
paper_title: Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL

problem: Multi-hop knowledge graph reasoning with RL suffers from sparse rewards and exponentially large action spaces. Prior dual-agent methods (e.g., CURL) can misguide the low-level agent by forcing it to mimic a high-level policy, causing policy shift and low training efficiency, especially for long-distance reasoning on sparse KGs.
method: FULORA introduces a dual-agent hierarchical RL framework where a high-level agent (GIANT) walks on a cluster-level KG and a low-level agent (DWARF) walks on the original entity-level KG, sharing history via an information-sharing vector. It proposes an efficient guidance-exploration mechanism: a constraint reward based on cosine state similarity between GIANT and DWARF that is only applied when GIANT reaches the correct cluster. The trade-off is optimized with REINFORCE and a state-dependent Lagrange multiplier λ(s_e)∈[0,1], learned via a cross-entropy objective to increase guidance when similarity is low and encourage exploration when similarity is high. This reduces harmful coupling, stabilizes learning, and improves long-distance reasoning performance.
paper_title: Walk Wisely on Graph: Knowledge Graph Reasoning with Dual Agents via Efficient Guidance-Exploration

problem: For large-scale Fulfillment Optimization (FO) in supply chains, practitioners need a provable speedup for single-trajectory simulation that is independent of the horizon T and scales on GPUs.
method: Partition time-steps by product so each process handles all arrivals of a subset of products, and analyze Picard iteration under mild structural assumptions on the policy (Inventory Independence, Consistency, Monotonicity). Prove convergence in at most J+1 iterations (refined to QT+1, where QT is the number of nodes that deplete capacity sequentially), independent of T, yielding an effective speedup ≈ M/J when work is balanced (Theorem 3.1 informal; Theorem 3.2 formal; partitioning by products and speedup scaling). The framework extends to inventory control with replenishment (OWMR), where Picard converges in at most 2R iterations with an (s, S) central policy (Theorem B.1).
paper_title: Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL

problem: Practically accelerating policy evaluation and end-to-end RL training for large SCO instances and assessing broader applicability to general RL tasks.
method: Implement Picard iteration on a single A100 GPU in JAX with vectorized map (vmap), chunked horizons, and early-reset of iterations. On FO with J=30, I=1M, T=3M and batch sizes up to M=1e4, achieve 350–450× speedup over sequential simulation (e.g., 441× at M=1e4) and outperform an optimized GPU Time Warp baseline by ~88× (Time Warp ~5× vs sequential). In a policy-gradient pipeline, reduce time-to-convergence from ~10 hours to under 2 minutes for the largest instance. Beyond SCO, in Gym MuJoCo (T=200), Picard converges within ≤15 iterations (≤5 for most tasks) to ≤0.1% relative RMSE, indicating potential 13–40× end-to-end speedups when dynamics evaluation is sufficiently cheap.
paper_title: Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL

problem: The high-level agent on the cluster graph receives extremely sparse binary rewards, leading to near-random early policies and poor guidance quality for the low-level agent.
method: FULORA introduces Dynamic Path Feedback, a reward-shaping objective for GIANT that scores entire rollouts using a similarity-based potential toward the target cluster rather than rewarding only terminal success. The shaped reward R_D augments the default reward with a similarity term scaled by α, and the authors prove that the optimal policy under shaping is consistent with the default-reward MDP. This accelerates convergence, yields more reliable guidance (higher cluster state similarity), and boosts accuracy on long-path queries.
paper_title: Walk Wisely on Graph: Knowledge Graph Reasoning with Dual Agents via Efficient Guidance-Exploration

problem: On the entity-level KG, many neighbors and relations per node make it hard for the low-level agent to focus on query-relevant edges, particularly in dense KGs and long-path settings.
method: DWARF integrates a graph attention mechanism into its LSTM-based policy to compute an attention vector over neighbors conditioned on the query and history (following AttnPath’s formulation), and feeds this into the policy network alongside entity/relation embeddings. This focuses exploration on the most relevant relations and neighbors, improving local decision quality and complementing high-level guidance; ablations show notable gains, especially on dense KGs (e.g., FB15K-237).
paper_title: Walk Wisely on Graph: Knowledge Graph Reasoning with Dual Agents via Efficient Guidance-Exploration

problem: Reinforcement learning from LLM feedback (RLAIF/RLHF) suffers when LLM rankings are noisy or hallucinated: using a learned score directly as reward causes instability, requires task-specific post-processing (e.g., normalization/thresholding, step penalties), and can mislead exploration.
method: Train a state-score (potential) function from LLM pairwise rankings using a Bradley–Terry preference loss, then shape rewards as the difference between consecutive state scores (potential-based reward). This uses output consistency as a proxy for LLM confidence: inconsistent rankings drive the two involved states to equal scores, yielding near-zero (uninformative) rewards rather than misleading signals (proved in Theorem 1; Appendix A). This formulation reduces sensitivity to reward scaling and obviates complex post-processing (Sec. 4.2; line 86), and empirically improves convergence speed and final returns over direct-reward baselines in Grid World and performs on par or better in MuJoCo (Sec. 5.3; Figs. 3–4).
paper_title: Navigating Noisy Feedback: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Error-Prone Language Models

problem: Lower-quality LLMs produce inconsistent rankings, degrading policy learning when relying on single noisy annotations; we need a simple way to make such feedback usable without human intervention.
method: Adopt a multi-query ranking strategy: query each state pair multiple times and compute confidence as the fraction of consistent preferences. Show that a confidence-weighted preference loss is equivalent to the standard loss over multi-query data (Lemma 1; Sec. 4.1; Appendix A), which naturally pushes score differences for inconsistent pairs toward zero. Synthetic studies demonstrate that with enough queries even datasets with ~60% ranking accuracy enable near-optimal learning (Fig. 7), and real experiments show 5-query potential-difference rewards with Mixtral outperform single-query and direct-reward baselines across tasks (Fig. 8; Sec. 5.4.2).
paper_title: Navigating Noisy Feedback: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Error-Prone Language Models

problem: AI feedback (RLAIF) often mislabels hard preference pairs, while full human annotation is costly. We need a way to triage where humans should intervene and where LLM labels can be trusted to reach human-level alignment efficiently.
method: Train a reward model (RM) on the LLM-labeled preference data and compute reward differences Δ between chosen and rejected responses to form a monotonic reward distribution across the dataset. Detect the 'elbow' and 'knee' points via the first derivative to coarsely segment easy/high-accuracy vs. hard/low-accuracy regions. Treat upper-left (pre-elbow) samples as high-confidence AI-correct labels, and identify bottom-right samples as likely AI mislabels; flip labels for the most extreme mislabeled region and start human annotation at the reflection of the elbow (on the x-axis) moving leftward. Validate coarse agreement by sampling a tiny subset (<0.1%) from the easy region to guard against prompt/model misalignment.
paper_title: RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment

problem: Given limited human feedback, how can we iteratively refine alignment without sacrificing data coverage or letting noisy labels dominate RM training?
method: Iteratively construct the next training set from (i) newly human-annotated hard samples and (ii) high-confidence AI-labeled samples left of the elbow, while excluding ambiguous middle regions via a back-off ratio β and amplifying human-corrected samples with an amplification ratio α. Retrain the RM each iteration, regenerate the reward distribution to expose new hard clusters, and repeat. Use random sharding to operate on a smaller subset during human-in-the-loop refinement, then apply the final-iteration RM to label the full corpus.
paper_title: RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment

problem: Transferring curated preference alignment into downstream LLMs typically requires expensive full-human datasets or policy RL.
method: Transfer reward knowledge by either integrating the final RM into PPO or labeling the entire dataset with the final RM and training DPO. This human-AI hybrid curation achieves full-human annotation–level alignment with only 6–7% human labels, and due to sanitized selection (controlled by β), can outperform fully human-annotated datasets on HH-RLHF and TL;DR for downstream tasks.
paper_title: RLTHF: Targeted Human Feedback for LLM Alignment

problem: Obtaining reliable step-level preference data typically requires human labels or stronger model distillation, and token-level decoding cannot ensure shared prefixes needed to preserve policy distribution during training.
method: Propose tree-based self-sampling on the model’s own responses/logits without any external distillation: iteratively Select (sample child steps by normalized average log-prob), Expand (branch C candidates), Collect (K sampled paths with full solutions), and Score (use a PRM to score each step). This produces step-level datasets with common-prefix trajectories and action preference pairs filtered by PRM score gaps (>0.5), enabling stable, policy-consistent stepwise preference optimization.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: There is a lack of theoretical grounding connecting offline step-level preference optimization to online reinforcement learning, limiting confidence in stability and policy improvement.
method: Define a preference decoding model π_pθ over binary branches and show that, under a specific reward r(τ)=∏_t π_ref(a_t|s_t)/π_pθ(a_t|s_t), the gradient of an offline every-step preference loss equals the on-policy policy gradient of π_pθ. This establishes an equivalence between offline step-DPO-style optimization and on-policy policy gradient under reward constraints, justifying SPPD’s self-training procedure.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: Adversarial imitation learning is sample-inefficient and unstable: on-policy sampling is repeatedly required and off-policy extensions often change the objective or require challenging min-max optimization with non-stationary rewards.
method: Reformulate distribution matching via the stationarity equation into a successor-sample form and prove it preserves the same set of basic feasible solutions (deterministic policies) as the original predecessor-sample equation (Theorem 1). This enables a temporal-difference update (Eq. 6) to directly learn a proxy ˜d(s,a) of the expert state-action distribution from off-policy data, and to train a deterministic policy via deterministic policy gradients, avoiding adversarial training and density-ratio min-max optimization.
paper_title: Deterministic and Discriminative Imitation (D2-Imitation): Revisiting Adversarial Imitation for Sample Efficiency

problem: The TD formulation requires rewards r(s,a)=(1−γ)p0(s)µE(a|s), which are unknown in apprenticeship learning where only expert state-action samples are available.
method: Exploit determinism of the expert to reduce rewards to a binary indicator independent of p0: r_d(s,a)=1 if a matches the expert action and 0 otherwise. Train a state-conditioned discriminator on expert pairs versus random action negatives per state to predict whether a matches the expert; use it online to partition transitions into two replay buffers (positive B+ and nil B0). Assign constant rewards (1 to B+, 0 to B0) and uniformly sample from both buffers to update ˜d via the TD rule and the deterministic policy via off-policy RL (e.g., DDPG/TD3), yielding a simple, sample-efficient algorithm without adversarial min-max.
paper_title: Deterministic and Discriminative Imitation (D2-Imitation): Revisiting Adversarial Imitation for Sample Efficiency

problem: Visual RL agents with MLP backbones suffer from gradient conflicts across tasks and stages, reducing sample efficiency and limiting performance in complex robotic manipulation.
method: Replace the MLP policy backbone with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE): a CNN encoder maps images to a latent z, a router h(z) selects top-k experts, and their outputs are combined with softmax weights to produce actions. This sparse, input-dependent routing allocates gradients to specialized experts, reducing cross-task/stage conflicts and improving learning. To prevent expert collapse, an auxiliary load-balancing loss (negative entropy of the batch-averaged expert selection distribution) is added to the actor loss, encouraging uniform expert utilization. Empirically, the MoE backbone shows higher gradient compatibility (cosine similarity) and balanced success across opposing tasks and multi-stage execution.
paper_title: MENTOR: Mixture-of-Experts Network with Task-Oriented Perturbation for Visual Reinforcement Learning

problem: Periodic parameter perturbations can help RL escape local minima, but prior methods sample task-agnostic Gaussian noise, yielding inefficient, unguided exploration; the choice of perturbation candidates remains underexplored.
method: Introduce a task-oriented perturbation mechanism that maintains a buffer S_top of top-performing agents (weights θ with rewards R) and fits a Gaussian Φ_oriented = N(μ_top^θ, σ_top^θ) over their weights. At perturb intervals T_p, sample a candidate ϕ ~ Φ_oriented and blend with current parameters via θ ← αθ + (1−α)ϕ, where α = clip(1−μβ, α_min, α_max) uses the dormant-ratio β to adapt perturbation strength. This yields perturbations carrying task-relevant structure, stabilizes dormant ratios, and accelerates learning; candidates from Φ_oriented improve over training and can even outperform the current agent, unlike random-noise candidates which yield near-zero reward.
paper_title: MENTOR: Mixture-of-Experts Network with Task-Oriented Perturbation for Visual Reinforcement Learning

problem: Standard TD(λ) assigns uniform credit to historical states and propagates TD errors without considering state importance, which leads to biased or unstable value estimates under visitation imbalance or noisy outcomes.
method: Introduce Discerning TD learning (DTD), which embeds a nonnegative emphasis function f(s) directly into a new discerning λ-return G_{λ,f} derived from a forward-view identity, then connects it to an efficient backward view. The resulting update reweights both history and error propagation via f: the eligibility trace accumulates as e ← γλ e + f(S)∇v̂(S,θ) and the parameter update is θ ← θ + α δ e f(S). DTD also minimizes an emphasized objective with Λ = F D F (F = diag(f)) and projection Π_f, effectively modulating the steady-state weighting by f^2 and enabling either predetermined or adaptive emphasis.
paper_title: Discerning Temporal Difference Learning

problem: Emphasis-aware TD methods need stability guarantees, especially when the emphasis is state- or parameter-dependent.
method: Define the discerning λ-return operator T_{λ,f} and prove it is a contraction for a function class Ξ: (i) under a norm bound on F for parameter-independent f; and (ii) under a Lipschitz condition on F(V_θ) for parameter-dependent f. Show Π_f T_{λ,f} remains a contraction, derive the associated deterministic variant with A = E[e(γϕ(S′)−ϕ(S))^⊤ f(S)] and b = E[e R f(S)], and prove DTD(λ) converges to a unique fixed point θ* satisfying Aθ* + b = 0 for any f ∈ Ξ.
paper_title: Discerning Temporal Difference Learning

problem: Practitioners lack a compact, task-aligned way to set emphasis that improves learning under aliasing, limited representation, or noise.
method: Use an adaptive, parameter-dependent emphasis defined as the absolute expected TD error (evaluated under the true dynamics) to align update magnitude with prediction difficulty. In experiments on 5-state random walk variants (tabular/inverted/dependent features) and the 13-state Boyan chain, as well as setups with visitation imbalance and perturbed rewards, this choice accelerates learning and reduces MSPBE versus TD(λ), on-policy ETD(λ), PTD, and TD(λ,w(·)); predetermined f(s) (e.g., inverse visitation or exp(−σ(s))) further rebalances updates and mitigates noise.
paper_title: Discerning Temporal Difference Learning

problem: RL agents typically need per-task training and struggle to execute unseen linear temporal logic (LTL) commands; we want zero-shot execution of novel LTL formulas, including in perceptual environments.
method: Compile each input LTL formula into a structured recurrent network whose topology mirrors the formula’s parse tree: every operator/predicate is a parameter-shared GRU sub-network, connected by linear decoders that pass the previous parent state to children and the next child state to parents. The root state is decoded into an Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) policy/value head, and the whole model is trained end-to-end across thousands of formulas. Rewards are defined by converting each formula to an automaton with Spot under finite-trace semantics and giving +1 in accepting states, −1 on violations, and small shaping rewards on valid transitions with decay. In the Craft domain, a co-trained feature-extraction network processes local map observations and feeds all sub-networks, enabling the same architecture to zero-shot generalize to unseen formulas and maps.
paper_title: Encoding formulas as deep networks: Reinforcement learning for zero-shot execution of LTL formulas

problem: Naively sampled random LTL formulas are often trivial, homogeneous, or (in embodied settings) unsatisfiable, which prevents meaningful training and evaluation of zero-shot generalization.
method: For the Symbol domain, generate a formula, convert it with Spot, and use dynamic programming to count accepted strings up to length n; reject formulas with acceptance ratio > 0.0001% to ensure hardness. Enforce diversity by mutating formulas via random subtree replacement and rejecting pairs where >10% of strings satisfying the first also satisfy the second. For the Craft domain, make tasks feasible by transforming each predicate to 'closer(predicate) U predicate' and providing the Manhattan-distance feature to that predicate; also add a recycling bin so agents can discard items. This pipeline yields hard, diverse, and executable formula sets for training and testing.
paper_title: Encoding formulas as deep networks: Reinforcement learning for zero-shot execution of LTL formulas

problem: Off-the-shelf LLMs often produce generic, non-distinctive outputs for similar documents, which undermines IR tasks like document summarization, document expansion, and data augmentation where distinguishing among near-duplicate or closely related items is crucial.
method: Propose Reinforcement Learning from Contrastive Feedback (RLCF), an unsupervised, group-wise alignment framework. It first builds groups of similar documents using an unsupervised dual-encoder retriever (e.g., Contriever) and generates an LLM response for each document. It then embeds documents and responses with the same encoder to compute a group-wise reciprocal rank (GRR) reward that favors responses most similar to their own document relative to others in the group. The LLM is optimized with PPO using GRR as reward plus a KL penalty to the base model, encouraging distinctive yet fluent outputs. Unlike point-wise RLHF/RLAIF, RLCF’s reusable embeddings make group-wise feedback efficient and scalable, and experiments show consistent gains across summarization, document expansion, and dense-retrieval data augmentation.
paper_title: Unsupervised Large Language Model Alignment for Information Retrieval via Contrastive Feedback

problem: Standard automatic metrics for summarization do not directly measure distinctiveness across groups of similar documents, making it hard to quantify improvements targeted by group-wise alignment.
method: Introduce Rouge-diff, an automatic evaluation metric tailored to assess distinctiveness among summaries for similar documents. Rouge-diff is a variant of ROUGE-N computed on deduplicated token sets, emphasizing unique content and reducing the influence of repeated tokens. It is used alongside GRR to automatically evaluate the distinctiveness improvements delivered by RLCF in summarization.
paper_title: Unsupervised Large Language Model Alignment for Information Retrieval via Contrastive Feedback

problem: In distributional RL, agents must update distributional targets online using finite samples of next states. Bellman closedness alone does not address sampling, and many popular statistical functionals (e.g., quantiles) are not preserved or cannot be estimated without bias, impeding exact and efficient learning.
method: Introduce Bellman unbiasedness: a sketch ψ is unbiased if there exists an estimator ϕψ that produces an unbiased estimate of the Bellman target sketch from k sampled sketches (Definition 3.4). Prove that Bellman unbiasedness holds if and only if the sketch is homogeneous of finite degree (Lemma 3.5), and characterize the only finite statistical functionals that are both Bellman closed and unbiased: exponential-polynomial functionals in general and, in the undiscounted/discounted setting used here, the span of moments (Theorem 3.6). Establish that quantiles are not Bellman closed under any additional sketch (Theorem 3.3; App. C), and clarify the complementary roles: categorical sketches are unbiased but not closed, while max/min are closed but not unbiased (Figure 1; §3.2).
paper_title: Bellman Unbiasedness: Toward Provably Efficient Distributional Reinforcement Learning with General Value Function Approximation

problem: Prior distRL analyses rely on distributional Bellman completeness over infinite-dimensional distribution spaces, which is intractable and typically enforced via discretized-reward MDPs—introducing misspecification that can yield linear regret.
method: Reformulate the structural assumption through Statistical Functional Bellman Completeness (Assumption 3.7): require only that the sketch (e.g., N moments) of the Bellman target lies in a finite function class FN, avoiding full distributional closure under translation/mixture. Show that distBC entails hidden misspecification ζ>0 leading to Ω(ζK) regret (Definition 3.8 and discussion in §3.3), whereas the functional view removes this source of error and enables finite-dimensional, exactly learnable updates.
paper_title: Bellman Unbiasedness: Toward Provably Efficient Distributional Reinforcement Learning with General Value Function Approximation

problem: Design a provably efficient online distributional RL algorithm with general value function approximation that avoids infinite-dimensional updates and achieves tight regret.
method: Propose SF-LSVI: perform moment least-squares regression per step to fit N normalized moments of the target distribution in FN (Eq. 197–200), using unbiased moment sketches computed from samples via the binomial theorem (Eq. 194). Plan optimistically with a bonus given by the width function over the confidence set (Algorithm 1; Remark B.1). Normalizing moments shrinks confidence regions (from O(H^N) to O(√N), §5). Leveraging vector-valued eluder dimension and the martingale property granted by Bellman unbiasedness, prove a regret bound ˜O(dE H^{3/2} √K) under Assumption 3.7 (Theorem 5.5), improving over V-EST-LSR’s ˜O(dE H^2 √K) with weaker assumptions (Table 1) and matching known lower bounds in linear MDPs.
paper_title: Bellman Unbiasedness: Toward Provably Efficient Distributional Reinforcement Learning with General Value Function Approximation

problem: EM-based Reward-Weighted Regression (RWR) is known to provide monotonic policy improvements under some conditions, but it lacked a clear, analyzable policy update in terms of value functions and precise conditions guaranteeing improvement without relying on potentially distorting nonlinear reward transformations.
method: Derive a closed-form RWR policy update (no function approximation) expressed via value functions: define B(π) with π_{n+1}(a|s) = (Q^{π_n}(s,a)/V^{π_n}(s)) · π_n(a|s). Prove this induces monotonic improvement of both V^{π} and Q^{π} at every state-action (Theorem 4.1), with strict improvement whenever Var_{a∼π_n(·|s)}[Q^{π_n}(s,a)]>0. Show that nonlinear reward transformations can change the optimal policy (Appendix A), so restrict to linear shifts yielding strictly positive rewards to ground the update and analysis (Theorem 3.1).
paper_title: Reward-Weighted Regression Converges to a Global Optimum

problem: Whether and under what conditions RWR converges to an optimal policy remained open, especially in general compact (continuous or mixed) state/action spaces without function approximation.
method: Establish global convergence of RWR to an optimal policy in compact spaces with strictly positive bounded rewards and an initial policy continuous and positive over actions. The proof shows V_n increases pointwise to V_L and Q_n→Q_L uniformly (via Q_L=W(V_L) and Dini’s theorem), and policy measures π_n(·|s) converge weakly relative to M(s)=argmax_a Q_L(s,a) using a quotient-topology/Portmanteau argument. It follows that (V_L,Q_L) are fixed points of the Bellman optimality operator and π_L is greedy w.r.t. Q_L, hence optimal (Theorem 5.1).
paper_title: Reward-Weighted Regression Converges to a Global Optimum

problem: RWR lacked established convergence rates, limiting understanding of its efficiency and practical behavior, particularly in finite MDPs.
method: In the finite state/action setting, bound the probability mass that π_n assigns to suboptimal actions by constructing sets A_ε and B_ε and bounding policy ratios (Lemma 5.2), yielding R-linear convergence of values: ||V*−V_n||_∞ = O(α_m^n) with 0≤α_m<1 depending on λ_m = max_{s} max_{a∉M(s)} Q*(s,a)/V*(s) (Corollary 5.1). Provide examples demonstrating the linear rate is tight in finite MDPs and that in continuous action spaces the rate can be sub-linear Θ(1/n) (Appendix D).
paper_title: Reward-Weighted Regression Converges to a Global Optimum

problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
method: Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.
paper_title: GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation

problem: Online updates over only newly arrived sessions cause forgetting, while naive reservoirs draw many uninformative samples and miss rapid shifts (e.g., new users/items), hindering adaptation in streaming session-based recommendation.
method: Propose a Wasserstein reservoir that maintains a compact sketch of history via standard reservoir updates, but selects online training sessions from the union of the current reservoir and incoming sessions with probability proportional to the Wasserstein (Earth Mover’s) distance between the model’s predicted recommendation distribution and the one-hot ground truth, directly including any sessions with unseen users/items. This focuses updates on sessions where the model’s output distribution deviates most from reality, avoiding issues with KL/TV for one-hot targets. The model is then updated on the sampled set and the reservoir is refreshed using reservoir sampling, enabling efficient, informative online adaptation.
paper_title: GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation

problem: Practical, low-order, Euclidean-invariant message-passing models (1-EWL/1-WL analogs) are known to be incomplete on 3D point clouds; it is unclear whether simple tests can still separate almost all point clouds under permutations and rigid motions.
method: Model a point cloud as a complete Euclidean graph via its centralized Gram matrix and analyze the Euclidean 1-WL (1-EWL) test. Define the geometric degree histogram and prove that after two 1-EWL iterations each node’s ordered distance multiset is uniquely identified, enabling recovery of the full distance matrix and thus the configuration up to O(3). Formally, two iterations of 1-EWL are complete on a full-measure subset (R^{3×n}_distinct) and fail only on a measure-zero set, establishing generic completeness.
paper_title: Complete Neural Networks for Complete Euclidean Graphs

problem: No polynomial-complexity invariant method was known to be complete (i.e., distinguish all nonisomorphic point clouds) under permutations and rigid motions, while using only low-order operations.
method: Introduce Euclidean WL tests on centralized Gram matrices and prove single-iteration completeness for low k by injecting geometric invariants into the update. Specifically: (i) Vanilla 3-EWL (initial colors = 3×3 Gram matrices on triplets) is O(3)-complete in R^3 with one iteration; (ii) 2-SEWL augments 2-WL with orientational information via triple products ⟨x_i × x_j, x_k⟩ (determinants), achieving SO(3)-completeness in one iteration; (iii) 2-EWL attains O(3)-completeness by combining 2-SEWL on X and a fixed reflection R0X, without increasing asymptotic complexity relative to vanilla 2-EWL. These tests yield full separation for all 3D point clouds, including chiral cases.
paper_title: Complete Neural Networks for Complete Euclidean Graphs

problem: WL-style completeness results typically rely on discrete hash functions and naive simulations lead to exponential growth of feature dimensions across iterations; a continuous, piecewise-differentiable GNN with equivalent separation power and reasonable complexity is needed.
method: Construct EWL-equivalent GNNs by replacing hash functions with continuous injective multiset embeddings Embed_θ built from 1D set-separating invariants (e.g., sort or power-sum) combined with random projections (polarization). Using semi-algebraic/intrinsic-dimension arguments, show the embedding dimension depends on the intrinsic image dimension (≤3n) rather than ambient dimension, avoiding exponential blow-up and keeping per-iteration features O(n). Implement 2-SEWLnet with embedding dimension 6n+1; prove that for almost all parameters it is separating (Theorem 6) and compute it in O(n^4 log n), achieving the same separation power as the complete 2-SEWL test.
paper_title: Complete Neural Networks for Complete Euclidean Graphs

problem: Single-pass, low-memory streaming maximization of a monotone submodular function under a cardinality constraint typically caps at a 0.5 approximation; can we beat 0.5 under natural random-order streams?
method: Salsa introduces adaptive thresholding in a single pass and runs three complementary procedures in parallel: (i) a dense-case routine that uses a high threshold for most of the stream then lowers it (Algorithm 1), and (ii) two non-dense routines, one with a fixed high threshold and one that drops from a high to a slightly lower threshold after an initial prefix (Algorithms 2–3). The thresholds are chosen based on OPT/k and adjusted using the value accumulated so far, leveraging random arrival to ensure inclusion of valuable items. Salsa guarantees an expected α>0.5 approximation on random-order streams while using O(k log k) memory and O(log k) evaluations per element, and still ensures ~0.5−ε under adversarial order. (Supported by Theorem 1.3 and Section 3; Algorithms 1–3; lines 43–47, 95–146.)
paper_title: Beyond 1 / 2 -Approximation for Submodular Maximization on Massive Data Streams

problem: Can we further improve streaming submodular maximization accuracy by allowing a small number of passes while retaining low memory and per-element evaluation costs?
method: Two-Pass uses pass-specific fixed thresholds tied to OPT/k: T1=2/3·OPT/k in the first pass and T2=4/9·OPT/k in the second, yielding a 5/9-approximation with O(k log k/ε) memory and O(log k/ε) evaluations per element. This generalizes to P-Pass, which applies thresholds T_i=(p/(p+1))^i·OPT/k over p passes, achieving a (1−(p/(p+1))^p−ε)-approximation that quickly approaches 1−1/e as p grows, with O(k log k/ε) memory and O(p log k/ε) evaluations. (Supported by Theorems 1.4–1.5; Algorithm 4; Section D/Algorithm 6; lines 49–56, 151–163, 786–796.)
paper_title: Beyond 1 / 2 -Approximation for Submodular Maximization on Massive Data Streams

problem: Is surpassing 0.5 in single-pass streaming with feasible-set queries possible in arbitrary-order streams, and can existing Sieve-Streaming be improved by random order?
method: The paper proves a memory lower bound: any single-pass algorithm that only queries feasible sets and achieves α>0.5 must use Ω(n/k) memory, showing 0.5 is tight under arbitrary order for low-memory methods (Theorem 1.1). It also constructs a hard family showing Sieve-Streaming’s approximation is at most 0.5+o(1) even with random arrival (Theorem 1.2 and Appendix C), implying new algorithmic ideas (like Salsa’s adaptive thresholds) are required to beat 0.5. (Supported by lines 27–41; Appendix B/C; Theorem C.1.)
paper_title: Beyond 1 / 2 -Approximation for Submodular Maximization on Massive Data Streams

problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
method: Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).
paper_title: Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval

problem: After filtering, many queries lack sufficient hard negatives and the remaining negatives lack diversity, especially for low-resource languages, limiting effective contrastive learning.
method: Fine-tune a multilingual LLM (Llama3.1-70B-instruct) with translated Alpaca summarization instructions (Multilingual Instruction Fine-Tuning) to improve multilingual generation, then apply Positive-Driven Back-Forward Generation: summarize the positive document w.r.t. the query, generate a new query from the summary, and retrieve additional negatives with the retriever ensemble. Add top candidates until each query has a fixed N hard negatives, standardizing quantity and increasing diversity; ablations show both MIFT and PDBG contribute to gains.
paper_title: Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval

problem: In multilingual contrastive training, in-batch negatives from different languages are often too easy, and topic imbalance reduces semantic diversity, weakening supervision.
method: Construct effective mini-batches by enforcing same-language samples (to make in-batch negatives harder) while diversifying topics using fastText-based topic labels derived from DBpedia Ontology and Yahoo Answers. Uniformly sample across language/topic to represent low-resource and underrepresented categories, and integrate language/topic-aware sampling weights into the contrastive loss. Ablations confirm both same-language batching and topic balancing improve retrieval performance.
paper_title: Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval

problem: Generative recommendation relies on discretized semantic IDs, but single-modality quantization and residual quantization’s deeper-layer semantic loss lead to weak hierarchical semantics, codebook under-utilization, and higher ID collisions.
method: MACRec introduces Cross-modal Item Quantization. It first creates dual-modality pseudo-labels by K-means over text and image embeddings to define positives across modalities (paper: Dual-modality Pseudo-label Generation). It then injects cross-modal contrastive learning at each residual VQ layer, using the other modality’s pseudo-labels to refine residuals and mitigate semantic loss and codebook collapse (paper §Cross-modal Quantization with Contrastive Learning; repo cross_index/models/rqvae.py class_con_cross_rq). A bidirectional contrastive reconstruction alignment between the quantized text/image representations further balances codebook utilization (paper §Cross-modal Reconstruction Alignment; repo compute_loss uses recon_contrast_weight). Layer-wise application from later RQ layers preserves modality-specific cues while compensating for deeper-layer loss (paper RQ5). This reduces ID collision rates and yields more even code assignment (paper RQ3–RQ4).
paper_title: Multi-Aspect Cross-modal Quantization for Generative Recommendation

problem: Even with multimodal IDs, GR training often fails to align and share information across modalities, limiting the ability to generate correct next-item IDs across text and image spaces.
method: MACRec adds multi-aspect alignment during generative training. Implicit alignment aligns textual and visual semantic ID embeddings for the same item in the encoder latent space via contrastive loss (paper §Implicit Alignment; repo modeling.py CrossModalContrastive.contrastive_loss with task_flag control). Explicit alignment introduces auxiliary generation tasks: item-level (item→image IDs and image→item IDs) and sequence-level (text-seq→next image ID and image-seq→next text ID), trained alongside standard next-token objectives in a T5-based Seq2Seq framework (paper §Explicit Alignment; repo data.py ItemImageDataset and FusionSeqRecDataset; finetune_contrastive.py). Inference uses constrained beam search over valid ID tokens and ensembles modality scores (paper §Training Objectives and Inference). These alignments improve cross-modal feature sharing and recommendation accuracy.
paper_title: Multi-Aspect Cross-modal Quantization for Generative Recommendation

problem: Cross-domain recommendation on sparse implicit-feedback data without user/item overlap suffers from severe data sparsity and label noise from negative sampling; directly aligning latent embeddings across domains is sub-optimal because different datasets induce different latent spaces.
method: Propose TDAR, a text-enhanced domain adaptation framework that uses domain-invariant textual features from reviews as anchors to align source and target. It concatenates fixed textual features with trainable user/item embeddings and feeds them to domain classifiers; embeddings are adversarially trained to confuse the classifiers so user/item representations align across domains in a shared latent-textual space (with separate user/item adaptation nets). On the target domain, training uses only positive labels plus the domain-adaptation signal, abandoning negative sampling to avoid label noise, while the denser source domain supplies negative supervision. This semi-supervised scheme transfers distributional patterns from a dense source to a sparse target and mitigates mislabeling on the target.
paper_title: Semi-supervised Collaborative Filtering by Text-enhanced Domain Adaptation

problem: Existing review-based features for recommendation are not domain-invariant, limiting their usefulness for cross-domain alignment and performance on sparse data.
method: Introduce a Text Memory Network (TMN) that maps users and items into a shared pretrained word semantic space (word2vec) and learns attention-like weights over review words via user/item/word factors (P, Q, T) with softmax, producing textual features as weighted sums of word embeddings. Inject these textual features into collaborative filtering (TCF) by combining them with latent factors in prediction, yielding robust, domain-invariant anchors that complement sparse interaction signals.
paper_title: Semi-supervised Collaborative Filtering by Text-enhanced Domain Adaptation

problem: Do marginal improvements in a platform’s content filter actually help when users face attention/information costs and adapt their scrutiny accordingly?
method: Model a two-player game between a filter and a consumer. The filter receives a noisy binary signal about legitimacy (true/false positive rates π0, π1) and decides to block/forward; the consumer chooses an information strategy µ that sets the fidelity of their own signal at a rational inattention cost C = λ·I(Ψc; X | af=1), then accepts/ignores. Analyze Perfect Bayesian Equilibria under aligned utilities, characterizing consumer behavior via beliefs q(σf) and thresholds qL, qH. Prove the socially optimal profile is either differentiating (σdif) or forwarding (σfwd), and fully characterize the marginal value of technology (Theorem 4.2): improvements are weakly Pareto-improving with “barriers to entry” (no gains until the filter is sufficiently accurate or σdif dominates).
paper_title: Content Filtering with Inattentive Information Consumers

problem: When platforms do not internalize users’ information costs (e.g., benchmark-driven filters), what inefficiencies arise and how can improving filter quality alleviate them?
method: Adopt semi-aligned utilities where the filter values action payoffs but not information costs (Vf = u(σ)). Show a commitment problem: with qL < qdif < qH < q and low-quality filters (Q(π0,π1) < Λ), the differentiating profile σdif is not an equilibrium yet strictly Pareto-dominates any equilibrium, making all equilibria Pareto-inefficient (Theorem 5.1). Establish a threshold result: once Q(π0,π1) > Λ, σdif becomes a Pareto-efficient equilibrium that Pareto-dominates others, and further quality improvements yield positive VoTC with constant MVoT for the filter and non-constant for the consumer (Theorems 5.2–5.3).
paper_title: Content Filtering with Inattentive Information Consumers

problem: How does a strategic attacker who chooses the malicious content rate change the value of improving filter quality and users’ attention decisions?
method: Extend the game with an attacker who sets ρ0 (hence q) before the consumer acts, and analyze equilibria under aligned utilities. Prove the attacker sets q to qL so the consumer never optimally examines content (Theorem 6.1). Show a negative VoTC phenomenon: sufficiently improving both π0 and π1 can strictly decrease the filter’s and consumer’s equilibrium utilities (Theorem 6.2) because the attacker raises attack propensity and the optimal profile shifts from σfwd to σdif, keeping the fraction of malicious content reaching the consumer constant while adding blocking costs on clean content.
paper_title: Content Filtering with Inattentive Information Consumers

problem: In large-scale recommender ranking, fusing multiple predicted user-feedback signals with fixed or greedily tuned weights fails to personalize by user/context and ignores long-term (session-level) satisfaction.
method: Formulate session-based multi-task fusion as an MDP where the state encodes user profile and recent interactions and the action is the fusion-weight vector applied to MTL outputs. Design a reward aggregating multiple behaviors (e.g., play time, play integrity, interactions) to capture user stickiness and activeness. Train entirely from logs via a BCQ-based actor-critic: a conditional VAE generates in-distribution candidate weight vectors, a perturbation network refines them, and double critics with clipped double Q-learning evaluate them—constraining OOD actions, reducing extrapolation error, and yielding personalized fusion weights at serving time.
paper_title: Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems

problem: Purely offline batch RL for fusion-weight optimization is constrained by behavior-policy coverage, risking local optima and persistent extrapolation issues in the continuous action space.
method: Introduce an online exploration module that gathers trajectories with a Mixed Multi-Exploration policy, combining random exploration and Gaussian action-noise around the current target policy’s actions. This enriches coverage with high-value, near-optimal actions while maintaining diversity, and the collected data are fed back into offline training—improving robustness and outperforming random-only exploration.
paper_title: Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems

problem: Evaluating new long-term optimization policies offline is risky because standard OPE can overestimate returns on out-of-distribution state-action pairs, potentially promoting harmful policies to online A/B tests.
method: Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.
paper_title: Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems

problem: Template-based synthetic query generation for VA speech/query priors covers only a narrow set of generic intents and struggles to produce entity-specific, tail-use-case queries needed for emerging entities.
method: Build an entity-centric prompting pipeline that injects each artist’s Wikipedia lead section (entity description) and name into a fixed intent prompt with examples (e.g., “play, queue, turn on, etc.”) to ask instruction-following LLMs (gpt-3.5-turbo, gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct, gpt-4) to generate K=40 natural VA queries per entity. A knowledge base of 14,161 artists is constructed by linking Wikidata (P2850) and schema.org MusicGroup metadata across Wikipedia and a streaming service; generated queries are normalized to remove VA wakewords. This produces longer, entity-aware queries that reference specific songs/albums and behaviors absent from rigid templates.
paper_title: Synthetic Query Generation using Large Language Models for Virtual Assistants

problem: It is unclear whether LLM-generated queries both resemble real VA usage and are specific enough to retrieve the intended entity, and whether they add value beyond templates.
method: Define two quantitative evaluations: (a) domain match via median negative log-likelihood under a Good-Turing-smoothed 4-gram back-off LM trained on two years of anonymized VA queries; (b) specificity via mean reciprocal rank using BM25-L (k1=1.5, b=0.75, δ=0.5) over an index built from each artist’s Wikipedia page and streaming profile. Experiments across all entities show instruction-following LLM queries are more verbose yet similar to VA usage but skew toward tail behavior (higher NLL than entity-name/templates), and are specific enough to retrieve their target entity (avg RR ≈ 0.70), though worse than entity-name/templates; gpt-4 is the weakest among the LLMs and increasing K lowers RR. Very low overlap with templates (mean Jaccard ≈ 0.0038) evidences complementarity, motivating combining both to cover head and tail use-cases.
paper_title: Synthetic Query Generation using Large Language Models for Virtual Assistants

problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
method: Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).
paper_title: Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting

problem: Modern TTS struggles with out-of-distribution instruction text (e.g., abbreviations, numerals, formulas), causing loss of linguistic information and limiting scalable construction of speech-instruction datasets without human annotation.
method: Apply multi-LLM zero-shot query rewriting to fit TTS training distributions: generate candidate rewrites with Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Phi-3-small-8k-instruct, and Qwen2-7B-Instruct; synthesize speech for each candidate (using ParlerTTS with 192 GPT-4-generated speaker style descriptions or fixed-voice MeloTTS/MMS-TTS); score each audio via the multi-agent SIM metric and select the best pair for training. This boosts average semantic similarity from 93.06% to 97.98% and data usability (Pass≥0.9) from ~72% to ~93%, and narrows quality gaps across TTS backends (Table 2).
paper_title: Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting

problem: Challenging rewrites that require complex, context-dependent knowledge remain difficult; different LLMs exhibit complementary strengths leading to failures on a subset of queries.
method: Perform knowledge fusion for rewriting: collect pairs of original text and the best rewrite that significantly improves SIM (q(A,E|ĉ,c)>α), fine-tune Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct with LoRA on these pairs to distill complementary rewriting knowledge, then re-rewrite failed samples and reselect via SIM. This yields consistent gains over the no-fusion variant across single- and multi-speaker settings (Table 2) and improves handling of context-aware and knowledge-intensive rewrites (case studies).
paper_title: Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting

problem: End-to-end speech-to-text translation (ST) without source transcripts cannot leverage ASR/MT pretraining and has limited parallel data. Directly generating source speech from target text for back-translation is hard due to short-to-long sequence generation and one-to-many speech variability.
method: BT4ST: introduce self-supervised discrete speech units as an intermediate representation and synthesize source speech from target-side monolingual text by cascading (i) a Transformer target-to-unit model that predicts reduced discrete units (collapsing repeats) and (ii) a unit-to-speech HiFiGAN vocoder with a duration predictor and a speaker encoder. Discrete units are obtained from HuBERT features via K-means (e.g., K=100), which shortens sequences and disentangles content from pitch/speaker, easing generation. The synthesized pseudo ST data is then used to pre-train and fine-tune the ST model with real data, yielding on average +2.3 BLEU on MuST-C En→De/Fr/Es.
paper_title: Back Translation for Speech-to-text Translation Without Transcripts

problem: Synthetic speech-text pairs generated via back translation vary in quality; naively training on all can hurt ST performance, especially without transcripts.
method: Train a unit-to-target model to translate generated units back to target text and score each synthetic sample by BLEU(ŷ,y); keep only the top ρ fraction (ρ=75% performs best) and discard the rest. Use a 2-stage schedule: pre-train the ST model on the selected synthetic data, then fine-tune on real ST data to mitigate noise. This filtering and training strategy improves MuST-C En→De from 26.2 to 26.6 BLEU at ρ=75% and underpins the overall +2.3 BLEU gains.
paper_title: Back Translation for Speech-to-text Translation Without Transcripts

problem: Single synthetic dataset may limit diversity in content and speaker characteristics, reducing the benefit of back-translated data.
method: Diverse BT4ST: induce diversity by enabling dropout during beam decoding in the target-to-unit model and by randomly sampling speaker embeddings in the unit-to-speech model to generate multiple distinct pseudo datasets from the same monolingual text. Train separate ST models on each pseudo dataset and ensemble them by averaging token-level log probabilities at decode time. Ensembling five models yields an average +4.0 BLEU improvement across En→De/Fr/Es over the non-BT baseline.
paper_title: Back Translation for Speech-to-text Translation Without Transcripts

problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
method: Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.
paper_title: Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers

problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
method: Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.
paper_title: Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers

problem: End-to-end Large Speech Language Models (LSLMs) underperform ASR+LLM pipelines on semantic understanding, and within the same LSLM there is a pronounced performance gap between text and speech inputs after alignment training.
method: The paper conducts a systematic, multi-backbone study (e.g., Llama and Qwen variants; full-parameter vs. LoRA) to quantify the modality gap, showing an average 25% drop for speech relative to base-model text, with most loss attributable to suboptimal speech–text alignment rather than to fine-tuning erosion. It analyzes sequence-level representations by computing per-layer mean embeddings for speech and text and measuring cosine similarity and Euclidean distance, revealing increasing directional alignment (cosine) but growing magnitude divergence (distance) with depth. It further shows a strong linear correlation between cross-modal representation similarity and the modality performance gap, especially under LoRA (e.g., high R^2 for cosine similarity), linking internal alignment quality to downstream disparities.
paper_title: Understanding the Modality Gap: An Empirical Study on the Speech-Text Alignment Mechanism of Large Speech Language Models

problem: Existing sequence-level measures incompletely capture how well speech frames align with text tokens, limiting diagnostic power for the modality gap.
method: The authors discover a spontaneous, nearly monotonic token-level alignment path between speech frames and text tokens by constructing layer-wise token-wise similarity matrices and tracing, for each text token, the speech frame of maximal similarity (or minimal distance). They propose the Alignment Path Score (APS), which aggregates alignment-path similarity across tokens and layers to quantify fine-grained alignment quality. APS exhibits stronger linear correlation with the modality gap than sequence-level metrics (e.g., higher R^2 under LoRA), and alignment paths show high monotonicity and consistency across models (e.g., Spearman ≈ 0.85 for cosine), indicating token-level alignment is a key mechanism underlying LSLM speech understanding.
paper_title: Understanding the Modality Gap: An Empirical Study on the Speech-Text Alignment Mechanism of Large Speech Language Models

problem: It is unclear whether improving token-level speech–text alignment causally enhances LSLM performance on spoken inputs.
method: They perform targeted inference-time interventions on speech token embeddings identified along the APS alignment path, modifying either only the three least-aligned tokens (Bottom3) or all aligned tokens. Two operations are tested: angle projection (forcing speech token direction to match the paired text token) and length normalization (matching norms). Angle projection improves or maintains accuracy in 6/8 settings and is especially reliable for LoRA models (Bottom3 generally best), whereas length normalization often degrades performance, providing causal evidence that increasing token-level angular alignment can enhance speech-input correctness.
paper_title: Understanding the Modality Gap: An Empirical Study on the Speech-Text Alignment Mechanism of Large Speech Language Models

problem: Long-form spoken question answering requires retrieving question-relevant segments from lengthy audio, but existing end-to-end speech-text retrievers perform poorly and direct speech–text alignment suffers from a large modality gap; pipeline ASR→text-retrieval can be accurate but is slower and error-prone.
method: CLSR is an end-to-end contrastive language–speech retriever that first converts acoustic features into text-like token representations, then aligns them in text space. It uses a non-autoregressive Paraformer with CIF to map frames to token steps and produces token distributions, followed by a vector-quantizer adaptor that yields one-hot-like tokens. These are projected into a frozen text encoder’s (BGE) embedding space, and sentence-level [CLS] embeddings are aligned with queries via symmetric InfoNCE. Joint training combines ASR losses (CE, MAE for CIF, MWER) with contrastive loss, yielding strong retrieval on Spoken-SQuAD*, LibriSQA, SLUE-SQA-5, and DRCD, outperforming E2E baselines (CLAP, SpeechDPR) and matching or surpassing Whisper+BGE on several datasets.
paper_title: End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering

problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
method: CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).
paper_title: End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering

problem: Feeding entire long audio to large audio language models (LALMs) is slow and harms answer quality due to irrelevant content.
method: CLSR is used as a speech retriever in a speech-RAG pipeline: long audio is segmented (e.g., 40 s windows), CLSR ranks segments by similarity to the question, and only the top segment is sent to a LALM (Qwen-Audio). On modified SLUE-SQA-5 with ~30-minute documents, this improves EM from 18.00 to 27.60 and F1 from 23.55 to 35.10, while reducing inference time by ~10×, demonstrating practical gains in both accuracy and latency for long-form SQA.
paper_title: End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering

problem: There is no public benchmark for Chinese ASR error correction, making it hard to study and compare methods across diverse error types and utterance lengths.
method: Construct ASR-EC by decoding four Chinese speech corpora (THCHS-30, AISHELL-1/2, WeNetSpeech) with two contrasting, industry-grade ASR pipelines—Kaldi-K1 (DNN-HMM with n-gram FST LM) and Kaldi-K2 (Zipformer-Transducer E2E), both pre-trained on 10k-hour Chinese speech—to generate erroneous transcripts. Split data into short (~13 chars) and long (~38 chars) subsets, cap zero-CER cases (keep 10%), and report substitution, deletion, and insertion error compositions, yielding two datasets (A*, B*) spanning varied difficulties.
paper_title: ASR-EC BENCHMARK: EVALUATING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS ON CHINESE ASR ERROR CORRECTION

problem: How to effectively leverage LLMs to correct Chinese ASR errors given that naïve prompting tends to over-correct and degrade CER.
method: Systematically evaluate three LLM adaptation paradigms for ASR EC: (1) prompting (zero-/few-shot) and a two-step multi-step prompting that first detects errors then corrects; (2) parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA to adapt LLMs to the task; and (3) multimodal augmentation that feeds a multimodal LLM with concatenated encoded raw audio plus instruction and erroneous transcript, trained end-to-end. Experiments on ASR-EC with ChatGLM3, Qwen, Baichuan2, and Qwen-Audio show prompting is ineffective due to over-correction, LoRA fine-tuning substantially improves CER and mitigates over-correction, and multimodal augmentation achieves the best (state-of-the-art) CER across short/long/mixed subsets.
paper_title: ASR-EC BENCHMARK: EVALUATING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS ON CHINESE ASR ERROR CORRECTION

problem: Multimodal LLMs (audio+text) are not tailored for ASR error correction out-of-the-box; it is unclear if targeted supervision can further reduce CER beyond standard ASR or vanilla multimodal ASR modes.
method: Fine-tune the audio-text model Qwen-Audio on ASR-EC by training it to output corrected text from concatenated audio encodings and erroneous transcripts. Compared to ASR baselines and Qwen-Audio’s vanilla ASR mode, the finetuned model yields large CER reductions across short/long/mixed sets (Table 5), demonstrating that ASR-EC supervision significantly boosts multimodal LLMs; remaining irreducible errors (e.g., names/pronouns without context) indicate a lower-bound CER absent external knowledge.
paper_title: ASR-EC BENCHMARK: EVALUATING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS ON CHINESE ASR ERROR CORRECTION

problem: Speech summarization is loosely defined and fragmented across ASR, text summarization, and application-specific communities, with unclear task boundaries (e.g., vs. video summarization) and heterogeneous modeling paradigms.
method: The survey formalizes speech-to-text summarization, specifying inputs (audio vs. transcripts), outputs (extractive vs. abstractive), and granularities, and maps adjacent tasks like smart chaptering and subtitle compression. It introduces the Speech–Video Modality Importance Spectrum to delineate where visual cues are supplementary vs. essential. It organizes approaches into cascaded (fine-tuned encoder–decoder models and prompted/adapted LLMs) and end-to-end systems, and provides a consolidated taxonomy of audio-encoder → projector → LLM architectures and training regimes (e.g., Conformer/HuBERT/Whisper with Q-Former/MLP/linear; frozen vs. LoRA vs. full finetuning) to unify disparate designs.
paper_title: Summarizing Speech: A Comprehensive Survey

problem: Progress is impeded by scarce, inconsistent datasets (often surrogate summaries), limited multilingual coverage, unavailable benchmarks, and evaluation practices that over-rely on ROUGE and transcript/reference-only judging, overlooking speech-specific phenomena.
method: The paper curates a comprehensive dataset catalog with availability and licensing caveats and an interactive index, audits limitations of surrogate summaries with empirical evidence, and recommends viable resources (e.g., SLUE-TED, NUTSHELL, VISTA, YTSeg, MeetingBank). It surveys data scaling strategies (LLM-generated references, synthetic TTS speech, ASR-error synthesis, cross-domain pretraining) and systematizes evaluation methods (human, lexical, embedding, trained evaluators, LLM-as-a-judge). It highlights concrete LLM-judge issues—biases, difficulty with factuality, position/length sensitivity, and the absence of audio- or even transcript-conditioned judges—and compiles practitioner-ready overviews (Tables B1–B2) to guide more faithful, speech-aware assessment.
paper_title: Summarizing Speech: A Comprehensive Survey

problem: There is limited comparative understanding of cascaded versus end-to-end speech summarization and which design choices (e.g., context length handling) most affect performance.
method: The survey aggregates reported results on the How2 benchmark across models (Table 3) to synthesize trends. It finds end-to-end systems generally outperform cascaded baselines, with performance most strongly shaped by processed context length, parameter count, and use of full inputs or synthetic enrichment. Models that process longer or full audio inputs surpass truncated-input systems, underscoring the centrality of long-context handling and efficient architectures for speech summarization.
paper_title: Summarizing Speech: A Comprehensive Survey

problem: End-to-end ASR/SLU encoders must capture both local and global dependencies, but popular designs like Conformer combine convolution and self-attention sequentially, making utilization of local/global cues hard to interpret or customize and leaving quadratic attention cost.
method: Branchformer introduces a two-branch encoder where one branch uses multi-headed self-attention with relative positional encoding for global context, and the other uses an MLP with convolutional spatial gating (cgMLP) employing depth-wise convolutions for local context. The branches are merged via either concatenation (default, best accuracy) or a learned weighted average whose attention-pooled weights expose layer-wise importance of local vs. global processing. This parallel, disentangled design yields better or comparable results to Conformer across ASR/SLU benchmarks and improves training stability on short-utterance data.
paper_title: Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding

problem: Self-attention’s quadratic complexity in sequence length limits efficiency for long speech inputs.
