method: Within Branchformer’s global-context branch, the standard self-attention can be swapped for Fastformer, an additive attention variant that uses attention-based pooling to summarize global context with linear complexity. Experiments show near-parity to self-attention Branchformer (e.g., minor CER/WER degradation) while maintaining linear scaling in encoder forward time, and performance remains comparable to Conformer and substantially better than cgMLP/Transformer baselines.
paper_title: Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding

problem: Deployed ASR/SLU systems often need variable inference complexity/speed without maintaining multiple models or retraining.
method: Branchformer applies branch dropout to the attention branch during training so the same trained model can run in two inference modes: (1) both branches for higher accuracy, or (2) a pruned cgMLP-only path with linear complexity. This requires no fine-tuning, reduces encoder forward time to linear scaling, and the pruned model achieves performance comparable to a standalone cgMLP trained from scratch.
paper_title: Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding

problem: Tight-tolerance robotic assembly requires precise, contact-aware control, but trajectory-centric RL with direct torque actions struggles to leverage contact constraints and remain stable in free space.
method: Make the policy’s action an operational-space wrench F_tip and execute it with a hybrid motion/force controller: project desired wrenches to joint torques via J^T pseudoinverse, and wrap with a low-gain position loop with motion/force weightings (Σ1, Σ2) for stability. Train a time-varying linear-Gaussian controller with iLQG (with entropy regularization) to output F_tip conditioned on state. This implicitly yields variable impedance—high impedance in free space and high admittance in contact—while exploiting natural task constraints. On four Siemens assembly tasks it substantially outperforms kinematics and torque-control iLQG baselines (e.g., 5/5 vs 0–1/5 successes on Tasks 1–2).
paper_title: Reinforcement Learning on Variable Impedance Controller for High-Precision Robotic Assembly

problem: Explicitly modeling Pfaffian contact constraints for manipulation is labor-intensive and brittle; controllers need to discover appropriate constraints and compliance from interaction.
method: Interpret the hybrid operational-space motion/force control with iLQG-learned F_tip as learning Pfaffian constraints A(q), where requested wrenches must lie in Col(A^T(q)), effectively constraining motion/forces at contact without hand-coding. The learned piecewise linear-Gaussian controller adapts compliance across phases, approximating holonomic and nonholonomic task constraints. Empirically, the resulting behaviors align with human heuristics (probing, alignment, force ramping) observed in Tasks 1–3.
paper_title: Reinforcement Learning on Variable Impedance Controller for High-Precision Robotic Assembly

problem: Global neural policies for force-based assembly often fail to generalize to small environment changes, and naïvely feeding raw F/T into early layers induces unstable free-space motions due to noise and spurious correlations.
method: Propose a neural network that low-pass filters wrist force/torque readings and injects them via late fusion at the penultimate layer, keeping early layers primarily state-driven in free space. Train the network with MDGPS to minimize KL divergence to the local iLQG controller (with additional KL constraints), enabling supervised imitation. On base shifts of 1–5 cm, the policy maintains high success (e.g., 9/10 at 1 cm, 8/10 at 2 cm) and dramatically outperforms early-fusion F/T networks (0/10), while slightly improving over iLQG in some settings.
paper_title: Reinforcement Learning on Variable Impedance Controller for High-Precision Robotic Assembly

problem: Robots must plan with newly acquired, diverse parameterized skills (mixed discrete/continuous parameters, differing implementations, not necessarily subgoal skills) and solve new tasks over time without retraining task-specific policies or relying on plan skeletons.
method: Perform search-based task planning directly in the space of (skill, parameter) tuples using learned Skill Effect Models (SEMs). Each skill has its own SEM that, given the current state and that skill’s parameters, predicts the terminal state and the total execution cost. SEMs are implemented as fully connected GNNs over object-centric state features, producing per-object state deltas and a graph-level cost prediction, enabling support for varying numbers of objects and mixed parameter types. Planning uses Weighted A* with an admissible heuristic and per-skill parameter sampling to construct successors, requiring only a goal condition function and avoiding subgoal or shared-embedding assumptions.
paper_title: Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation

problem: Learning accurate, planning-relevant models of skill effects is hard because the distribution of states visited during planning/execution is unknown a priori, and the robot must continually incorporate new skills in a lifelong setting.
method: Interleave SEM training with planner-driven data collection. Initialize each new skill’s SEM with single-skill executions sampled from the known initial state distribution, then run the planner with current SEMs on training tasks to generate candidate paths. Sample paths from the planning graph—biased toward longer paths and those that include newly added skills—simulate them to collect (state, parameter, terminal state, cost) transitions, filter duplicates, and fine-tune SEMs for both new and existing skills as needed. This yields data concentrated on planning-relevant regions and supports incremental addition of skills; empirically, training on planner-generated data achieves higher success with fewer samples than training on random executions.
paper_title: Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation

problem: Online simulation of long-horizon skills during planning is prohibitively slow, and it is unclear whether random parameter sampling in continuous state/parameter spaces yields a search graph suitable for guarantees.
method: Replace online simulation with one-shot SEM predictions at the skill level to dramatically reduce planning time while maintaining accuracy suitable for search. Use Weighted A* for completeness and bounded suboptimality on the constructed graph given an admissible heuristic. Theoretical analysis shows that, under Lipschitz continuity of skill transitions/cost and sufficiently small dispersion of sampled parameters, the constructed graph yields paths whose terminal states and costs are within bounded error of true solutions, supporting efficient search in continuous spaces.
paper_title: Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation

problem: How to teach a robot novel manipulation tasks from only a single human demonstration, without object-specific models, extensive demonstrations, or exploration during contact-rich interaction, while still executing complex fine manipulation trajectories.
method: Model imitation as state estimation with a coarse-to-fine decomposition. Define a task-specific 'bottleneck' pose (the end-effector pose where interaction starts) from the single demonstration, then train a vision model f(I) to predict the relative bottleneck pose T_EB via self-supervised data collection by automatically moving an end-effector-mounted camera around the object (no camera calibration required). Simplify prediction to 3-DOF by constraining vertical orientation and known height, recording a residual rotation R for reorientation. At test time, follow a linear IK approach toward the sequentially estimated bottleneck pose, apply a last-inch correction with a second network g(I) trained on near-bottleneck images, rotate by R, and replay the demonstration’s local end-effector velocities for the fine interaction. This yields single-demo learning across diverse tasks with an analytical, interpretable controller.
paper_title: Coarse-to-Fine Imitation Learning: Robot Manipulation from a Single Demonstration

problem: Single-image visual servoing suffers from noisy pose predictions during approach, leading to inaccuracies that degrade open-loop interaction when only one demonstration is available.
method: Fuse multiple per-frame bottleneck pose predictions from f(I) using explicit sequential state estimation: (1) Batch inverse-variance averaging and (2) Bayes filtering, each assuming Gaussian uncertainties. Compare three uncertainty sources—MC Dropout, a learned uncertainty predictor from validation errors, and a constant prior from f(I)’s validation error—and find that filtering with a constant prior typically outperforms visual servoing and other variants for approach accuracy, and improves overall imitation success when combined with the last-inch correction.
paper_title: Coarse-to-Fine Imitation Learning: Robot Manipulation from a Single Demonstration

problem: In goal-image-conditioned robotic rearrangement, matching objects between the current scene and the goal image degrades under visual shifts (pose, background) and typically assumes the exact same object instances appear in both images, which is impractical for real deployments.
method: Introduce a visual-semantic matching approach that leverages CLIP to incorporate object semantics into matching. After instance segmentation, crops from source and target images are encoded with CLIP’s image encoder, and a set of K text prompts (semantic labels) are encoded with CLIP’s text encoder. Classification matrices C_s and C_t are formed via cosine similarities between visual and semantic embeddings, and the final source–target similarity is computed as S = C_t C_s^T, followed by assignment via argmax or the Hungarian algorithm. Two practical variants are used: CLIP-SemFeat-K (all K labels) and CLIP-SemFeat-N (only labels known to be present). Lightweight prompt engineering is applied to improve zero-shot alignment. This semantic projection enables robust cross-instance matching and outperforms purely visual baselines in LVIS 8-way/20-way settings (e.g., 58.8%/40.1% vs 50.6%/35.5% for ResNet50-G).
paper_title: Semantically Grounded Object Matching for Robust Robotic Scene Rearrangement

problem: There is a lack of controlled evaluation isolating how object-matching methods fail as visual shift increases, and no standard setup to study cross-instance matching for rearrangement.
method: Provide a controlled analysis across curated same-instance splits of the APC dataset (Easy/Medium/Hard/Hardest) to systematically vary viewpoint, pose, and background, and construct synthetic N-way cross-instance matching tasks using LVIS. Results show that purely visual features (e.g., ResNet50, AlexNet, color histograms) degrade sharply with increasing shift and across instances, while CLIP-based visual-semantic matching degrades less in APC and clearly dominates in LVIS cross-instance tasks. This establishes the fundamental limitation of instance-only matching and validates semantics as a robust disambiguating signal.
paper_title: Semantically Grounded Object Matching for Robust Robotic Scene Rearrangement

problem: Robots need to execute multi-object rearrangement from goal images that may not share any object instances with the current scene.
method: Integrate the proposed CLIP-based semantic matcher into a full manipulation pipeline: instance segmentation for object crops, GG-CNN for grasping, and a mask-based collision planner for pick-and-place. Demonstrate multi-step tabletop rearrangement on a Franka Panda using goal images with entirely different object instances, achieving higher cross-instance matching accuracy than visual baselines (e.g., 74.2% with CLIP-SemFeat-N vs 55.6% ResNet50-G) and successful end-to-end rearrangements (e.g., highlighter, stapler, lego blocks, citrus fruit) without object-instance overlap.
paper_title: Semantically Grounded Object Matching for Robust Robotic Scene Rearrangement

problem: Robotic rearrangement in clutter is hard due to high-dimensional kinodynamics, physical/perception uncertainties, and the impracticality of specifying explicit goal configurations. Open-loop kinodynamic planners often fail on real executions and are inefficient when extensively exploring the full valid state space.
method: Introduce dhRRT, a kinodynamic RRT framework with dynamic planning horizons driven by a task heuristic h(·). The planner monitors progress and, when a heuristic decrease exceeds a threshold p or a maximum tree depth Dmax is reached, executes the best control segment and re-observes the true state, interleaving planning and execution to close the loop. This progress-based execution allows implicit goal specifications via simple heuristics instead of explicit goal states and focuses exploration on task-relevant subspaces. Across grasping, relocating, and sorting, dhRRT improves robustness and efficiency over kdRRT and a replanning baseline (r-kdRRT), handling inaccurate models and nondeterministic physics.
paper_title: Rearrangement-Based Manipulation via Kinodynamic Planning and Dynamic Planning Horizons

problem: For planar nonprehensile rearrangement, end-effector motions must be constrained to the workspace while being executed by a high-DOF manipulator without hitting singularities or invalid configurations. Efficiently mapping sampled end-effector actions to executable joint controls is nontrivial.
method: Sample controls in the end-effector velocity space se(2) and perform a continuous Jacobian-based projection (J†v) along the transition to generate feasible joint-space controls. Discretize the control duration and at each intermediate state verify validity and manipulability (via √det(JJ^T)) against a threshold, rejecting edges when projections are infeasible. This ensures only executable edges are added to the kinodynamic tree, adheres to the task manifold without specialized constraint planners, and avoids singular regions during rearrangement planning.
paper_title: Rearrangement-Based Manipulation via Kinodynamic Planning and Dynamic Planning Horizons

problem: End-to-end imitation learning for mobile manipulation suffers from a large sim-to-real gap, exacerbated by multi-sensor inputs (RGB, depth) whose domain shifts differ by modality.
method: Augment behavior cloning with a Variational Information Bottleneck to learn domain-agnostic, compressed visual embeddings shared across sim and real. Each modality uses a stochastic encoder (ResNet-18) producing a 64-D Gaussian, an action decoder MLP, and a learned mixture-of-Gaussians prior r(z) with 512 components; training minimizes a Huber BC loss plus β·KL(p(z|x)||r(z)), with β linearly annealed and 8 Monte Carlo samples per input. Training on mixed sim+real data encourages shared bits across domains, reducing divergence between sim/real embeddings and closing the sim-to-real gap, yielding 96% real-world success (+16% over the best single-modality baseline).
paper_title: Bayesian Imitation Learning for End-to-End Mobile Manipulation

problem: Naive multi-sensor fusion can degrade performance because the robot cannot gauge when a modality (e.g., RGB vs. depth) is unreliable or out-of-distribution.
method: Use the per-instance VIB rate (the KL divergence D_KL[p(z|x) || r(z)]) as a calibrated, modality-specific uncertainty signal and fuse actions accordingly. Train independent VIB-regularized models per modality and, at inference, compute weights from rates via either softmax (discrete switching) or linear normalization to form the fused action a = Σ_i w_i a_i, down-weighting high-uncertainty sensors. This uncertainty-aware fusion outperforms concatenation-based fusion in the real world (e.g., 96% vs. 75%, a +21% absolute gain), and ablations show the fused policy preferentially follows the better-performing modality (depth).
paper_title: Bayesian Imitation Learning for End-to-End Mobile Manipulation

problem: Policies learned end-to-end are often black boxes, making it hard to understand which inputs matter or when the model is uncertain.
method: Leverage VIB’s learned rates and latent structure for explainability: higher rates indicate inputs that require more information to act accurately. In door opening, rates peak during the task-critical latch manipulation phase for both modalities, while being low during base-only navigation, and nearest-neighbor analysis in latent space retrieves sim and real images from the same task phase, evidencing domain-agnostic, action-relevant representations. These properties provide interpretable diagnostics for model behavior and sensor reliability.
paper_title: Bayesian Imitation Learning for End-to-End Mobile Manipulation

problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
method: The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.
paper_title: Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting

problem: Identifying a textile’s production method and material class from robot interaction requires choosing actions and sensing that expose discriminative dynamics.
method: A dual-arm Franka Emika Panda setup with 6-axis wrist force–torque sensors performs two human-inspired exploratory procedures: pulling and twisting. The system records synchronized FT signals from active and passive arms, downsamples them to compact time series, and constructs per-arm and joint datasets. A CNN with 2D convolutions along each signal channel is trained on these sequences, achieving up to 100% test accuracy on construction-based classes, with pulling consistently outperforming twisting; combining active+passive measurements further improves performance.
paper_title: Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting

problem: It is unclear which force–torque channels and temporal segments are most informative for textile classification, limiting principled design of exploration strategies.
method: The authors assess per-signal importance using linear SVMs trained on individual FT channels and apply GradCAM to the CNN to visualize contributions over time and channels. Results show force Fz and torques Tx, Ty are especially discriminative (e.g., Fz alone reaches 100% accuracy for pull/construction), and that the model attends to different patterns for woven vs. knitted textiles. These insights indicate which measurements and actions to prioritize when designing future active exploration policies.
paper_title: Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting

problem: Parameter perturbation helps visual RL agents escape local minima, but sampling perturbations from fixed random initializations ignores task structure, yielding inefficient exploration and unstable learning.
method: Introduce a task-oriented perturbation mechanism that maintains a fixed-size buffer S_top of top-performing historical agents. Fit a Gaussian Φ_oriented = N(μ_top^θ, σ_top^θ) over their weights and periodically sample a candidate ϕ from this distribution to perturb the current agent. Blend weights via θ ← αθ + (1−α)ϕ, where α is set by the dormant-ratio-based schedule α = clip(1 − μβ, α_min, α_max) (Sec. 3.2, Alg. 1; Sec. 2). This yields perturbations biased toward promising regions, leading to faster skill acquisition and consistently lower, smoother dormant ratios than random-noise perturbations (Fig. 5).
paper_title: MENTOR: Mixture-of-Experts Network with Task-Oriented Perturbation for Visual Reinforcement Learning

problem: Robotic manipulation in low-data regimes benefits from tactile sensing, but large-scale pretraining exists mainly for vision. Collecting internet-scale tactile datasets is impractical, and vision alone struggles to perceive subtle contact events, limiting generalization.
method: Use piezo contact microphones on the robot gripper to capture contact-rich vibrations as audio and transfer internet-scale audio-visual pretraining to this modality. Initialize the audio encoder with AVID pre-trained on Audioset and the visual encoder with R3M; keep both unfrozen during policy learning. Train an end-to-end behavior cloning policy that fuses audio and vision via self-attention to predict actions. This is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first use of large-scale multisensory (audio-visual) pretraining for manipulation and yields substantial gains over vision-only and audio-from-scratch/BYOL-A baselines across three real tasks, with improved robustness to large visual domain shifts.
paper_title: Hearing Touch: Audio-Visual Pretraining for Contact-Rich Manipulation

problem: Fusing time-synchronized vision and contact audio into stable yet reactive manipulation actions is challenging, especially when demonstrations are non-Markovian and contact cues are subtle and fast.
method: Preprocess 2-second windows of averaged contact-mic audio into mel spectrograms and pair them with 4 synchronized images. Encode each stream with pretrained encoders, add learned positional embeddings, and fuse with a transformer self-attention block. Predict short-horizon sequences of 6-DoF delta end-effector actions and execute h steps before replanning (quasi open-loop) to remain responsive to audio while ensuring temporal consistency. Ablations show self-attention fusion is crucial and pretrained audio features transfer well—even when frozen—highlighting the effectiveness of the fusion design and pretraining choices.
paper_title: Hearing Touch: Audio-Visual Pretraining for Contact-Rich Manipulation

problem: Collecting diverse, robot-centric runtime policy code for training is costly and inefficient, typically requiring simulator setups and human annotations, which limits scalability for zero-shot robotic manipulation.
method: Video2Code is a low-cost, automatic two-stage pipeline that synthesizes executable policy code directly from in-the-wild instructional videos. It (1) extracts 16 keyframes per filtered DROID video and uses a Draft VLM (Gemini-1.5-Flash) to produce step-by-step, robot-centric natural language plans, then (2) prompts a code-domain LLM (DeepSeek-Coder-V2) with API definitions and rules to translate plans into executable API calls. Perception/control APIs are implemented with GroundingDINO and AnyGrasp, yielding 115k image-instruction-code samples for SFT. Ablations show Video2Code boosts zero-shot manipulation performance by +42.3% on RLBench and +45.4% on LIBERO over training without it.
paper_title: Robotic Programmer: Video Instructed Policy Code Generation for Robotic Manipulation

problem: Zero-shot execution of free-form language instructions in novel environments requires bridging high-level task descriptions with visually grounded low-level control without per-environment fine-tuning.
method: RoboPro is a unified multimodal policy-code generator that takes visual observations and free-form instructions and outputs executable policy code over a perception-and-control API library. It uses a SigLIP-L vision encoder and a code LLM (CodeQwen-1.5) connected via a lightweight adaptor, trained in three stages (visual alignment, pre-training on image-text, and SFT on 115k Video2Code data plus LLaVA-1.5). The generated code composes explainable API calls into sub-trajectories, enabling robust visual grounding and long-horizon reasoning. RoboPro achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance, surpassing GPT-4o by 11.6% on RLBench and by 17.4% on LIBERO, and transfers to real-world tasks without fine-tuning.
paper_title: Robotic Programmer: Video Instructed Policy Code Generation for Robotic Manipulation

problem: Policy code generation systems can be brittle to changes in API formats and to previously unseen, task-specific skills, hindering deployment across varied robotic configurations.
method: RoboPro employs an extendable API library with explicit perception (Lper) and control (Lcon) abstractions and detailed in-context prompts that define function semantics, parameterization, and usage while avoiding hardcoding. This design teaches the model to internalize atomic skills from API definitions, improving robustness to API Renaming and Refactoring, with minimal performance drop on RLBench. It also zero-shot adapts to new task-specific APIs for compositional tasks (e.g., Water Plants, Hit Ball, Scoop Cube), outperforming CaP and GPT-4o on these unseen skill sets.
paper_title: Robotic Programmer: Video Instructed Policy Code Generation for Robotic Manipulation

problem: Real-world imitation learning requires large quantities of high-quality robot demonstrations, but collecting them is slow, expert-intensive, and hard to scale beyond research labs.
method: Introduce RoboCrowd, an in-person crowdsourcing framework that makes public, non-expert teleoperation feasible and safe while aligning user motivation with data quality. The system builds on the ALOHA bimanual puppeteering platform and adds safety and usability features (e.g., plexiglass enclosure, extended leader-arm spacing, collision avoidance via a parallel MuJoCo simulation, mechanical stop for episode termination), plus a tablet UI with onboarding/tutorial, ID-based user tracking, an 'End Zone' for physical rewards, and gamification (points, leaderboard). It operationalizes three incentive classes—material rewards embedded in tasks, intrinsically interesting/challenging tasks, and social comparison via a leaderboard—to shape which demonstrations users produce in public settings.
paper_title: RoboCrowd: Scaling Robot Data Collection through Crowdsourcing

problem: It is unclear which incentives reliably drive non-experts in public spaces to produce more and better robot demonstrations.
method: Design scenes and tasks that instantiate three incentive mechanisms and deploy the system for two weeks in a university café, yielding 231 unique users and 817 interaction episodes annotated with task and quality labels (0–3). Empirical analyses show: (a) material rewards affect task choice—users spent roughly twice as many timesteps on the preferred candy bin-pick; (b) intrinsic interest increases engagement with harder tasks—users spent 1.5× more time on a challenging dispenser task than an easier bin-pick despite a less-preferred reward, and 4.18× more time opening a Ziploc vs. a same-reward bin-pick; (c) social comparison correlates with better data—users who viewed the leaderboard provided significantly more demonstrations (p < 0.001) of higher average quality (p < 0.05).
paper_title: RoboCrowd: Scaling Robot Data Collection through Crowdsourcing

problem: Crowdsourced demonstrations from non-experts may be noisy and diverse; it is important to determine whether they help train effective robot policies.
method: Evaluate ACT policies under three regimes: expert-only, naïve co-training with crowdsourced data, and pre-train (expert+crowd) then fine-tune on expert-only. Across six tasks, crowdsourced data can degrade performance when naïvely co-trained due to low-quality behaviors, but serves as useful pre-training that improves fine-tuned policies over expert-only baselines. Notably, pre-training on crowd data for a related task (hi-chew-ziploc) and fine-tuning on 50 expert demos for a new task (tool-ziploc) yields a 20% absolute improvement in staged success rates over expert-only, demonstrating transfer of shared manipulation skills.
paper_title: RoboCrowd: Scaling Robot Data Collection through Crowdsourcing
